title: PySpark: Data Engineering Track

chapters.title: F1 Platform Foundations & Architecture
chapters.description: Understand platform architecture, capacities, and workspace operating model.

blocks.content: Module F1 Platform Foundations & Architecture The Unified Analytics Platform Estimated Time: 90 Minutes Module Objective: Understand what Microsoft Fabric is, how its workloads share a single SaaS foundation, and why the unified architecture matters for every role in the data team.
Welcome to Module F1

This is where everything begins. Microsoft Fabric is not a collection of tools stitched together. It is a single platform with a single storage layer, a single security model, and a single billing system that serves every role on the data team — from the engineer writing PySpark to the analyst building dashboards to the data scientist training models. That sentence is the single most important insight in this module. Before Fabric, building an enterprise data platform meant assembling a dozen Azure services: Azure Data Factory for orchestration, Azure Data Lake Storage for raw files, Azure Synapse for SQL analytics, Azure Databricks for Spark, Power BI for reporting, and a web of connections, credentials, and configuration holding it all together. Each service had its own storage, its own security model, its own billing meter, and its own learning curve. The integration between services was the hardest part — and it was your responsibility to build and maintain it. Fabric replaces that assembly with a unified SaaS experience. You open one portal. Your data lives in one lake. Your compute is drawn from one capacity pool. Your security is enforced at one layer. And every workload — pipelines, Spark, SQL, streaming, BI, data science — operates on that shared foundation. This module gives you the architectural mental model that makes everything else in this course make sense. When you understand how Fabric is constructed, every subsequent module becomes a natural extension of what you already know. Conversely, if you skip this module and jump straight to building pipelines or writing PySpark, you will constantly wonder why things are where they are, how they connect, and what happens under the hood. Architecture first. Always. We will cover: what Fabric is and why it exists, the seven workloads and how they interoperate, OneLake as the unified storage layer, the capacity and compute model, workspaces and items, the Fabric portal, personas and roles, how data flows end-to-end across the platform, how Fabric replaces the legacy Azure stack, licensing and cost planning, production architecture patterns, and a checkpoint exercise that tests your understanding of the entire platform.

Microsoft Fabric is an end-to-end analytics platform delivered as Software-as-a-Service (SaaS). It was announced at Microsoft Build in May 2023 and reached general availability in November 2023. Since then it has become one of the fastest-growing data platforms in the enterprise market. The simplest way to understand Fabric: it is one platform that does everything your data team needs — data movement, data engineering, data warehousing, real-time analytics, data science, and business intelligence — without requiring you to provision, connect, or manage separate services. The SaaS Model and What It Changes Traditional analytics platforms are Platform-as-a-Service (PaaS). You provision a cluster, configure networking, manage scaling, apply patches, and monitor infrastructure. You are responsible for keeping the lights on. Fabric is SaaS. Microsoft manages the infrastructure entirely. There are no clusters to provision, no virtual networks to configure (unless you choose to), no patches to apply. You open a browser, sign in, and start working. This is not a cosmetic difference. It changes the economics and the operational model fundamentally: No idle costs for paused clusters — Fabric’s Spark pools start on demand from warm pools and cost nothing when idle. In the PaaS world, a Databricks cluster or Synapse SQL pool that sits idle still bills you. In Fabric, idle costs zero. No infrastructure management — Microsoft handles scaling, patching, availability, and disaster recovery for the platform. Your team focuses on data, not on Kubernetes upgrades or VM sizing. Unified billing — A single capacity unit (CU) pool serves all workloads. You do not get separate bills for storage, compute, and networking from seven different services. One meter, one invoice. Instant provisioning — Creating a Lakehouse, a Warehouse, or a Notebook takes seconds, not minutes or hours. There is no cluster spin-up, no resource group deployment, no ARM template to write. Automatic updates — New features appear in the platform without migration projects. You are always on the latest version. This is a double-edged sword — you get features faster, but you must test for regressions — but the net effect is overwhelmingly positive. The mental model shift from PaaS to SaaS is the first thing you must internalize. In the PaaS world, your team’s mental model is: “I have a cluster. I configure it. I monitor it. I scale it.” In the Fabric world, your mental model becomes: “I have a capacity. I create items. I write code. The platform runs it.” The infrastructure is invisible. The Three Pillars of Unification Fabric’s competitive advantage is not that any single workload is best-in-class. Databricks has a more mature Spark implementation. Snowflake has a more established SQL warehouse. Power BI Desktop has more visualization options than the Fabric web experience. What makes Fabric unique is the unification — and that unification rests on three pillars:

Pillar 1: One Storage (OneLake) — Every workload — Spark, SQL, KQL, Power BI, ML — reads from and writes to the same storage layer. When a Spark notebook writes a Delta table, the Warehouse can query it with T-SQL. Power BI can visualize it with DirectLake. A KQL query can scan it. No copying. No ETL between services. One copy of data, many readers. Pillar 2: One Security (Entra ID + OneLake Security) — Authentication, authorization, and data protection are enforced at one layer. A user’s access to a Delta table is the same whether they access it through Spark, SQL, KQL, or Power BI. You do not configure security in seven different consoles for seven different engines. One security model, consistent everywhere. Pillar 3: One Billing (Fabric Capacities) — All workloads share a single pool of compute resources measured in Capacity Units (CUs). You do not purchase Spark vCores, SQL DTUs, KQL capacity, and Power BI Premium separately. One pool, shared by all.

These three pillars eliminate the three biggest pain points of the legacy multi-service approach: data silos (solved by OneLake), security fragmentation (solved by unified identity), and cost unpredictability (solved by shared capacity).
The Fabric Architecture Stack
Positioning: Fabric vs. Databricks vs. Synapse vs. Snowflake
The enterprise analytics market has several major platforms. Understanding where Fabric sits helps you evaluate it honestly:

Platform Model Strengths Key Difference from Fabric Microsoft Fabric SaaS (unified) All-in-one: ETL, Spark, SQL, BI, streaming, ML in one portal with one lake This IS Fabric Databricks PaaS (multi-cloud) Best-in-class Spark, MLflow, Unity Catalog, Delta Lake originator Separate platform; no native BI layer; you bring your own storage and orchestration Azure Synapse Analytics PaaS (Azure) SQL pools, Spark pools, pipelines in one Azure resource Fabric’s predecessor; being deprecated in favor of Fabric; no SaaS model Snowflake SaaS (multi-cloud) Elastic SQL warehouse, data sharing, strong governance SQL-centric; limited Spark/ML; no native BI; separate from orchestration Google BigQuery Serverless SaaS (GCP) Massive scale SQL analytics, BigQuery ML, Omni for multi-cloud SQL-only compute; no native ETL orchestration; BI through Looker (separate product)

Key insight: Fabric’s competitive advantage is not that any single workload is best-in-class. It is that all workloads share one storage layer, one security model, and one governance framework. The integration is the product.

Fabric organizes its capabilities into workloads. Each workload is a set of tools optimized for a specific role or task, but they all operate on the same underlying platform. Think of workloads as different lenses on the same data — the same Delta table looks like a DataFrame through the Spark lens, a SQL table through the Warehouse lens, a KQL table through the Eventhouse lens, and a semantic model column through the Power BI lens. The underlying bytes are identical.

1. Data Factory Data Factory is Fabric’s orchestration and data movement engine. It contains two sub-tools: Pipelines — Code-free orchestration workflows built from activities: Copy Activity (move data from A to B), Notebook Activity (run a Spark notebook), Stored Procedure Activity (execute T-SQL), ForEach (iterate over collections), If Condition (branch logic), Web Activity (call REST APIs), and dozens more. Pipelines are how you schedule, sequence, and monitor batch workloads. Dataflows Gen2 — Power Query-based low-code transformations with 300+ connectors. You use a visual, drag-and-drop interface to connect to sources, apply transformations (filter, merge, pivot, unpivot, add columns), and write to a Fabric destination (Lakehouse, Warehouse, or KQL Database). Dataflows Gen2 are the right choice when the source data is straightforward and the team prefers visual transformation over PySpark code. If data needs to move from point A to point B, or a sequence of jobs needs to run on a schedule, Data Factory is where you build it. 2. Data Engineering Data Engineering is the Spark-powered workload for building ETL/ELT pipelines at scale. Its primary items are: Lakehouse — The storage artifact that holds files (Files/ section for raw CSVs, JSONs, images) and managed Delta tables (Tables/ section). Every Lakehouse automatically generates a SQL analytics endpoint (a read-only T-SQL surface over the Delta tables) and a default semantic model for Power BI. The Lakehouse is the most commonly created item in Fabric. Notebooks — Interactive, multi-language development environments for PySpark, Spark SQL, Scala, and R. Notebooks support markdown cells for documentation, visualization output, parameterized execution (for pipeline integration), and collaboration features (shared notebooks, %run for modularization). Environments — Configuration packages that define library dependencies and Spark settings for Notebooks. When your Notebook needs a specific PyPI package or a custom wheel, you attach an Environment to ensure consistent, reproducible execution. If you are transforming, cleaning, enriching, or reshaping data at scale, this is your workload. This is the workload you will spend the most time in as a data engineer, and it is the focus of this entire track. 3. Data Science Data Science shares the same Notebooks and Spark engine as Data Engineering, but adds: Experiments — MLflow-tracked training runs. Every time you train a model, the parameters, metrics, and artifacts are logged automatically. You can compare runs side by side and identify the best-performing configuration. Models — A registry for versioned ML artifacts. When a model is ready for production, you register it in the Model Registry with stage transitions (Staging → Production → Archived). PREDICT() function — A T-SQL function that invokes a registered model for batch scoring. This lets SQL analysts score data without writing Python — they call PREDICT() in a SQL query, and the model runs behind the scenes. If you are training models, tracking experiments, or deploying predictions, this workload provides the scaffolding. 4. Data Warehouse The Data Warehouse is a fully managed, T-SQL-native analytical store. It supports full DML (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE), stored procedures, views, functions, and rich SQL analytics. Under the hood, it stores data in Delta format in OneLake, but exposes a familiar SQL Server interface. Key distinctions from the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint: the Warehouse supports write operations (DML), stored procedures, cross-database queries, and granular SQL security (GRANT/DENY/REVOKE on individual objects). The Lakehouse SQL endpoint is read-only. If your team thinks in T-SQL and needs a governed, high-performance analytical database with full write support, this is the workload. 5. Real-Time Intelligence Real-Time Intelligence handles streaming and time-series workloads. Its key items: Eventhouse — A KQL (Kusto Query Language) database cluster optimized for high-velocity, append-heavy data. Designed for billions of events with sub-second query performance. Time-series operators (make-series, anomaly detection) are built into the query language. Eventstreams — No-code streaming ingestion and routing. Connect a source (Event Hubs, IoT Hub, custom apps, sample data generators), apply optional transformations (filter, aggregate, manage fields), and route to one or more destinations (Eventhouse, Lakehouse, custom endpoint). Real-Time Dashboards — Auto-refreshing visualizations powered by KQL queries. These are not Power BI reports — they are purpose-built for operational monitoring with live data. Activator — Event-driven alerting. Define a condition (a KQL query that evaluates to true), and Activator fires an action: send an email, post to Teams, trigger a Power Automate flow, or call a webhook. This turns data into automated decisions. If data arrives continuously and you need sub-second query responses, this is the workload. 6. Power BI Power BI is Fabric’s business intelligence layer, and it is the most widely adopted component — used by over 550,000 organizations worldwide. In Fabric, Power BI gains DirectLake mode, which lets semantic models query Delta tables in OneLake directly without importing data or using DirectQuery. This eliminates the traditional trade-off between performance (Import mode, data copied into memory) and freshness (DirectQuery mode, every query hits the source). Power BI covers semantic models (the analytical data model with relationships, hierarchies, and calculated measures), DAX (the formula language for business calculations), reports (interactive visualizations), dashboards (pinned tiles from multiple reports), paginated reports (pixel-perfect, printable layouts), and governed distribution through apps. 7. SQL Database SQL Database is Fabric’s transactional (OLTP) engine — a fully managed SQL Server-based database for operational workloads. Unlike the Data Warehouse (which is analytical and optimized for large scans), SQL Database supports normalized schemas, indexes, triggers, and full transactional isolation for high-concurrency read/write workloads. Its data automatically replicates to OneLake as Delta tables, enabling analytics without ETL. You get an operational database that feeds the analytical platform in near-real-time, with no pipeline to build or maintain. This is the newest Fabric workload, reaching general availability in late 2025.

The critical takeaway: these seven workloads are not seven products. They are seven experiences built on one platform. They share OneLake for storage, Microsoft Entra ID for identity, and Fabric capacities for compute. Data created in one workload is instantly accessible in every other workload without copying, moving, or configuring connections.

OneLake is Fabric’s storage layer, and it is the foundation on which everything else is built. Every Fabric workload reads from and writes to OneLake. There is no separate storage account to provision, no connection string to configure, no access key to manage. When you create a Lakehouse, the data goes to OneLake. When you create a Warehouse, the data goes to OneLake. When Power BI uses DirectLake mode, it reads from OneLake. Think of OneLake as OneDrive for data. Just as every Microsoft 365 user gets a OneDrive that stores their files in a single namespace, every Fabric tenant gets a OneLake that stores all analytical data in a single namespace. The parallel is intentional — Microsoft designed OneLake to be as invisible as OneDrive. You do not think about where OneDrive stores your files. You should not have to think about where OneLake stores your tables. Why One Copy of Data Matters In legacy architectures, data is copied between services constantly. Raw files land in ADLS Gen2. A pipeline copies them into a Synapse SQL pool. Another process exports aggregates to a separate storage account for Power BI. A data science team extracts a sample to their own storage for training. Each copy introduces lag, inconsistency, cost, and a security boundary to manage. The math is unforgiving. If you have 5 copies of a 10 TB dataset across 5 services, you are paying for 50 TB of storage and maintaining 5 pipelines to keep them in sync. When a source record is corrected, the correction must propagate to all 5 copies — and until it does, different teams see different numbers. This is not a hypothetical problem. It is the most common source of data trust issues in large organizations. OneLake eliminates this. Because all workloads share the same storage layer, a Delta table written by a Spark notebook is instantly queryable by the Warehouse’s SQL engine, instantly visible to Power BI’s DirectLake mode, and instantly accessible from a KQL database — without copying a single byte.
One Write, Many Readers

Spark Notebook writes Delta table to OneLake
                    │
                    ▼
           ┌─────────────────┐
           │   OneLake       │
           │  (Delta table)  │
           └─────┬───┬───┬───┘
                 │   │   │
        ┌───────┘   │   └───────┐
        ▼           ▼           ▼
  SQL Warehouse  Power BI    KQL Query
  (T-SQL query)  (DirectLake) (Eventhouse)
Same bytes. Same version. Zero copy.
The Hierarchical Namespace OneLake organizes data in a four-level hierarchy:

Tenant (your organization — exactly one OneLake)
  └── Workspace A
  │   └── Lakehouse: Sales
  │   │   └── Files/ (raw CSVs, JSONs, images)
  │   │   └── Tables/ (managed Delta tables)
  │   └── Warehouse: Analytics
  │   └── Notebook: ETL_Pipeline
  │
  └── Workspace B
      └── Lakehouse: Marketing
      └── Eventhouse: Clickstream

Under the hood, OneLake maps to Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ADLS Gen2). The files are stored as Delta Parquet, the industry-standard open format. This means you can access OneLake data through standard ADLS Gen2 APIs, mount it locally with OneLake File Explorer (a Windows application), and read it with any tool that understands Parquet or Delta Lake. Delta Lake as the Default Format Every table in Fabric — whether created in a Lakehouse, a Warehouse, or a SQL Database — is stored in Delta Lake format. Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer that adds ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel to Parquet files. It consists of two things: Parquet data files (the actual columnar data) and a JSON transaction log (the _delta_log/ directory that tracks every change). This is not a configuration choice. It is a platform decision. Fabric is a Delta-native platform. When you INSERT a row into a Warehouse table, a Delta commit is written to OneLake. When you run df.write.saveAsTable() in a Notebook, a Delta commit is written to OneLake. The format is always Delta. Understanding Delta Lake is not optional — it is foundational to every workload. Module F2 covers Delta Lake in depth. OneLake Shortcuts Shortcuts are virtual pointers to data stored outside OneLake. You can create a shortcut to an ADLS Gen2 container, an Amazon S3 bucket, a Google Cloud Storage location, or a Dataverse environment. The data stays where it is — Fabric reads it in place without copying. This is how Fabric bridges the gap between a unified platform and the reality that enterprise data lives in many places. Shortcuts give you zero-copy access to external data while maintaining the OneLake namespace as the single point of discovery. From a query’s perspective, a shortcut looks and behaves exactly like a local table — the consumer does not know (and does not need to know) whether the data is in OneLake or in an external storage account.

sections.title: Capacities, SKUs, and the Compute Model

blocks.content: Fabric’s compute is organized around capacities. A capacity is a pool of compute resources, measured in Capacity Units (CUs), that is shared across all Fabric workloads within the workspaces assigned to that capacity. This is fundamentally different from the legacy model, where each service had its own compute. In the old world, your Synapse SQL pool had dedicated DTUs, your Databricks cluster had its own VMs, and your ADF integration runtime had separate compute. In Fabric, one capacity pool serves pipelines, Spark jobs, SQL queries, KQL queries, and Power BI refreshes. F-SKUs: The Capacity Tiers Fabric capacities come in tiers called F-SKUs. Each SKU specifies a CU count:

SKU CUs Spark vCores (Approx) Typical Use Case F2 2 8 Individual developers, proof-of-concept, learning F4 4 16 Small team development and testing F8 8 32 Small team production workloads F16 16 64 Medium team production workloads F32 32 128 Department-level production workloads F64 64 256 Large-scale production workloads F128 128 512 Enterprise-scale production workloads F256+ 256+ 1024+ Large enterprise, heavy concurrent workloads

Every Fabric workload consumes CUs from this shared pool. When a Spark job runs, it draws CUs. When a SQL query executes, it draws CUs. When a pipeline copies data, it draws CUs. The consumption is metered and tracked in the Fabric Capacity Metrics app. How CU Consumption Works Not all CU consumption is equal. Different operations consume CUs at different rates, and understanding this is critical for capacity planning: Interactive operations — Queries, notebook cell executions, and report renders consume CUs from the interactive pool. These have priority because a human is waiting for a response. Background operations — Scheduled refreshes, pipeline runs, and data processing consume CUs from the background pool. These are lower priority and can be deferred if the capacity is under pressure. Fabric uses a smoothing window to evaluate consumption. A short spike — like a large Spark job that runs for 60 seconds — is averaged over a longer window (typically 5 minutes), so brief bursts do not trigger throttling. But sustained heavy usage will. Throttling and Burst When sustained usage exceeds your capacity for an extended period, Fabric applies throttling in stages: Stage 1: Background delay — Background operations are delayed. Your pipeline might wait longer to start. Power BI scheduled refreshes might queue. Interactive operations are not affected. Stage 2: Interactive delay — If overuse continues, interactive operations begin experiencing latency. Queries take longer to return. Notebook cells take longer to execute. Stage 3: Rejection — In extreme sustained overuse, Fabric begins rejecting new operations. This is the platform telling you: you need a larger capacity. The Fabric Capacity Metrics app is your primary tool for understanding consumption patterns. It shows CU usage over time, broken down by workload and by workspace. You should check it weekly during the first month of any production deployment. Autoscale Billing for Spark Introduced in 2025, Autoscale Billing is a serverless, pay-as-you-go option where Spark jobs run independently of your Fabric capacity and are billed only for execution time. This has a profound architectural implication: it prevents Spark jobs from consuming CUs that SQL queries, Power BI reports, and other workloads need. Without Autoscale, a large Spark transformation can consume your entire capacity for 20 minutes, causing every other workload to throttle. With Autoscale, the Spark job runs on separate, elastic compute and your capacity pool remains available for interactive workloads. The trade-off is cost — Autoscale Spark bills per-second based on vCores consumed, additive to your capacity cost. For bursty Spark workloads, it is almost always worth it. Storage Billing Storage in OneLake is billed separately from compute. You pay per GB stored, similar to ADLS Gen2 pricing. Shortcut data (data that lives outside OneLake) does not incur Fabric storage charges — you pay the storage provider directly. Delta Lake maintenance operations (OPTIMIZE, VACUUM) play a critical role in storage cost management, and you will learn these in Module F2.

sections.title: Workspaces, Items, and the Fabric Object Model

blocks.content: Everything in Fabric lives inside a workspace. A workspace is a container — think of it as a project folder that groups related items together and applies shared security and capacity. Workspaces A workspace is simultaneously: A collaboration boundary — Team members are granted roles (Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer) at the workspace level. The role determines what they can do: Admins manage settings and security, Members create and edit all items, Contributors create and edit their own items, Viewers can only read. A capacity binding — Each workspace is assigned to a Fabric capacity, which determines the compute resources available to everything in that workspace. Changing the capacity assignment of a workspace changes the compute available to all items in it. A governance scope — Sensitivity labels, Git integration, and deployment pipelines operate at the workspace level. When you connect a workspace to Git, all supported items in that workspace are version-controlled. An organizational unit — Workspaces can be organized into domains (e.g., Finance, Marketing, Operations) for data mesh-style governance. Domains are metadata groupings that help users discover and trust data assets across the organization. A common production pattern is to create separate workspaces for development, testing, and production, and use Fabric’s Deployment Pipelines to promote items between them:

┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐    ┌────────────┐
  │  sales_dev  │ ──▶ │ sales_test │ ──▶ │ sales_prod │
  │            │    │            │    │            │
  │ Lakehouse  │    │ Lakehouse  │    │ Lakehouse  │
  │ Notebooks  │    │ Notebooks  │    │ Notebooks  │
  │ Pipelines  │    │ Pipelines  │    │ Pipelines  │
  └────────────┘    └────────────┘    └────────────┘
      Dev          Test/UAT        Production
     (F4)           (F8)            (F64)

Items Inside a workspace, you create items. An item is a first-class Fabric object — it has a type, a name, metadata, and a lifecycle. Here are the item types you will encounter most frequently as a data engineer:

Item Type Workload Purpose Lakehouse Data Engineering Dual-interface store: Files/ for raw data, Tables/ for managed Delta tables, plus auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint and default semantic model Warehouse Data Warehouse T-SQL-native analytical database with full DML support, stored procedures, views, and cross-database queries Notebook Data Eng. / Data Science Interactive multi-language code environment (PySpark, SQL, Scala, R) with markdown, visualization, and parameterized execution Pipeline Data Factory Orchestration workflow with activities, triggers, parameters, and scheduling. The backbone of batch processing. Dataflow Gen2 Data Factory Low-code Power Query-based transformation with 300+ connectors and configurable data destinations Environment Data Engineering Library and Spark configuration management for Notebooks. Ensures reproducible execution. Semantic Model Power BI In-memory analytical model with DAX measures, relationships, and hierarchies Report Power BI Interactive data visualization with filters, slicers, drillthrough, and conditional formatting Eventhouse Real-Time Intelligence KQL database cluster for streaming and time-series data with sub-second query performance Eventstream Real-Time Intelligence No-code streaming ingestion, transformation, and routing to multiple destinations SQL Database SQL Database Transactional OLTP database with automatic OneLake replication for analytics ML Model Data Science Registered, versioned machine learning model artifact in the MLflow Model Registry ML Experiment Data Science MLflow experiment tracking for training runs with automatic metric and artifact logging
Naming Conventions A disciplined naming convention prevents chaos as your Fabric estate grows. Here is a recommended pattern:

Workspaces:   {domain}_{environment}      e.g., sales_dev, sales_prod
  Lakehouses:   lh_{layer}_{domain}         e.g., lh_bronze_sales, lh_gold
  Warehouses:   wh_{domain}                 e.g., wh_sales, wh_enterprise
  Notebooks:    nb_{purpose}_{domain}        e.g., nb_etl_orders, nb_dq_check
  Pipelines:    pl_{purpose}_{domain}        e.g., pl_ingest_crm, pl_daily
  Environments: env_{purpose}                e.g., env_spark_ml, env_default
  Semantic M.:  sm_{domain}                  e.g., sm_sales, sm_finance
  Reports:      rpt_{name}                   e.g., rpt_sales_dashboard
The exact convention matters less than consistency. Pick a pattern, document it, and enforce it from day one. Renaming items after they are referenced by pipelines, notebooks, and reports is painful.

sections.title: The Fabric Portal — Navigating the UI

blocks.content:
The Fabric portal is accessed at app.fabric.microsoft.com. It is a browser-based experience that serves as the single entry point for all Fabric workloads. There is no separate portal for Data Factory, Spark, or Power BI — everything lives in one interface.
Home Screen
The home screen shows recently accessed items, recommended items, and quick-create shortcuts. The left navigation rail provides access to workspaces, the OneLake catalog, and workload-specific experiences.
Workspace View
Selecting a workspace shows all items in a list or grid view. You can filter by item type, sort by date modified, and search by name. Each item shows its type icon, name, last modified date, and owner. This is where you will spend most of your time navigating between Lakehouses, Notebooks, and Pipelines.
The Workload Switcher
At the bottom of the left navigation rail, a workload switcher lets you change your current experience: Data Engineering, Data Factory, Data Science, Data Warehouse, Real-Time Intelligence, or Power BI. Switching workloads changes the available creation options (what types of items you can create) but does not change your data — all workloads share the same workspaces and the same OneLake storage.
Important: the workload switcher is a lens, not a boundary. When you are in the Data Engineering experience and you see a Warehouse in your workspace, you can open it. When you are in the Power BI experience and you see a Lakehouse, you can view its tables. The switcher changes the creation options, not the visibility.
The OneLake Catalog
The OneLake catalog is a metadata search and discovery experience. It indexes every item in every workspace that you have access to. You can search by name, filter by item type, view lineage (which items depend on which), and see schema information — all without opening the item. Think of it as a search engine for your data platform.
Settings and Administration
Workspace settings control capacity assignment, Git integration, and security. Tenant settings (accessible to Fabric admins) control platform-wide feature flags, licensing, and governance policies. The Fabric Admin portal provides usage metrics, capacity monitoring, and audit logs. As a data engineer, you will primarily use workspace settings; the Fabric Admin portal is covered in the Administration track.
Hands-On Orientation
Before moving forward, take five minutes to explore the portal:
•  Sign in to app.fabric.microsoft.com
•  Create a workspace (or use an existing one)
•  Switch between workloads in the left navigation
•  Create a Lakehouse and observe the Files/ and Tables/ sections
•  Open the OneLake catalog and browse available data assets
•  Check the workspace settings: note the capacity assignment and Git integration options
This orientation builds muscle memory. The rest of this course assumes you can navigate the portal confidently.

Fabric is designed for the entire data team, not just engineers. Different personas use different workloads, but they all work on the same platform and the same data. Understanding these personas helps you design solutions that serve your whole organization, not just your own role.

Persona Primary Workloads Key Activities Data Engineer Data Engineering, Data Factory Build ETL/ELT pipelines with Notebooks and PySpark. Create and maintain Lakehouses. Orchestrate workflows with Pipelines. Manage Delta table health (OPTIMIZE, VACUUM). Design medallion architecture (Bronze/Silver/Gold). Data / SQL Analyst Data Warehouse, Power BI Write T-SQL queries against the Warehouse or Lakehouse SQL endpoint. Build semantic models and DAX measures. Create reports and dashboards. Analyze data for business insights. Data Scientist Data Science, Data Engineering Explore data in Notebooks. Engineer features with PySpark. Train and track models with MLflow. Deploy batch predictions with PREDICT(). Monitor model drift. BI Developer Power BI Design semantic models with proper star schemas. Write DAX measures for business KPIs. Build interactive reports with drillthrough and bookmarks. Manage governed distribution through apps. Platform / Fabric Admin Administration, all workloads Manage tenant settings and feature flags. Monitor capacity utilization and throttling. Configure security policies, domains, and deployment pipelines. Audit activity logs. Analytics Engineer Data Engineering, Data Warehouse Bridge engineering and analytics. Build the Gold layer of the medallion architecture. Create governed views and tables for downstream consumption. Ensure data quality.

Key insight: the power of Fabric is that these personas do not need separate environments. The data engineer who builds a Delta table in a Notebook and the analyst who queries that same table through the SQL analytics endpoint are working on the same data, in the same workspace, with zero data movement. The data scientist who trains a model on Silver-layer data and the BI developer who visualizes predictions in a report are consuming the same Delta files in OneLake. The platform eliminates the organizational silos that traditionally exist between these roles.

sections.title: End-to-End Data Flow — How It All Connects

blocks.content: The best way to understand Fabric’s architecture is to trace a real data flow from source to business decision. This walkthrough follows a single order record from an ERP system all the way to an executive dashboard. Every step happens on the same platform, in the same tenant, through OneLake.

Scenario: Contoso Retail — Daily Sales Analytics Contoso is a retail company. Every day, their ERP system exports order files as CSV. The data team needs to ingest, clean, model, and visualize this data so that executives can see yesterday’s sales performance by 9:00 AM.

Step 1: Ingestion (Data Factory → OneLake)
A Pipeline (pl_ingest_erp) runs at 6:00 AM. Its Copy Activity connects to Contoso’s Azure Storage account, reads yesterday’s order CSV, and lands it in the Lakehouse’s Files/ section: lh_bronze/Files/landing/erp/orders_2026-03-07.csv.
The file is now in OneLake. It has not been transformed. It is a raw CSV sitting in the Files/ landing zone. This is the Bronze layer — data exactly as the source system produced it.
Step 2: Bronze Loading (Notebook → Delta Table)
The Pipeline triggers a Notebook Activity next. The notebook nb_bronze_load reads the CSV from the Files/ landing zone, adds metadata columns (_load_timestamp, _source_file, _source_system), and appends the rows to the raw_orders Delta table in lh_bronze/Tables/.
The data is now a Delta table — columnar Parquet files with a transaction log. It is queryable by SQL, Spark, and KQL. But it is still raw: the types are strings, there may be duplicates, and nulls have not been handled.
Step 3: Silver Cleansing (Notebook → Delta Table)
The Pipeline triggers nb_silver_orders. This notebook reads raw_orders from lh_bronze, deduplicates by order_id (keeping the latest _load_timestamp), casts amount from string to DECIMAL, validates dates (rejects future dates), standardizes status values (lowercase, trim whitespace), and MERGEs into lh_silver/Tables/orders.
The Silver table is clean, typed, deduplicated, and governed. An analyst querying lh_silver can trust the data.
Step 4: Gold Modeling (Notebook → Star Schema)
The Pipeline triggers nb_gold_fact_orders. This notebook reads Silver orders and customers, resolves business keys to surrogate keys, joins to dimension tables (dim_date, dim_customers, dim_products), calculates line_amount and net_amount, and writes fact_orders to lh_gold/Tables/.
The Gold layer is a proper star schema: fact tables with foreign keys to dimension tables. This is the layer that Power BI reads.
Step 5: Power BI DirectLake (Semantic Model → Report)
The Power BI semantic model (sm_contoso) is configured in DirectLake mode, pointing directly at the Delta tables in lh_gold. There is no data import. There is no DirectQuery connection string. Power BI reads the Parquet files from OneLake into its in-memory VertiPaq engine, applies DAX measures (YTD revenue, margin %, period-over-period growth), and serves the interactive report (rpt_sales_dashboard).
The executive opens the report at 8:45 AM and sees yesterday’s sales. The data traveled from an ERP CSV to an executive dashboard through one platform, one storage layer, and zero copies between services.
The Complete Data Flow

ERP CSV                                                    Executive
    │                                                        Dashboard
    ▼                                                           ▲
  ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌──────────┐   ┌─────────┐
  │ Pipeline │──▶│  Bronze  │──▶│  Silver  │──▶│  Gold   │──▶ Power BI
  │ (Copy)  │   │ (append) │   │ (MERGE)  │   │ (star)  │   DirectLake
  └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └──────────┘   └─────────┘
  lh_bronze        lh_bronze        lh_silver        lh_gold
  Files/            Tables/          Tables/          Tables/
  landing/erp/      raw_orders       orders           fact_orders
────────── ALL IN ONELAKE. ONE STORAGE LAYER. ──────────

Why this matters: In the legacy stack, this flow would require Azure Data Factory (separate service), ADLS Gen2 (separate storage account), Databricks (separate Spark cluster), Azure Synapse (separate SQL engine), and Power BI (separate BI service). Five services, five billing meters, five security configurations, and a web of connections to maintain. In Fabric, it is one platform, one storage layer, and one pipeline.

sections.title: Fabric vs. the Legacy Azure Stack

blocks.content: If you are coming from the Azure data ecosystem, you already know many of the concepts in Fabric — they have new names, new interfaces, and new integration points, but the underlying ideas are familiar. This mapping helps you translate your existing knowledge:

Legacy Azure Service Fabric Equivalent What Changed Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ADLS Gen2) OneLake No separate storage account to provision. OneLake is automatic, tenant-wide, and Delta-native. ADLS Gen2 is still the underlying protocol. Azure Data Factory (ADF) Data Factory (Pipelines + Dataflows Gen2) Same concepts (activities, triggers), but Fabric-native. Runs inside the portal, writes to OneLake directly, and consumes CUs from Fabric capacity. Azure Synapse — SQL Pools Data Warehouse Fully managed, no dedicated pool provisioning. Decoupled compute and storage. Stores data in Delta format in OneLake. Azure Synapse — Spark Pools Spark in Data Engineering Serverless Spark with auto-provisioning. Starter and warm pools for fast session start. Environments for library management. Azure Databricks Notebooks + Spark + Lakehouse Fabric’s Spark is functionally similar but integrated with OneLake, SQL, and Power BI. Databricks remains a separate platform with Unity Catalog. Azure HDInsight Spark in Data Engineering HDInsight was the original managed Hadoop/Spark on Azure. Fabric’s Spark is its modern replacement. Power BI Service (standalone) Power BI (within Fabric) Same reports and semantic models, but now with DirectLake mode, OneLake integration, and unified security. Azure Stream Analytics Eventstreams Eventstreams replaces Stream Analytics for most Fabric scenarios, with no-code stream processing and direct routing to OneLake. Azure Data Explorer (ADX) Eventhouse (KQL Database) Same KQL language, same columnar engine, now integrated into Fabric with OneLake storage. Azure SQL Database SQL Database in Fabric Transactional SQL database with automatic replication to OneLake for analytics.

Important context: Azure Synapse Analytics is being gradually deprecated in favor of Fabric. Microsoft has announced that Synapse workloads should migrate to Fabric for long-term support. If you are building new projects today, build them on Fabric.
Understanding Fabric’s cost model before you start building prevents surprises. The model is simpler than the legacy Azure stack, but it requires capacity planning. Licensing Options

License Type How It Works Best For Free Trial 60-day trial with an F64 capacity equivalent. No credit card required. Full functionality. Learning, evaluation, proof-of-concept. This is how most individuals start with Fabric. Pay-As-You-Go (PAYG) Hourly billing for the capacity SKU you choose. Turn it on, turn it off. No commitment. Variable workloads, development environments, teams that want flexibility without commitment. Reserved Capacity 1-year or 3-year commitment for a specific SKU. Significant discount (typically 20–40% savings over PAYG). Production workloads with predictable, steady-state usage. Fabric Free (Power BI Free) Limited individual access. Can view shared content but cannot create workspaces or full Fabric items. Individual users who only consume reports shared by others. Power BI Pro / PPU Per-user licenses for Power BI sharing and distribution. Required for some collaboration scenarios even within Fabric. Report sharing and app distribution in organizations without Premium/Fabric capacity.

Estimating Monthly Costs A rough framework for estimating Fabric costs: Compute — Determined by the F-SKU you choose. An F64 capacity is the entry point for most production workloads. Cost varies by region and commitment type. Check the Azure pricing calculator for current rates. Storage — Billed per GB stored in OneLake. Delta Lake format with OPTIMIZE and VACUUM keeps storage efficient. Shortcut data is billed at the source, not in OneLake. Autoscale Spark — If enabled, Spark jobs are billed separately at a per-second rate based on vCores consumed. This is additive to your capacity cost but prevents Spark from starving other workloads. Networking — Standard Azure egress charges apply for data leaving OneLake. Intra-region data movement within Fabric is free. Cost Planning Best Practices

items: Start with the free trial for evaluation and learning.
Use PAYG for development environments that can be paused outside business hours.
Right-size your production capacity using the Fabric Capacity Metrics app.
Consider Autoscale Billing for Spark if Spark workloads are bursty and compete with SQL/BI workloads for CUs.
Use reserved capacity for stable production workloads to save 20–40%.
Monitor the Capacity Metrics app weekly during the first month to understand your actual consumption pattern.
OPTIMIZE and VACUUM your Delta tables regularly to minimize storage costs and maximize query performance.

blocks.content: Before you start building, it helps to see the architectural patterns that successful Fabric deployments follow. These patterns recur across industries and scales. You do not need to implement all of them on day one, but knowing they exist shapes how you design your initial platform. Pattern 1: Medallion Lakehouse The most common pattern. Three Lakehouses represent three data quality layers: Bronze (raw), Silver (cleansed), and Gold (modeled). Pipelines orchestrate Notebooks that transform data through each layer. Power BI reads from Gold via DirectLake. This pattern is the foundation of the Data Engineering capstone project. Pattern 2: Lakehouse + Warehouse Hybrid The Lakehouse handles ingestion and Spark-based transformation (Bronze and Silver). The Warehouse handles the Gold layer for SQL-heavy analytics teams. Data flows from Lakehouse to Warehouse via cross-database queries or views. This pattern is ideal when the analytics team is strong in T-SQL and needs stored procedures, parameterized views, and granular SQL security. Pattern 3: Real-Time + Batch Dual Architecture Eventstreams route streaming data to both an Eventhouse (for real-time KQL queries) and a Lakehouse (for batch processing). The Eventhouse serves real-time dashboards and alerting. The Lakehouse integrates streaming data with batch sources in the medallion architecture. This pattern is essential for IoT, clickstream, and operational monitoring scenarios. Pattern 4: Domain-Based Workspace Strategy Large organizations create workspaces per domain (Finance, Marketing, Operations) with separate dev/test/prod stages. Deployment Pipelines promote items between stages. Domains in the OneLake catalog provide cross-domain discovery. This pattern implements a data mesh-inspired governance model within Fabric’s unified platform. Anti-Patterns to Avoid

The monolithic workspace — Putting everything in one workspace. This makes security too coarse (everyone sees everything), deployment too risky (one mistake affects all items), and navigation impossible. Use separate workspaces for separate domains and stages. The data swamp Bronze — Landing data in Bronze without metadata columns (_load_timestamp, _source_system, _source_file). When something goes wrong, you cannot trace which file produced which rows. Always add audit metadata at ingestion. Skipping Silver — Going directly from Bronze to Gold. This creates Gold tables with data quality issues embedded in them. The Silver layer exists to enforce type correctness, deduplication, and null handling before any business logic is applied. Copy-paste security — Configuring security differently in the Lakehouse, the Warehouse, and Power BI. This creates gaps where a user can access data through one path but not another. Use OneLake data access roles for consistent storage-layer security. Ignoring Delta maintenance — Never running OPTIMIZE or VACUUM on Delta tables. Over time, small files accumulate (the small file problem), queries slow down, and storage costs increase. Schedule maintenance weekly for active tables.
Module F1 Checkpoint
You now understand:

items: What Microsoft Fabric is — a unified SaaS analytics platform with one storage layer (OneLake), one compute pool (Capacity Units), and one security model (Microsoft Entra ID)
The seven workloads — Data Factory, Data Engineering, Data Science, Data Warehouse, Real-Time Intelligence, Power BI, and SQL Database — and what each is designed for
OneLake — the single storage foundation, Delta Lake as the default format, shortcuts for zero-copy external access
Capacities and compute — F-SKUs, CU consumption, throttling and burst behavior, autoscale billing for Spark
Workspaces and items — the organizational model, item types, naming conventions, and dev/test/prod patterns
The Fabric portal — navigation, workspace views, workload switcher, OneLake catalog, and settings
Personas — which roles use which workloads, and how they collaborate on shared data without data copies
End-to-end data flow — how a record travels from an ERP CSV through Bronze/Silver/Gold to a Power BI dashboard, all within one platform
Legacy mapping — how Fabric replaces Azure Data Factory, Synapse, HDInsight, ADLS Gen2, and other Azure data services
Licensing and cost planning — trial, PAYG, reserved capacity, and how to estimate monthly costs
Production architecture patterns — medallion Lakehouse, hybrid Lakehouse+Warehouse, real-time+batch, domain-based workspaces, and anti-patterns to avoid

blocks.content: Architecture Quiz
Before moving to Module F2, test yourself:

items: Draw the Fabric architecture from memory: workloads at the top, OneLake in the middle, capacity and security at the bottom. Can you name all seven workloads?
A colleague asks: “What is the difference between a Lakehouse and a Warehouse in Fabric?” How do you answer in three sentences?
Your manager asks: “Why would we move from Azure Synapse to Fabric?” What are the three strongest reasons?
An analyst wants to query a Delta table that a data engineer created in a Notebook. Does the analyst need Spark? What interface do they use?
Trace a data record from a source CSV to a Power BI report. Name every Fabric item it passes through and the workload responsible for each step.
You are planning capacity for a team of 10 data engineers and 20 analysts. What F-SKU would you start with, and how would you monitor whether it is sufficient?
Name three anti-patterns that new Fabric deployments commonly fall into. For each, explain why it is harmful and what the correct pattern is.
A stakeholder asks: “How does Fabric handle security? Can a data engineer see data that is meant only for the finance team?” Explain the layered security model at a high level.

blocks.content: You are not just learning a tool.
You are building the mental model of a platform that unifies data movement, engineering, analytics, real-time streaming, machine learning, and business intelligence into one shared foundation.

In Module F2, we go deep into OneLake — the storage layer that makes all of this possible. You will understand the hierarchical namespace in detail, Delta Lake internals (transaction logs, file layout, schema evolution), shortcuts and mirroring, OneLake data access roles, ADLS Gen2 API compatibility, storage metrics, and hands-on operations that you will use every day as a data engineer.

chapters.title: F2 OneLake Storage Foundation
chapters.description: Learn OneLake structure, storage patterns, and Delta-first data layout.

blocks.content: Module F2 OneLake — The Storage Foundation One Lake. One Copy. One Truth. Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Master OneLake as the unified storage layer — understand the hierarchical namespace, Delta Lake internals, shortcuts, mirroring, programmatic access, OneLake security, storage maintenance, and how every Fabric workload reads and writes through a single namespace.
Welcome to Module F2

In Module F1, you learned that Microsoft Fabric is a unified platform with seven workloads sharing one storage layer, one compute pool, and one security model. This module is about that storage layer. OneLake is the single most important architectural decision in Fabric, and understanding it deeply will change how you think about every other topic in this course. Every Delta table you create in a Lakehouse, every row you INSERT into a Warehouse, every streaming event you land in an Eventhouse, every column a Power BI report reads — all of it lives in OneLake. There is no second storage system. There is no sidecar. There is no cache that secretly holds the real data somewhere else. OneLake is it. The consequence is profound: when one workload writes data, every other workload can read it instantly, without copying, without ETL, without configuring a connection. A Spark notebook writes a Delta table. The Warehouse’s SQL engine queries it. Power BI visualizes it. A KQL queryset scans it. All reading from the same bytes on the same storage layer. That sentence is the single most important insight in this module. We will now take OneLake apart, layer by layer: the hierarchical namespace and the ADLS Gen2 foundation, the Delta Lake format that underpins every table (transaction log mechanics, checkpoint files, read protocol), shortcuts that bring external data into the namespace without copying, mirroring that replicates external databases, programmatic access via APIs and the File Explorer, the OneLake Catalog for discovery and governance, data formats and the conversion pattern, OneLake Security for storage-layer access control, V-Order optimization, storage maintenance (OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, Z-ORDER), monitoring and cost management, and a hands-on checkpoint lab.

OneLake is Fabric’s unified storage layer. It is provisioned automatically with every Fabric tenant — there is no setup, no storage account to create, no access key to generate. When your organization activates Fabric, OneLake exists. The best analogy is OneDrive for data. Every Microsoft 365 user gets a OneDrive that stores their documents in a single, managed namespace. Every Fabric tenant gets a OneLake that stores all analytical data in a single, managed namespace. The parallel is intentional — Microsoft designed OneLake to be as invisible as OneDrive. You do not think about where OneDrive stores your files. You should not have to think about where OneLake stores your tables. The Hierarchical Namespace OneLake organizes data in a four-level hierarchy:

Tenant (your organization — exactly one OneLake per tenant)
  │
  ├── Workspace: sales_prod
  │   ├── Lakehouse: lh_bronze_sales
  │   │   ├── Files/
  │   │   │   ├── landing/erp/orders_2026-03-07.csv
  │   │   │   └── landing/crm/customers.json
  │   │   └── Tables/
  │   │       ├── raw_orders/      (Delta table)
  │   │       │   ├── _delta_log/
  │   │       │   ├── part-00000.snappy.parquet
  │   │       │   └── part-00001.snappy.parquet
  │   │       └── raw_customers/   (Delta table)
  │   ├── Lakehouse: lh_gold_sales
  │   │   └── Tables/
  │   │       ├── fact_orders/
  │   │       └── dim_customers/
  │   └── Warehouse: wh_sales
  │       └── dbo.fact_orders  (Delta, via T-SQL)
  │
  └── Workspace: marketing_prod
      └── Lakehouse: lh_campaigns
          └── Tables/campaigns/

Tenant is the top level — your organization’s Fabric instance. Every Fabric tenant has exactly one OneLake. There is no multi-region OneLake and no per-department OneLake. One tenant, one lake. Workspace is the primary organizational container. Each workspace maps to a folder in OneLake. Workspaces carry security (who can access), capacity binding (which compute pool pays for operations), and governance scope (sensitivity labels, Git integration). Item is a Fabric object that owns storage. A Lakehouse is an item. A Warehouse is an item. An Eventhouse is an item. Each item gets its own folder within the workspace’s OneLake path. Not all items own storage — a Notebook, for example, does not have its own OneLake folder because it writes to a Lakehouse. Files and Tables are the data itself. Inside a Lakehouse, you see two sections: Files/ (for unstructured and semi-structured data like CSVs, JSONs, images) and Tables/ (for managed Delta tables). Inside a Warehouse, all data is in managed tables. Under the Hood: ADLS Gen2 OneLake is built on Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 (ADLS Gen2). The files in OneLake are stored in ADLS Gen2 containers with hierarchical namespace enabled. This is not an implementation detail you can ignore — it has practical consequences: ADLS Gen2 APIs work. Any tool that can read ADLS Gen2 (Azure Storage Explorer, azcopy, the Azure Storage SDKs, Spark with abfss:// paths) can read OneLake. The endpoint is onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com. Standard Parquet tooling works. Because the underlying files are Parquet (wrapped in Delta Lake’s transaction log), external tools like DuckDB, Polars, pandas, or Apache Arrow can read OneLake files directly if they authenticate correctly. File paths are predictable. The OneLake path follows the pattern:
https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/ {workspace_name}/{item_name}.{item_type}/{path}
Example: https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/ sales_prod/lh_bronze_sales.Lakehouse/Tables/raw_orders/

You do not normally need to think about ADLS Gen2. Fabric abstracts it away. But when you need to integrate with external tools, connect from outside Fabric, or debug storage-level issues, knowing this foundation is essential.

sections.title: Delta Lake — The Universal Table Format

blocks.content: Every table in Fabric is a Delta Lake table. This is not optional. It is not a configuration choice. It is a platform-level decision that applies to every Lakehouse table, every Warehouse table, every SQL Database table that replicates to OneLake, and every mirrored table. Delta Lake is an open-source storage layer, originally created by Databricks, that adds three critical capabilities to plain Parquet files: ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel. Understanding what Delta Lake is and how it works at the file level is essential because every table you touch in this course is a Delta table. What Makes a Delta Table A Delta table is not a single file. It is a directory containing two things: Parquet data files — The actual data, stored in columnar Parquet format. Each file contains a subset of the rows. The number of files depends on how the data was written and how many partitions exist. Parquet’s columnar layout means that reading a single column (e.g., SELECT amount FROM orders) only reads the bytes for that column, skipping all others. The _delta_log/ directory — A folder containing JSON files that form the transaction log. Each JSON file represents a commit — a record of what changed. The log is the source of truth for what data is currently in the table.

Tables/orders/
  ├── _delta_log/
  │   ├── 00000000000000000000.json    ← Commit 0 (table created)
  │   ├── 00000000000000000001.json    ← Commit 1 (rows appended)
  │   ├── 00000000000000000002.json    ← Commit 2 (MERGE executed)
  │   ├── 00000000000000000003.json    ← Commit 3 (more appends)
  │   └── 00000000000000000004.json    ← Commit 4 (OPTIMIZE ran)
  ├── part-00000-aaa.snappy.parquet   ← Active data file
  ├── part-00001-bbb.snappy.parquet   ← Active data file
  ├── part-00002-ccc.snappy.parquet   ← Active data file
  └── part-00003-ddd.snappy.parquet   ← Orphaned (removed by commit 4)

The Transaction Log in Detail The _delta_log/ is the heart of Delta Lake. Every operation — append, overwrite, update, delete, merge, optimize — creates a new JSON file in this directory. Each JSON file contains a list of actions: add — Records that a new Parquet file has been added to the table. Includes the file path, size, number of records, and column statistics (min/max values per column). remove — Records that a Parquet file is no longer part of the current table version. The file is not physically deleted yet — it is logically removed. VACUUM physically deletes it later. metaData — Records the table schema, partition columns, and table properties. Present in commit 0 and whenever the schema changes. commitInfo — Metadata about the commit: timestamp, operation type, who committed, and performance metrics.
Example: What a commit JSON looks like (simplified)

{ "add": { "path": "part-00000-aaa.snappy.parquet", "size": 48372891, "dataChange": true, "stats": "{\"numRecords\":125000, \"minValues\":{\"order_date\":\"2026-01-01\"}, \"maxValues\":{\"order_date\":\"2026-03-07\"}}" } } { "commitInfo": { "operation": "WRITE", "operationParameters": {"mode": "Append"}, "timestamp": 1741363200000 } }

When you read a Delta table, the engine reads the transaction log first to determine which Parquet files are currently valid (all “add” actions minus all “remove” actions). Then it reads only those files. When you write, the engine writes new Parquet files and appends a new JSON entry. The log commit is atomic — this is what enables ACID transactions. Checkpoint Files As the number of commits grows, reading every JSON file in _delta_log/ becomes expensive. Delta Lake solves this with checkpoint files — Parquet-formatted snapshots of the cumulative state of the transaction log at a specific version. By default, a checkpoint is created every 10 commits.

When reading the table, the engine finds _last_checkpoint, reads the checkpoint Parquet file (which contains the complete state at version 10), and then applies only the JSON commits after version 10 (commits 11, 12, etc.). This makes reads fast even for tables with thousands of commits. Why this matters for data engineers: If you are debugging a table that seems slow to read, check the _delta_log/. If there are hundreds of JSON files without a recent checkpoint, the table scan is reading all of them. Running OPTIMIZE triggers a new checkpoint. Understanding this mechanics is the difference between guessing and diagnosing. ACID Transactions ACID stands for Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, and Durability. In practical terms for data engineering: Atomicity — A write either fully succeeds or fully fails. You will never see a table in a half-written state. If a Spark job crashes mid-write, the transaction log is not updated, so readers never see partial data. The orphaned Parquet files are cleaned up by the next VACUUM. Consistency — Schema enforcement ensures that every row conforms to the table’s schema. You cannot accidentally write a string into an integer column. The write fails with a clear error before any data is committed. Isolation — Concurrent readers and writers do not interfere. A long-running read sees a consistent snapshot of the table (the version that existed when the read started), even if writes are happening simultaneously. This is called snapshot isolation. Durability — Once a commit is written to the transaction log, the data is durable in OneLake’s ADLS Gen2 storage. The data survives process crashes, cluster restarts, and session timeouts. Before Delta Lake, data lakes were notorious for the “data swamp” problem — files overwritten mid-read, schema drift, no rollback, no audit trail. Delta Lake solves all of these problems. Fabric adopts Delta as the universal format precisely because it turns a raw data lake into a reliable, governed data platform. Time Travel Because the transaction log records every commit, Delta Lake can reconstruct the table as it existed at any previous version. This is called time travel, and it is invaluable for debugging, auditing, and reproducibility.
PySpark Time Travel

# Read the table as it was 3 versions ago
df = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .option('versionAsOf', 3) \
    .load('Tables/orders')

# Read the table as it was at a specific timestamp
df = spark.read.format('delta') \
    .option('timestampAsOf', '2026-01-15') \
    .load('Tables/orders')
SQL Time Travel

SELECT * FROM orders VERSION AS OF 3

SELECT * FROM orders TIMESTAMP AS OF '2026-01-15T00:00:00'
View Table History
DESCRIBE HISTORY orders -- Returns: version, timestamp, userId, operation, operationParameters

Time travel is invaluable for: debugging (“what did this table look like before yesterday’s pipeline ran?”), auditing (“who changed what and when?”), reproducibility (“train the model on last Tuesday’s snapshot”), and recovery (“restore the table to the version before the bad write”). Important: Time travel depends on the transaction log and the underlying Parquet files. If you run VACUUM to delete old files, you lose the ability to travel to versions that referenced those files. VACUUM and time travel are in tension — you must balance storage cost against historical access. We cover VACUUM in detail in Part 9. Schema Enforcement and Evolution Delta Lake enforces schema on write. If you try to append a DataFrame with a column that does not exist in the table, or with a mismatched type, the write fails with a clear error. This is a safety mechanism that prevents silent data corruption.

# This will FAIL if df_new has a column not in the target table
df_new.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('orders')

# Schema evolution: allow new columns to be added automatically
df_new.write.format('delta') \
    .option('mergeSchema', 'true') \
    .mode('append') \
    .saveAsTable('orders')

# In SQL, you can also evolve schema explicitly
ALTER TABLE orders ADD COLUMNS (loyalty_tier STRING)

Schema enforcement prevents accidental corruption. Schema evolution provides controlled flexibility when the data model legitimately changes. Together, they give you the safety of a database with the flexibility of a data lake.

Shortcuts are virtual pointers to data that lives outside your Lakehouse — or even outside OneLake entirely. They make external data appear as if it were a native OneLake folder or table, without copying a single byte. This is one of Fabric’s most powerful architectural features. Enterprise data does not live in one place. It is scattered across Azure storage accounts, AWS S3 buckets, Google Cloud Storage, Dataverse environments, and other Fabric workspaces. Shortcuts let you bring all of that data into a single, unified namespace without data movement. How Shortcuts Work A shortcut is a metadata pointer stored in OneLake. When a query reads from a shortcut, the Fabric engine resolves the pointer and reads directly from the source. The data never transits through an intermediate copy.

lh_bronze_sales (Lakehouse)
  ├── Tables/
  │   ├── orders          (local Delta table)
  │   ├── products    ───▶ Shortcut → ADLS Gen2 account
  │   └── inventory   ───▶ Shortcut → Amazon S3 bucket
  └── Files/
      └── reference/  ───▶ Shortcut → Another Lakehouse

From the query engine's perspective, all four are identical.
  SELECT * FROM orders   -- works
  SELECT * FROM products -- works (reads from ADLS Gen2)
  SELECT * FROM inventory-- works (reads from S3)
Supported Shortcut Sources

Source Connection Type Notes Another Fabric Lakehouse Internal (OneLake) Cross-workspace, cross-tenant. No data movement. Ideal for sharing Gold tables across teams. Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 External (ADLS Gen2) Most common external shortcut. Requires authentication (service principal, managed identity, or shared key). Amazon S3 External (S3) Cross-cloud access. S3-compatible APIs. Requires IAM credentials configured in Fabric. Google Cloud Storage External (GCS) Cross-cloud access. Requires GCS credentials. Dataverse External (Dataverse) Direct access to Dynamics 365 / Power Platform data. No export pipeline needed. OneLake within same workspace Internal (OneLake) Useful for creating logical views across Lakehouses within the same project.

Creating Shortcuts Shortcuts can be created through the Fabric portal UI or programmatically via the OneLake REST API. In the portal: open your Lakehouse, right-click on the Tables/ or Files/ section, select “New shortcut,” choose the source type, provide connection details, name the shortcut, and confirm. The shortcut appears instantly. No data is copied. Shortcut Best Practices Use shortcuts for reference data sharing. If your Gold Lakehouse has dimension tables that multiple teams need, other teams should create shortcuts to those tables rather than copying them. One copy, many readers. Use shortcuts for cross-cloud access. If your organization stores some data in AWS S3, create S3 shortcuts rather than building copy pipelines. This eliminates data movement costs and latency. Be aware of cross-region latency. A shortcut to an S3 bucket in us-east-1 from a Fabric capacity in West Europe will add network latency to every query. For high-frequency queries, consider whether the latency is acceptable or whether mirroring is a better option. Shortcuts respect source permissions. Access to shortcutted data requires both Fabric permissions (can the user see the Lakehouse?) and source permissions (does the connection credential have access to the external path?). Warning: Shortcuts to external sources are read-only. You cannot write to an S3 bucket or ADLS Gen2 container through a shortcut. Writes must go through native Fabric mechanisms. Internal shortcuts (to another Lakehouse) also follow the source’s write permissions.

Mirroring is Fabric’s mechanism for near-real-time replication of external databases into OneLake. Unlike shortcuts (which point to data in place without copying), mirroring creates a continuously updated copy of the source data as Delta tables in OneLake. This is the bridge between operational systems and the analytical platform. Your transactional database keeps running its OLTP workload. Fabric mirrors the data into OneLake, where every analytical workload can query it without impacting the source. Supported Mirroring Sources

Source Replication Method Status Azure SQL Database Change Data Capture (CDC) Generally Available Azure SQL Managed Instance CDC via gateway Generally Available SQL Server (on-prem / VM) CDC or Change Feed (SQL Server 2025) Preview Azure Cosmos DB (NoSQL) Change Feed Generally Available Snowflake Managed tables + Iceberg tables Generally Available PostgreSQL (Azure / generic) Logical replication Generally Available Azure Databricks (Delta) Change Data Feed Preview

How Mirroring Works Initial snapshot — Fabric reads the entire source table and writes it as a Delta table in OneLake. This is a one-time bulk copy. Continuous replication — After the snapshot, Fabric subscribes to the source’s change feed (CDC, logical replication, etc.) and applies incremental changes to the Delta table. Inserts, updates, and deletes flow automatically. Delta table output — The result is a standard Delta table in OneLake, queryable by all Fabric workloads. The table includes system columns for tracking replication metadata. Latency depends on the source and change volume, but is typically seconds to minutes. This is not a daily batch — it is near-real-time replication. Mirroring vs. Shortcuts vs. Pipelines

Approach Data Movement Latency Best For Shortcut None (read in place) Real-time (network latency only) External analytics files (ADLS, S3). Reference data. Cross-Lakehouse sharing. Mirroring Continuous replication Seconds to minutes Operational databases (SQL, Cosmos, Snowflake). Near-real-time analytics on transactional data. Pipeline (Copy) Batch copy Minutes to hours (schedule-dependent) One-time loads, complex transformations, sources without CDC support.

Choose shortcuts when you want to query external data without copying it. Choose mirroring when you need a continuously fresh copy of a transactional database. Choose pipelines when you need batch control, transformation logic, or the source does not support change feeds.

sections.title: OneLake File Explorer and Programmatic Access

blocks.content: OneLake is not locked inside the Fabric portal. You can access it from your desktop, from scripts, and from external tools. OneLake File Explorer OneLake File Explorer is a Windows application that mounts OneLake as a drive on your local machine. Once installed and authenticated, you see your Fabric workspaces, Lakehouses, and files in Windows File Explorer — just like a mapped network drive. This enables dragging CSV files directly into a Lakehouse, opening Parquet files in local tools, and copying files without using the portal. Important: OneLake File Explorer is currently Windows-only. macOS and Linux users can access OneLake through the REST API or the Azure Storage SDKs. Programmatic Access via REST API OneLake exposes a standard ADLS Gen2-compatible REST API. Any HTTP client can interact with OneLake files:

# Python: List files in a Lakehouse using the Azure Storage SDK
from azure.identity import DefaultAzureCredential
from azure.storage.filedatalake import DataLakeServiceClient

credential = DefaultAzureCredential()
service = DataLakeServiceClient(
    account_url='https://onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com',
    credential=credential
)

# Navigate the hierarchy
fs = service.get_file_system_client('sales_prod')  # workspace
paths = fs.get_paths('lh_bronze_sales.Lakehouse/Files')
for path in paths:
    print(path.name)

The endpoint is always onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com. Authentication uses Microsoft Entra ID tokens. The Azure Identity library’s DefaultAzureCredential handles this automatically. Access from Notebooks (abfss:// Protocol) Inside a Fabric Notebook, you access OneLake through the integrated Spark session. The Lakehouse attached to your Notebook is automatically mounted, so you can reference files and tables with short paths:

# Read a Delta table from the attached Lakehouse
df = spark.read.format('delta').load('Tables/orders')

# Read a CSV from the Files section
df = spark.read.csv('Files/landing/erp/orders.csv', header=True)

# Write a new Delta table
df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').save('Tables/clean_orders')
Cross-Lakehouse Access

# Use abfss:// paths for cross-Lakehouse access
df = spark.read.format('delta').load(
    'abfss://sales_prod@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/'
    'lh_gold_sales.Lakehouse/Tables/fact_orders'
)

The abfss:// protocol is the Azure Blob File System Secure protocol. It is the standard way to reference ADLS Gen2 paths in Spark. The pattern is: abfss://{workspace}@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/{item_name}.{item_type}/{path}. You will use this pattern whenever your Notebook needs to read from a Lakehouse that is not directly attached.

A data lake is only useful if people can find what is in it. OneLake Catalog is Fabric’s built-in discovery and metadata layer. It provides a searchable, browsable index of every data asset in your Fabric tenant. What the Catalog Indexes Every Fabric item — Lakehouses, Warehouses, Notebooks, Pipelines, Reports, Semantic Models, Eventhouses, SQL Databases. Tables and columns — For every Delta table, the catalog knows the schema: column names, data types, and nullability. Metadata — Owner, last modified date, workspace, domain, sensitivity labels, endorsement status. Lineage — Where data came from and where it flows. If a Pipeline ingests data into a Lakehouse that feeds a Warehouse that feeds a Power BI report, the catalog tracks that chain. Domains, Tags, and Endorsement Domains are organizational groups that map to business areas (Finance, Marketing, Operations). Workspaces can be assigned to domains, and the catalog lets you browse by domain. This is Fabric’s approach to data mesh governance — each domain owns its data while participating in a shared platform. Tags are custom labels that domain administrators can define. Items can be tagged with domain-specific metadata (e.g., “quarterly_report”, “pii_data”, “deprecated”). Tags improve discoverability beyond names and descriptions. Endorsement signals data quality. Two levels: Promoted (the owner considers it ready for broad use) and Certified (an authorized reviewer has verified it meets organizational standards). Endorsed items appear with badges in the catalog, making it easy to distinguish production-quality assets from experimental work.

sections.title: Data Formats — Delta, Parquet, CSV, JSON

blocks.content: OneLake can store any file format. But not all formats are equal in Fabric, and understanding the hierarchy of formats shapes your data engineering decisions.

Format Type Schema Transactions When Used in Fabric Delta Lake Columnar + log Enforced Full ACID Every managed table. Default and preferred format for all structured data. Parquet Columnar Embedded None Raw data files, external data, interop with non-Delta tools. Foundation of Delta. CSV Row-based text None None Landing zone for external data, exports from legacy systems. Always convert to Delta for analytics. JSON / NDJSON Semi-structured text None None API responses, event logs. Read with Spark, convert to Delta.
The Conversion Pattern A core pattern in Fabric data engineering: land raw data in any format in Files/, then convert to Delta in Tables/ as quickly as possible.

External Source (CSV, JSON, Parquet, API response)
          │
          ▼
  Files/landing/   (raw files, as-is from source)
          │
          ▼  [PySpark Notebook: read, validate, add metadata]
          │
  Tables/raw_data  (Delta table — schema-enforced, queryable)

Once data is in Delta format, every Fabric workload can query it: Spark, the Warehouse SQL engine, the Lakehouse SQL analytics endpoint, Power BI DirectLake, and KQL. Raw CSV and JSON files in Files/ are only accessible through Spark or direct file APIs — they cannot be queried by the SQL engine or Power BI. Core principle: The Files/ section is for landing and staging. The Tables/ section is for analytics-ready data. Get data from Files/ to Tables/ as early as possible in your pipeline. This is a fundamental rule of Fabric data engineering. Why Not Just Use Parquet? Parquet is an excellent columnar format, and Delta Lake is built on top of it. So why not skip Delta and use plain Parquet? The answer is the transaction log. Without it, you lose ACID transactions (concurrent reads and writes can corrupt data), schema enforcement (nothing prevents writing mismatched columns), time travel (no history of changes), MERGE, UPDATE, DELETE (plain Parquet only supports full-file overwrites), and the Warehouse SQL engine (which requires Delta format). In Fabric, Delta is always the right choice for structured analytical data. Plain Parquet is appropriate only for interoperability with external systems that do not understand Delta.

OneLake Security is Fabric’s vision for unified data access control enforced at the storage layer. The idea is simple and powerful: security rules live with the data, not with the engine. No matter which Fabric engine reads the data — Spark, SQL, KQL, Power BI — the same security rules apply. This is a fundamental shift from the legacy approach, where each engine had its own security model. In the old world, you configured row-level security separately in Synapse SQL, in Power BI, and in Databricks. If they were inconsistent, users could see different data depending on which tool they used. OneLake Security eliminates this by pushing security to the storage layer itself. Security Layers

Layer Granularity Mechanism Workspace Entire workspace Workspace roles: Admin, Member, Contributor, Viewer. Controls who can see and modify any item. Item Single item Per-item sharing and permissions. Controls who can access a specific Lakehouse, Warehouse, or Report. Table Single table within an item OneLake Security table-level permissions. Controls which tables a user can read. Column Specific columns Column-level security. Hides sensitive columns (e.g., SSN, salary) from unauthorized users. Row Specific rows Row-level security (RLS). Users see only the rows they are authorized to access (e.g., only their region).
How OneLake Security Applies Across Engines

User Query (Spark / SQL / KQL / Power BI)
          │
          ▼
  ┌───────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │         OneLake Security Layer            │
  │  ─ Check table access: allowed?          │
  │  ─ Check column access: which visible?   │
  │  ─ Check row-level rules: which rows?    │
  └─────────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                      │
                      ▼
          Filtered data returned to engine
                      │
                      ▼
          Query result (authorized data only)

This means a data engineer can configure security once, and it applies everywhere — in Notebooks, in the Warehouse, in Power BI reports, and in KQL queries. No duplication. No inconsistency. Important: OneLake Security is a newer capability that reached public preview in 2025 and is progressing toward general availability. During preview, some limitations may apply. Many organizations still configure engine-specific security (T-SQL RLS in the Warehouse, DAX RLS in Power BI) alongside OneLake Security. As the feature matures, the goal is a single security configuration point.
