Delta tables are not “set and forget.” Over time, frequent writes create small files (the small file problem), old versions accumulate unused Parquet files, and data becomes physically unordered. Three maintenance operations keep tables healthy, fast, and cost-efficient. OPTIMIZE — Compacting Small Files Every time you append data to a Delta table, Spark writes new Parquet files. If you append 1,000 rows every hour, you get 24 small files per day, 168 per week, 720 per month. Each file adds overhead: the engine must open, read metadata from, and close each file during a query. Below a certain file size (~128 MB is the Fabric target), the overhead of opening files dominates actual data reading. OPTIMIZE compacts many small files into fewer large files. It rewrites the data without changing any values — the logical content of the table is identical before and after.
-- Basic OPTIMIZE: compact small files OPTIMIZE orders
-- OPTIMIZE with Z-ORDER: compact AND cluster by column(s) OPTIMIZE orders ZORDER BY (customer_id)
-- OPTIMIZE with Z-ORDER on multiple columns OPTIMIZE orders ZORDER BY (customer_id, order_date)
-- In PySpark from delta.tables import DeltaTable dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'Tables/orders') dt.optimize().executeCompaction() dt.optimize().executeZOrderBy('customer_id')

Z-ORDER — Data Clustering Z-ORDER is a clustering technique that co-locates related data in the same files. When you Z-ORDER by customer_id, all rows for a given customer end up in the same (or nearby) files. This means a query filtering by customer_id skips most files entirely — the column statistics in the transaction log tell the engine which files could possibly contain the target customer_id.

Before Z-ORDER:                    After Z-ORDER:
  File 1: customers A,M,Z,B,Q        File 1: customers A,B,C,D,E
  File 2: customers C,X,A,F,P        File 2: customers F,G,H,I,J
  File 3: customers D,A,R,Z,E        File 3: customers K,L,M,N,O

  WHERE customer_id = 'A'            WHERE customer_id = 'A'
  → Must scan all 3 files             → Scan only File 1 (data skip!)

Z-ORDER is most effective on columns that appear frequently in WHERE clauses. Choose 1–3 columns. More than 3 reduces effectiveness because the clustering becomes diluted across too many dimensions. VACUUM — Reclaiming Storage When OPTIMIZE rewrites files, or when UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE logically removes data, the old Parquet files are not physically deleted. They are marked as “removed” in the transaction log but remain on disk. This is by design — time travel needs those files to reconstruct historical versions. VACUUM physically deletes Parquet files that are no longer referenced by any version within the retention period:
-- Delete unreferenced files older than 7 days (default) VACUUM orders
-- Delete unreferenced files older than 24 hours VACUUM orders RETAIN 24 HOURS

-- In PySpark
dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'Tables/orders')
dt.vacuum(168)  # 168 hours = 7 days

Warning: Setting VACUUM retention below 7 days is dangerous. Any readers or writers that started before the vacuum may fail because files they expected are gone. In production, stick to the 7-day default unless you have a specific reason and fully understand the risk. After vacuuming, you cannot time-travel to versions older than the retention period. The Maintenance Schedule For production tables that receive regular writes, a standard maintenance pattern:

Run OPTIMIZE first, then VACUUM. OPTIMIZE creates new compacted files and marks old small files as removed. VACUUM then physically deletes the removed files. If you VACUUM without OPTIMIZE first, you reclaim space from old versions but the small file problem persists. V-Order Optimization V-Order is a Fabric-proprietary write-time optimization that reorders data within Parquet files for faster reads across all engines. When V-Order is enabled (which it is by default in Fabric), writes take slightly longer, but reads are significantly faster — especially for Power BI DirectLake and SQL analytics workloads. V-Order applies columnar sorting, dictionary encoding, and other optimizations that are compatible with standard Parquet readers but optimized for Fabric’s engines. You do not need to configure it — it is on by default. But understanding that it exists explains why Fabric-written Parquet files often perform better than Parquet files written by external tools. If you are writing data from outside Fabric (e.g., from a Databricks cluster or a Python script) and then reading it in Fabric, the data will not have V-Order. Running OPTIMIZE in Fabric re-writes the files with V-Order applied, giving you the read performance boost.

How Storage Is Billed You pay for the total volume of data stored in OneLake, measured in GB per month: Data stored in OneLake — All Parquet data files, Delta transaction logs, and Files/ section contents count toward your storage consumption. Shortcut data is NOT billed in OneLake — Because shortcuts point to external data, the storage cost stays with the source provider (ADLS Gen2, S3, GCS). Mirrored data IS billed in OneLake — Because mirroring creates a physical copy in Delta format, the replicated data counts toward your OneLake storage. Soft-deleted and versioned data — Old Delta file versions that have not been vacuumed still consume storage. This is a common source of unexpected costs. DESCRIBE DETAIL — Inspecting Table Storage The most useful command for understanding a Delta table’s physical state:
-- SQL DESCRIBE DETAIL orders

-- Returns:
-- format: delta
-- location: abfss://...
-- numFiles: 47           <-- number of active Parquet files
-- sizeInBytes: 398217340 <-- total size of active files
-- properties: {delta.enableChangeDataFeed: true, ...}
-- PySpark equivalent dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, 'Tables/orders') dt.detail().show()

Run DESCRIBE DETAIL before and after OPTIMIZE to see the file count drop and the average file size increase. This is the most direct way to verify that maintenance is working. Monitoring Storage Consumption Fabric Capacity Metrics app — Shows storage consumption trends at the capacity and workspace level. Workspace settings — Each workspace shows its current storage consumption. DESCRIBE DETAIL per table — Returns the size of a specific Delta table including file count and total bytes. DESCRIBE HISTORY — Returns the full commit history of a Delta table: version, timestamp, operation, user, and metrics. Cost Optimization Best Practices

items: Run OPTIMIZE regularly on tables that receive frequent small appends (daily or weekly).
Run VACUUM after OPTIMIZE to reclaim storage from old file versions.
Use shortcuts instead of copies for reference data and cross-team sharing.
Use partitioning judiciously — too many partitions create too many small files, increasing storage overhead and query latency.
Convert raw files (CSV, JSON) to Delta as early as possible — Delta’s columnar compression is dramatically more storage-efficient.
Monitor storage weekly during early adoption to understand growth patterns and set retention policies.
For large tables, consider incremental OPTIMIZE (where clause) to avoid rewriting the entire table on every maintenance run.

sections.title: OneLake Production Patterns for Data Engineers

blocks.content: Now that you understand OneLake’s architecture, formats, security, and maintenance, here are the production patterns that successful data engineering teams follow. Pattern 1: The Landing Zone Raw data from external sources always lands in Files/ first, never directly into Tables/. This preserves the original file as an audit trail and allows the Bronze-loading notebook to add metadata columns (_load_timestamp, _source_file, _source_system) during the conversion to Delta. If the Bronze notebook fails, the raw file is still there for re-processing.

Files/                                  Tables/
  ├── landing/                              ├── raw_orders (Delta)
  │   ├── erp/                              ├── raw_customers (Delta)
  │   │   └── orders_2026-03-07.csv       └── raw_products (Delta)
  │   ├── crm/
  │   │   └── customers.json             Pipeline lands files
  │   └── catalog/                       in Files/landing/.
  │       └── products.json              Notebook reads Files/,
  └── archive/                               writes to Tables/.
      └── erp/2026-03-06/  (processed)

Pattern 2: Cross-Lakehouse Shortcuts for Medallion Layers In a medallion architecture with separate Lakehouses for Bronze, Silver, and Gold, use shortcuts to make upstream tables available downstream without copying. The Silver notebook reads from lh_bronze via a shortcut. The Gold notebook reads from lh_silver via a shortcut. Each Lakehouse owns its layer’s data while having read access to the layer below. Pattern 3: Partitioning Strategy Partitioning physically organizes data into subdirectories based on column values. A table partitioned by date stores each date’s data in a separate folder. This enables partition pruning — queries filtering by date skip entire folders.

# Write with partition
df.write.format('delta') \
    .partitionBy('order_date') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .saveAsTable('orders_partitioned')

# Resulting structure:
# Tables/orders_partitioned/
#   order_date=2026-03-01/  part-00000.parquet
#   order_date=2026-03-02/  part-00000.parquet
#   order_date=2026-03-03/  part-00000.parquet

Partitioning warning: Over-partitioning is one of the most common mistakes in data engineering. If you partition by a high-cardinality column (e.g., customer_id with 1 million unique values), you create 1 million subdirectories, each with tiny files. This destroys query performance. Partition only on low-cardinality columns (date, region, status) where each partition has at least 100 MB of data. For high-cardinality filtering, use Z-ORDER instead of partitioning. Pattern 4: File Organization Conventions A consistent Files/ structure makes Lakehouses navigable across teams:

Files/
  landing/           # Raw files from external sources
    {source}/        # One folder per source system
      {file}         # Landing files (CSV, JSON, Parquet)
  archive/           # Processed files moved here after loading
    {source}/{date}/ # Archived by source and processing date
  temp/              # Temporary files (notebook scratch space)
  reference/         # Static reference data (country codes, etc.)
Module F2 Checkpoint
You now understand:

items: OneLake architecture — the four-level hierarchy (tenant → workspace → item → files and tables), ADLS Gen2 under the hood, and the onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com endpoint
Delta Lake internals — Parquet data files + JSON transaction log, checkpoint files every 10 commits, the read protocol (find checkpoint, apply subsequent commits), add/remove actions
ACID transactions — atomicity (no partial writes), consistency (schema enforcement), isolation (snapshot reads), durability (committed = persisted)
Time travel — VERSION AS OF, TIMESTAMP AS OF, DESCRIBE HISTORY, and the tension between time travel and VACUUM
Schema enforcement and evolution — write-time validation, mergeSchema option, ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMNS
Shortcuts — virtual pointers to external data (ADLS Gen2, S3, GCS, Dataverse, other Lakehouses) for zero-copy access
Mirroring — near-real-time replication from operational databases (SQL, Cosmos, Snowflake, PostgreSQL) into OneLake Delta tables
Programmatic access — OneLake File Explorer, ADLS Gen2 REST APIs, Azure Storage SDKs, abfss:// paths in Spark, cross-Lakehouse access patterns
OneLake Catalog — discovery, domains, tags, endorsement, and lineage tracking
Data formats — Delta as the preferred format, the Files/ → Tables/ conversion pattern, why plain Parquet is insufficient
OneLake Security — table-level, column-level, and row-level security enforced at the storage layer across all engines
Storage maintenance — OPTIMIZE (compaction), Z-ORDER (clustering), VACUUM (reclaim space), V-Order (read optimization), DESCRIBE DETAIL
Production patterns — landing zones, cross-Lakehouse shortcuts, partitioning strategy, file organization conventions

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab
Before moving to Module DE1, complete these exercises in a Fabric workspace:

items: Create a Lakehouse. Upload a CSV file to Files/landing/. Write a PySpark notebook that reads the CSV, adds a _load_timestamp column, and writes it as a Delta table in Tables/. Verify the table appears in the SQL analytics endpoint.
Run DESCRIBE DETAIL on your Delta table in a SQL cell. Note the file count and size. Run DESCRIBE HISTORY to see the commit log.
Append more rows to the table (write the same CSV again with mode ‘append’). Run DESCRIBE HISTORY again. Observe the new commit. Use VERSION AS OF to read the table as it was before the append.
Run OPTIMIZE on the table. Run DESCRIBE DETAIL again. Compare file count before and after.

Create a shortcut in your Lakehouse that points to another Lakehouse (or an ADLS Gen2 container if available). Query the shortcutted data from a Notebook and confirm it works identically to local data.
Open the OneLake Catalog. Search for the items you created. Examine the metadata: schema, owner, workspace, lineage.
Navigate to the _delta_log/ folder of your table using a Notebook (list files in the directory). Count the JSON files. Identify whether a checkpoint file exists.
Run VACUUM on the table. Verify with DESCRIBE DETAIL that storage is reclaimed. Attempt time travel to a version before the vacuum and observe the error.

blocks.content: You are not just storing files.

You are operating a unified storage layer that serves as the single source of truth for every engine, every workload, and every user in your organization. Every table is transactional. Every file is discoverable. Every access is governed. Every version is tracked.

In Module DE1, we build on this foundation with the Lakehouse — the primary data engineering item in Fabric. You will learn how to create Lakehouses, load data through every available path, manage the dual Files/ and Tables/ interface, use the SQL analytics endpoint, understand when to choose a Lakehouse over a Warehouse, and establish the organizational patterns that keep a multi-Lakehouse architecture clean and maintainable.

chapters.title: DE1 Lakehouse Fundamentals
chapters.description: Build practical Lakehouse fundamentals for ingestion, tables, and analytics readiness.

blocks.content: Module DE1 Lakehouse Fundamentals Where Files Meet Tables Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Build and operate a Lakehouse — the core data engineering item in Fabric. Master the dual interface (Files + Tables), every ingestion path, the SQL analytics endpoint, the default semantic model, Lakehouse vs. Warehouse decision framework, multi-Lakehouse architecture, and production best practices.
Welcome to Module DE1

In Module F1, you learned the architecture. In Module F2, you learned the storage layer. Now you build on that foundation with the item you will use more than any other in this course: the Lakehouse. The Lakehouse is Fabric’s primary data engineering artifact. It is where raw files land, where Delta tables are created, where Spark notebooks read and write, where the SQL analytics endpoint auto-generates a T-SQL surface, and where Power BI’s DirectLake mode reads data for dashboards. If OneLake is the storage layer, the Lakehouse is the door through which most data enters and exits that layer. That sentence is the single most important insight in this module. The Lakehouse implements the lakehouse paradigm — a modern architecture that combines the flexibility of a data lake (store anything, any format, any scale) with the structure of a data warehouse (schema enforcement, SQL querying, ACID transactions). Before this paradigm existed, organizations had to choose between a lake (flexible but ungoverned) and a warehouse (governed but rigid). The Lakehouse gives you both in one item. This module takes you from creation to production: what a Lakehouse is and what Fabric creates behind the scenes, every way to load data into it, the dual Files/ and Tables/ interface with code examples for each, the auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint and its capabilities and limitations, the default semantic model and DirectLake, when to choose a Lakehouse over a Warehouse, multi-Lakehouse architecture for medallion layers, production best practices, and a comprehensive hands-on checkpoint.

The Lakehouse is a Fabric item that provides a dual-interface storage experience: an unstructured file system (Files/) and a managed table system (Tables/), both backed by OneLake. It is the convergence point where raw data arrives and structured, analytics-ready data emerges. The Lakehouse Paradigm The lakehouse paradigm was popularized by Databricks and has become the dominant architectural pattern for modern data platforms. The core idea: use an open file format (Delta Lake on Parquet) stored in a data lake, but layer on the governance features traditionally found only in warehouses — ACID transactions, schema enforcement, SQL access, and fine-grained security. Fabric’s Lakehouse is a concrete implementation of this paradigm. When you create a Lakehouse in Fabric, you get four things in one item: A Files/ section — A folder hierarchy in OneLake for landing raw, unstructured, or semi-structured data. CSVs, JSONs, images, Parquet files, XML — anything can go here. No schema is enforced. This is your landing zone. A Tables/ section — A managed area where Delta tables live. Every table here has a schema, ACID transactions, and a transaction log. These tables are queryable by Spark, by the SQL analytics endpoint, and by Power BI. A SQL analytics endpoint — An auto-generated, read-only T-SQL interface that exposes every Delta table in the Tables/ section. SQL analysts can query Lakehouse tables without Spark, without Python, without notebooks. They just write T-SQL. A default semantic model — An auto-generated Power BI semantic model that makes Lakehouse tables immediately available for reporting. You can build a Power BI report directly on top of a Lakehouse without any additional modeling.
The Four-in-One Lakehouse

┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │                    LAKEHOUSE                      │
  ├─────────────────────────┬───────────────────────────┤
  │  Files/              │  Tables/                    │
  │  (raw, any format)   │  (managed Delta tables)     │
  │  CSVs, JSON, images  │  Schema-enforced, ACID      │
  │  No schema required  │  Queryable by ALL engines   │
  └─────────────────────────┴───────────────────────────┘
                        │                  │
                        ▼                  ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  SQL Analytics Endpoint (auto-generated)          │
  │  Read-only T-SQL over Tables/ Delta tables        │
  │  JOINs, CTEs, window functions, views, security   │
  └──────────────────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
                         │
                         ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  Default Semantic Model (auto-generated)          │
  │  DirectLake — Power BI reads Delta from OneLake   │
  │  No import, no refresh, near-import performance    │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

This four-in-one design is what makes the Lakehouse the most versatile item in Fabric. It serves data engineers (Files/ + Spark), SQL analysts (SQL analytics endpoint), and BI developers (default semantic model) from a single item.

Creating a Lakehouse takes seconds. There is no cluster to provision, no storage account to configure, no schema to define upfront. You give it a name, and Fabric does the rest.
Step-by-Step Creation
•  Navigate to your workspace in the Fabric portal.
•  Click “+ New item” (or use the New button in the top toolbar).
•  Select “Lakehouse” from the item types. (Switch to the Data Engineering workload if you don’t see it.)
•  Enter a name following your naming convention (e.g., lh_bronze_sales).
•  Click “Create.”
Within seconds, your Lakehouse appears. You will see two empty sections in the explorer pane: Files and Tables. Both are backed by OneLake.
What Happens Behind the Scenes
When you create a Lakehouse, Fabric does several things automatically:
Creates an OneLake folder — The folder structure is: {workspace}/{lakehouse_name}.Lakehouse/Files/ and {workspace}/{lakehouse_name}.Lakehouse/Tables/.
Generates a SQL analytics endpoint — A separate Fabric item that provides a read-only T-SQL interface over the Tables/ section. It has its own connection string and appears as a SQL database in tools like SSMS and Azure Data Studio.
Creates a default semantic model — A DirectLake Power BI dataset that mirrors the tables in the Lakehouse. Automatically updated when tables change.
Registers in the OneLake Catalog — The Lakehouse is discoverable, searchable, and trackable from the moment it exists.
You do not configure any of this. It is automatic. This is the SaaS advantage — Fabric handles the wiring so you can focus on the data.
Navigating the Lakehouse Explorer
The Lakehouse explorer in the Fabric portal has three views, each representing a different lens on the same data:
Lakehouse view — The default view showing Files/ and Tables/ sections. This is where data engineers work. You can upload files, create folders, preview data, and manage tables.
SQL analytics endpoint view — Tables appear as SQL objects. You can write T-SQL queries, inspect schemas, create views, and configure permissions. The view looks like a traditional database explorer.
Default semantic model view — The auto-generated Power BI model. You can manage which tables are included, define relationships, and create basic measures.
All three views represent the same underlying Delta tables in OneLake. They are three interfaces on one Lakehouse, just as the seven workloads are seven interfaces on one platform.
Data enters a Lakehouse through multiple paths. Understanding all of them lets you choose the right one for each scenario.

Ingestion Method Target Code Required Best For Portal upload (drag-and-drop) Files/ None Quick uploads of small files during development. Not for production. OneLake File Explorer Files/ None Dragging files from your Windows desktop into OneLake. Good for ad-hoc loading. Notebook (PySpark) Files/ or Tables/ Python / Spark SQL Production ETL pipelines. Reading any format, transforming, writing Delta tables. Pipeline (Copy Activity) Files/ or Tables/ None (visual) Scheduled batch ingestion from 300+ sources. Enterprise orchestration. Dataflow Gen2 Tables/ None (visual) Low-code transformations with Power Query. Analyst-friendly ingestion. Shortcut Files/ or Tables/ None Zero-copy access to external data (ADLS Gen2, S3, other Lakehouses). No data movement. Mirroring Tables/ None Near-real-time replication from operational databases (SQL, Cosmos, Snowflake). ADLS Gen2 API / SDK Files/ Python / REST Programmatic upload from external systems. CI/CD pipelines. Automated data feeds.

The Typical Ingestion Flow In most production environments, the ingestion flow follows a two-step pattern: Step 1: Land raw data in Files/. Use a Pipeline Copy Activity, an API upload, or a shortcut to place raw files (CSV, JSON, Parquet) into the Files/ section. No transformation happens here — the goal is to capture the source data as-is, as quickly as possible. Step 2: Transform and write to Tables/. A Spark notebook reads from Files/, applies cleaning and transformation logic (type casting, deduplication, null handling, metadata columns), and writes the result as a Delta table in Tables/. Once data is in Tables/, it is queryable by every engine in Fabric.

External Source (ERP, CRM, API, file share)
          │
          ▼  [Pipeline Copy Activity / API / Shortcut]
  Files/landing/{source}/   (CSV, JSON, Parquet — as-is)
          │
          ▼  [Notebook: spark.read → transform → df.write]
  Tables/raw_{entity}       (Delta table — queryable by all engines)
          │
          ▼  [SQL Analytics Endpoint / Power BI / Warehouse]
  Downstream consumers

This two-step pattern is the foundation of the medallion architecture (Bronze → Silver → Gold) that we formalize in Module DE7. For now, the key principle is: land fast in Files/, transform into Tables/. Code: Loading Data from Files/ to Tables/ The most common operation in Fabric data engineering — reading raw files and creating Delta tables:
Reading CSV

# Read CSV from the Files/ landing zone
raw = spark.read.csv(
    'Files/landing/erp/orders_2026-03-07.csv',
    header=True,
    inferSchema=True
)
raw.printSchema()
raw.show(5)
Reading JSON

# Read JSON (single file or directory)
customers = spark.read.json(
    'Files/landing/crm/customers.json',
    multiLine=True  # Set True if JSON is pretty-printed
)
Reading Parquet

# Read Parquet (most efficient raw format)
products = spark.read.parquet('Files/landing/catalog/')
Writing Delta Tables

# Write as a managed Delta table (recommended)
raw.write.format('delta') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .saveAsTable('raw_orders')

# Append mode (for incremental loads)
new_data.write.format('delta') \
    .mode('append') \
    .saveAsTable('raw_orders')

# Write with metadata columns (production pattern)
from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, lit, input_file_name

raw_with_meta = raw \
    .withColumn('_load_timestamp', current_timestamp()) \
    .withColumn('_source_system', lit('ERP')) \
    .withColumn('_source_file', input_file_name())

raw_with_meta.write.format('delta') \
    .mode('append') \
    .saveAsTable('raw_orders')

Important: The metadata columns (_load_timestamp, _source_system, _source_file) are critical for production. When something goes wrong — and it will — these columns let you trace which file produced which rows, when the data was loaded, and from which source system. Always add them at the Bronze layer. This is not optional for production pipelines. Direct Table Write (Skipping Files/) You can also write directly to Tables/ without landing in Files/ first. A Pipeline Copy Activity can write Parquet directly to the Tables/ section, where Fabric auto-registers it as a Delta table. This shortcut is useful for simple ingestion scenarios where no transformation is needed. However, you lose the audit trail of the original file and the ability to re-process if the load has issues.

sections.title: The Files Section — Unstructured and Semi-Structured

blocks.content:
The Files/ section of a Lakehouse is an open folder hierarchy in OneLake. There are no rules about what you put here, no schema enforcement, and no table registration. It is your landing zone, your staging area, and your archive.
What Goes in Files/
•  Raw CSV exports from source systems
•  JSON payloads from APIs
•  Parquet files from external pipelines
•  Images, PDFs, and binary files for ML workloads
•  XML, NDJSON, and other semi-structured formats
•  Archived data that needs to be retained but not queried frequently
Organizing the Files/ Hierarchy
A disciplined folder structure prevents the Files/ section from becoming a data swamp:

  Files/
  ├── landing/              # Raw files from external sources
  │   ├── erp/              # Source system: ERP
  │   │   ├── orders_2026-03-06.csv
  │   │   └── orders_2026-03-07.csv
  │   ├── crm/              # Source system: CRM
  │   │   └── customers_full.json
  │   └── catalog/           # Source system: Product Catalog
  │       └── products.parquet
  ├── staging/              # Temporary files during processing
  │   └── merge_temp/
  ├── archive/              # Processed files moved here
  │   └── erp/2026-03-06/   # Archived by source and date
  └── reference/            # Static reference data
      └── country_codes.csv

Key principles: use date-based subfolders for incremental loads, separate landing from staging from archive, and name folders descriptively. This structure makes it easy for notebooks to read specific partitions (e.g., spark.read.csv('Files/landing/erp/orders_2026-03-07.csv')) and for humans to navigate in the portal. Files/ Limitations Files in the Files/ section are not queryable by the SQL analytics endpoint or by Power BI DirectLake. Only Delta tables in the Tables/ section are visible to those engines. This is by design — raw files lack the schema and transaction guarantees that SQL and BI tools require. If you need to query raw files with SQL, you have two options: convert them to Delta tables (the recommended path), or use Spark SQL’s ability to query files directly from a Notebook:
%%sql -- Query CSV files directly (exploration only, not for production) SELECT * FROM csv.`Files/landing/erp/orders_2026-03-07.csv` LIMIT 10
-- Query Parquet files directly SELECT * FROM parquet.`Files/landing/catalog/`
This is useful for exploration but not for production analytics. The read path is slower, there is no schema enforcement, and no downstream tool can use it.

sections.title: The Tables Section — Managed Delta Tables

blocks.content: The Tables/ section is where analytics-ready data lives. Every table here is a Delta Lake table — schema-enforced, ACID-transactional, time-travel-capable, and queryable by every Fabric engine. Creating Tables Tables can be created through multiple paths: From PySpark (Most Common)

# Read raw CSV from Files/
raw = spark.read.csv('Files/landing/erp/', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# Transform
from pyspark.sql.functions import col, current_date
clean = raw.filter(col('amount') > 0) \
    .withColumn('load_date', current_date())

# Write as a managed Delta table
clean.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('orders')
From Spark SQL (DDL)

%%sql
CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS orders (
    order_id        STRING,
    customer_id     STRING,
    amount          DECIMAL(12,2),
    order_date      DATE,
    _load_timestamp TIMESTAMP
)
USING DELTA

From a Pipeline Copy Activity
Configure the Copy Activity to write directly to the Tables/ section. Fabric auto-registers the output as a Delta table.
From a Dataflow Gen2
Configure the data destination as a Lakehouse table. Dataflows Gen2 write directly to managed Delta tables with Power Query transformations applied.
Table Registration and Auto-Discovery
Once a table exists in the Tables/ section, Fabric automatically:
•  Registers the table in the Lakehouse’s metastore so it is visible to Spark SQL (spark.sql('SELECT * FROM orders')).
•  Exposes the table through the SQL analytics endpoint so T-SQL clients can query it.
•  Adds the table to the default semantic model so Power BI can report on it.
•  Indexes the table in the OneLake Catalog for discovery.
Inspecting Table Metadata

# Schema
spark.sql('DESCRIBE TABLE orders').show(truncate=False)

# Extended metadata (location, format, properties)
spark.sql('DESCRIBE TABLE EXTENDED orders').show(truncate=False)

# File-level details (size, file count, partitioning)
spark.sql('DESCRIBE DETAIL orders').show()

# Commit history
spark.sql('DESCRIBE HISTORY orders').show()

Table Partitioning Large tables benefit from partitioning — organizing data files into subdirectories by one or more columns. Partitioning enables partition pruning, where the engine skips entire directories that do not match the query filter.

# Write a partitioned table
df.write.format('delta') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .partitionBy('order_date') \
    .saveAsTable('orders_partitioned')

Warning: Over-partitioning is one of the most common mistakes in data engineering. If your partition column has high cardinality (e.g., customer_id with millions of values), you will create millions of tiny files. This destroys performance. Partition by low-cardinality columns (date, region, status) and aim for file sizes between 128 MB and 1 GB after OPTIMIZE. For high-cardinality filtering, use Z-ORDER instead of partitioning.

Every Lakehouse automatically generates a SQL analytics endpoint — a read-only T-SQL interface that exposes all Delta tables in the Tables/ section. This is one of Fabric’s most powerful integration features: it means SQL analysts, BI tools, and any JDBC/ODBC client can query Lakehouse data without Spark. How It Works The SQL analytics endpoint is a separate Fabric item that is created and managed automatically alongside the Lakehouse. When a query hits the endpoint, Fabric’s distributed SQL engine reads the Delta tables directly from OneLake. There is no data copy, no import step, no sync delay. The endpoint reads the same Delta files that Spark writes.

Spark Notebook writes Delta table
          │
          ▼
  OneLake: Tables/orders/ (same Delta files)
          │                     │
          ▼                     ▼
  Spark reads it          SQL Endpoint reads it
  df = spark.table(...)   SELECT * FROM orders
  (PySpark API)           (T-SQL syntax, SSMS/ADS)

What You Can Do
•  SELECT queries with full T-SQL syntax: JOINs, CTEs, window functions, aggregations, subqueries.
•  CREATE VIEW to define reusable SQL abstractions over Lakehouse tables.
•  Table-level and column-level permissions for security.
•  Row-level security through security policies.
•  Cross-database queries: join Lakehouse tables with Warehouse tables using three-part naming (e.g., wh_sales.dbo.dim_customers).
What You Cannot Do
The SQL analytics endpoint is read-only. It does not support:
•  INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, or MERGE — all write operations must go through Spark or Pipelines.
•  CREATE TABLE — tables are created through Spark, Pipelines, or Dataflows.
•  Stored procedures — the SQL analytics endpoint does not support procedural T-SQL.
If you need full DML through T-SQL, use a Fabric Data Warehouse instead. The Warehouse provides a full T-SQL surface with INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, and stored procedures. The decision between Lakehouse and Warehouse often comes down to whether your team needs T-SQL writes.
Connecting External Tools
The SQL analytics endpoint has a standard TDS (Tabular Data Stream) connection string. You can connect from SSMS, Azure Data Studio, DBeaver, DataGrip, Power BI Desktop, Excel, or any JDBC/ODBC client. The connection string is found in the Lakehouse settings or by clicking the SQL analytics endpoint item in the workspace.
SQL Endpoint Example Queries

-- Query a Lakehouse table from the SQL endpoint SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count, SUM(amount) AS total_revenue FROM orders WHERE order_date >= '2026-01-01' GROUP BY customer_id ORDER BY total_revenue DESC

-- Create a view for reusable analytics CREATE VIEW vw_monthly_revenue AS SELECT YEAR(order_date) AS yr, MONTH(order_date) AS mo, SUM(amount) AS revenue FROM orders GROUP BY YEAR(order_date), MONTH(order_date)
-- Cross-database query (join Lakehouse to Warehouse) SELECT l.order_id, l.amount, w.customer_name FROM orders AS l JOIN wh_sales.dbo.dim_customers AS w ON l.customer_id = w.customer_id

Every Lakehouse automatically generates a Power BI semantic model. This model is a DirectLake dataset that reads Delta tables directly from OneLake — no import, no DirectQuery, no scheduled refresh. DirectLake: The Third Mode

Mode Data Location Performance Freshness Import Copied into Power BI model Fastest (in-memory) Stale until next refresh DirectQuery Queried from source on demand Slower (network round-trip) Always current DirectLake Read directly from OneLake Delta files Near-import speed Always current (no copy)

DirectLake combines the performance of Import (data is read into the VertiPaq engine’s columnar format) with the freshness of DirectQuery (no copy to maintain). It works because the data is already in columnar Delta/Parquet format in OneLake — the VertiPaq engine can read it directly. Customizing the Default Model The auto-generated model is a starting point, not a finished product. You can customize it by selecting which tables to include, defining relationships between tables (fact-to-dimension joins), adding DAX measures for calculated business metrics, and setting data types and formatting for better report readability. When the Default Model Falls Short The default semantic model has limitations: it includes all columns from included tables with no pruning, does not support complex DAX until you manually add it, auto-detects relationships that may not match your star schema, and does not support composite models. For production BI, create a dedicated semantic model connected to the Lakehouse via DirectLake, as covered in the BI track. DirectLake Fallback Behavior DirectLake has a fallback mechanism. If a query is too complex for the DirectLake engine (e.g., it requires features not yet supported), Power BI automatically falls back to DirectQuery mode for that specific query. This is transparent to the user, but the query will be slower. Understanding this fallback explains why some reports feel snappy and others feel sluggish on the same dataset — the slow ones may be falling back to DirectQuery. Monitoring fallback events in the Fabric Capacity Metrics app helps you identify and optimize these cases.

sections.title: Lakehouse vs. Warehouse — When to Use Which

blocks.content: This is one of the most common questions in Fabric: should I use a Lakehouse or a Warehouse? The answer depends on your team, your workload, and your data lifecycle. Architectural Comparison Both the Lakehouse and the Warehouse store data as Delta tables in OneLake. The underlying storage is identical. The difference is in the interface and capabilities each provides on top of that storage.

Criterion Lakehouse Data Warehouse Primary Interface Spark (PySpark, Spark SQL, Scala, R) T-SQL SQL Access Read-only SQL analytics endpoint (auto-generated) Full DML: INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE, MERGE, stored procedures Write Path Spark notebooks, Pipelines, Dataflows T-SQL DML, Pipelines, Dataflows, COPY INTO Schema Flexibility Files/ accepts any format; Tables/ are Delta All tables are structured Delta from creation Language Support Python, SQL, Scala, R in notebooks T-SQL only Spark Access Native — Lakehouse is Spark-first Can read from Spark, but not Spark-native Power BI Integration DirectLake via default semantic model DirectLake via default semantic model Stored Procedures Not supported Fully supported Ideal Persona Data engineers, data scientists SQL analysts, analytics engineers, BI developers Partitioning Control Full Spark-level partition control Automatic distribution (round-robin) Cross-DB Queries Three-part naming to Warehouse tables Three-part naming to Lakehouse tables

Decision Framework Choose a Lakehouse when: your team writes PySpark, you need the Files/ landing zone for raw data, you process semi-structured formats (JSON, XML, images), you need Spark-level control over partitioning and optimization, or you are building the medallion architecture’s Bronze and Silver layers. Choose a Warehouse when: your team thinks in T-SQL, you need full DML (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE), you want stored procedures for encapsulated logic, your workload is pure SQL analytics without Spark, or you are building a governed Gold layer for SQL analysts. Use both when: your platform serves both data engineers and SQL analysts. A common pattern: data engineers build Bronze and Silver layers in Lakehouses with PySpark, then the Gold layer is either a Lakehouse (if downstream is Power BI DirectLake) or a Warehouse (if downstream needs T-SQL DML and stored procedures). Important: There is no wrong answer. Both items store Delta tables in OneLake. Both are queryable. Both feed Power BI. The choice is about which interface your team is most productive with. In many organizations, the answer is both — Lakehouses for engineering, Warehouses for SQL analytics.

In production, you will rarely have a single Lakehouse. The medallion architecture pattern — Bronze, Silver, Gold — typically uses separate Lakehouses for each layer. Understanding why and how to organize multiple Lakehouses is critical for building maintainable platforms. Why Separate Lakehouses? Security isolation — Different teams need different access levels. Raw Bronze data contains PII and messy records that most analysts should never see. Silver data is cleansed but still granular. Gold data is modeled for consumption. Separate Lakehouses let you apply different workspace roles and permissions to each layer. Lifecycle independence — Bronze tables are append-heavy with frequent small writes. Gold tables are rewritten less often but must be highly optimized for queries. Different OPTIMIZE and VACUUM schedules make sense for different layers. Governance clarity — Endorsement, sensitivity labels, and OneLake Catalog descriptions are cleaner when each Lakehouse represents a clear data quality tier. Capacity isolation (optional) — In large deployments, Bronze and Gold Lakehouses can be in different workspaces bound to different capacities, preventing heavy ingestion jobs from impacting BI query performance. The Standard Multi-Lakehouse Pattern

Workspace: sales_prod
  │
  ├── lh_bronze_sales          lh_silver_sales          lh_gold_sales
  │   ├── Files/               ├── Tables/              ├── Tables/
  │   │   └── landing/         │   ├── orders           │   ├── fact_orders
  │   └── Tables/              │   ├── customers        │   ├── dim_customers
  │       ├── raw_orders       │   └── products         │   ├── dim_products
  │       ├── raw_customers    │                          │   └── dim_date
  │       └── raw_products     │                          │
  │                              │                          │
  │   ┌── Notebook reads ───┘  ┌── Notebook reads ───┘
  │   │  from Bronze              │  from Silver
  │   │  via shortcut              │  via shortcut
  │   │  or abfss:// path          │  or abfss:// path

Cross-Lakehouse Data Access When a Silver notebook needs to read from the Bronze Lakehouse, it has three options: Shortcut (recommended) — Create a shortcut in lh_silver that points to lh_bronze/Tables/raw_orders. The Silver notebook reads from the shortcut as if it were a local table. Zero copy, immediate access. abfss:// path — Read directly using the full OneLake path. This avoids creating shortcuts but makes notebook code less portable. Attached Lakehouse switching — A Notebook can have multiple Lakehouses attached (one default, others as additional). The default determines the short path resolution; additional Lakehouses are accessible via their names.
Code: Reading from Another Lakehouse

# Option 1: Read via shortcut (if shortcut is created)
bronze_orders = spark.read.format('delta').load('Tables/raw_orders')

# Option 2: Read via abfss:// path
bronze_orders = spark.read.format('delta').load(
    'abfss://sales_prod@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/'
    'lh_bronze_sales.Lakehouse/Tables/raw_orders'
)

# Option 3: Read from additional attached Lakehouse
bronze_orders = spark.sql(
    'SELECT * FROM lh_bronze_sales.raw_orders'
)

Single Lakehouse vs. Multi-Lakehouse For small projects or proof-of-concept work, a single Lakehouse with all layers (using naming prefixes like bronze_orders, silver_orders, gold_fact_orders) is simpler and perfectly adequate. For production platforms serving multiple teams with different security requirements, separate Lakehouses per layer are the standard. There is no single correct answer — the trade-off is simplicity vs. governance granularity.

File Organization
•  Keep the Files/ section organized with a clear folder hierarchy: landing/, staging/, archive/.
•  Use date-based subfolders for incremental data (e.g., landing/erp/2026-03-07/).
•  Never leave raw files in Files/ indefinitely — process them into Tables/ and archive or delete the originals.
•  Avoid deeply nested folder hierarchies. Two to three levels of depth is sufficient.
Table Organization
•  Name tables descriptively: orders, dim_customers, fact_daily_revenue — not table1, temp, or test_final_v2.
•  Use the medallion pattern (Bronze, Silver, Gold) to organize tables by data quality level.
•  Document table purposes in the OneLake Catalog using descriptions and tags.
•  Endorse (Promote or Certify) tables that are ready for downstream consumption.
Table Maintenance
Delta tables require regular maintenance to stay performant:
%%sql -- Weekly maintenance script
-- Step 1: Compact and cluster OPTIMIZE orders ZORDER BY (customer_id); OPTIMIZE customers; OPTIMIZE products;
-- Step 2: Reclaim storage (run AFTER optimize) VACUUM orders; VACUUM customers; VACUUM products;
Naming Conventions

Item Pattern Example Lakehouse lh_{layer}_{domain} lh_bronze_sales, lh_gold_finance Tables (Bronze) raw_{entity} raw_orders, raw_customers Tables (Silver) {entity} (clean name) orders, customers, products Tables (Gold) fact_{metric} or dim_{entity} fact_orders, dim_customers, dim_date Files folders landing/{source}/{date}/ landing/erp/2026-03-07/ Notebooks nb_{layer}_{entity} nb_bronze_orders, nb_silver_customers
Security

items: Apply workspace roles as your first line of defense. Only team members who need access should be in the workspace.
Use OneLake Security (table-level, column-level) for finer-grained control.
For the SQL analytics endpoint, configure T-SQL permissions (GRANT SELECT on specific tables) to restrict analyst access.
For the default semantic model, configure Row-Level Security (RLS) in DAX if reports are shared broadly.
Never give analysts direct access to Bronze Lakehouses — they should consume from Silver or Gold only.

blocks.content: Pattern 1: The Bronze Load Notebook Every production Lakehouse has a Bronze load notebook that follows this template:

# nb_bronze_orders
# Reads raw files from Files/landing/, adds metadata, writes to Bronze Delta table

from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, lit, input_file_name

# Step 1: Read raw files
raw = spark.read.csv(
    'Files/landing/erp/',
    header=True,
    inferSchema=True
)

# Step 2: Add audit metadata
bronze = raw \
    .withColumn('_load_timestamp', current_timestamp()) \
    .withColumn('_source_system', lit('ERP')) \
    .withColumn('_source_file', input_file_name())

# Step 3: Append to Bronze table
bronze.write.format('delta') \
    .mode('append') \
    .saveAsTable('raw_orders')

# Step 4: Verify
print(f'Loaded {bronze.count()} rows')
spark.sql('DESCRIBE DETAIL raw_orders').show()
Pattern 2: Parameterized Notebooks Production notebooks accept parameters so they can be reused across sources and triggered by Pipelines:

# Cell 1: Parameters (Pipeline sets these)
source_path = 'Files/landing/erp/'     # Default value
source_system = 'ERP'                   # Default value
target_table = 'raw_orders'             # Default value

# Cell 2: Logic (same for all sources)
raw = spark.read.csv(source_path, header=True, inferSchema=True)
bronze = raw \
    .withColumn('_load_timestamp', current_timestamp()) \
    .withColumn('_source_system', lit(source_system)) \
    .withColumn('_source_file', input_file_name())
bronze.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable(target_table)

When a Pipeline triggers this notebook, it overrides the default parameter values. One notebook, many sources. Pattern 3: File Archiving After Load After successfully loading files into Delta tables, move them from landing/ to archive/ so they are not re-processed:

import shutil
from notebookutils import mssparkutils

# Move processed files to archive
mssparkutils.fs.mv(
    'Files/landing/erp/orders_2026-03-07.csv',
    'Files/archive/erp/2026-03-07/orders_2026-03-07.csv',
    True  # Create target directory if needed
)
Pattern 4: Lakehouse Maintenance Notebook A dedicated maintenance notebook runs weekly (triggered by a Pipeline) to keep all tables in a Lakehouse healthy:

# nb_maintenance_bronze
# Run weekly after all daily loads complete

tables = spark.sql('SHOW TABLES').collect()

for table in tables:
    name = table.tableName
    print(f'Optimizing {name}...')
    spark.sql(f'OPTIMIZE {name}')
    spark.sql(f'VACUUM {name}')
    detail = spark.sql(f'DESCRIBE DETAIL {name}').collect()[0]
    print(f'  Files: {detail.numFiles}, Size: {detail.sizeInBytes/1e6:.1f} MB')
Module DE1 Checkpoint
You now understand:

items: What a Lakehouse is — the dual-interface item that combines Files/ (raw storage) with Tables/ (managed Delta tables), plus an auto-generated SQL analytics endpoint and default semantic model
How to create a Lakehouse — one-click creation with automatic OneLake storage, SQL endpoint, semantic model, and catalog registration
Every ingestion path — portal upload, File Explorer, Notebooks, Pipelines, Dataflows Gen2, shortcuts, mirroring, and APIs
Loading code patterns — reading CSV, JSON, Parquet, writing Delta tables with metadata columns, append vs. overwrite modes
The Files/ section — the unstructured landing zone for raw data, organized with date-based folder hierarchies
The Tables/ section — managed Delta tables with schema enforcement, created from Notebooks, Pipelines, Dataflows, or SQL DDL
The SQL analytics endpoint — auto-generated read-only T-SQL interface with views, security, and cross-database queries, but no DML
DirectLake and the default semantic model — Power BI reads Delta directly from OneLake with no import, including fallback behavior
Lakehouse vs. Warehouse — choose by team skill (Spark vs. T-SQL) and write requirements, not by performance
Multi-Lakehouse architecture — separate Lakehouses per medallion layer for security isolation, lifecycle independence, and governance clarity
Cross-Lakehouse access — shortcuts, abfss:// paths, and additional attached Lakehouses
Production patterns — Bronze load notebooks, parameterized notebooks, file archiving, maintenance notebooks
Best practices — file organization, table naming, maintenance schedules, security layering

blocks.content: Build a Lakehouse from Scratch
Before moving to Module DE2, complete this end-to-end exercise:

items: Create a Lakehouse named lh_workshop in your workspace.
Download a sample CSV dataset (e.g., from Kaggle or a public dataset) and upload it to Files/landing/.
Create a Spark notebook attached to the Lakehouse. Read the CSV, add _load_timestamp and _source_system metadata columns, and write it as a Delta table in Tables/.
Verify the table appears in the Tables/ section of the Lakehouse explorer.
Switch to the SQL analytics endpoint view. Write a T-SQL query that aggregates the data (COUNT, SUM, GROUP BY). Confirm the table is queryable via SQL.
Open the default semantic model. Create a simple Power BI report with one visual on the Lakehouse data.
Run DESCRIBE DETAIL on your table. Note the file count. Run OPTIMIZE. Run DESCRIBE DETAIL again and observe the compaction.
Append more rows (re-read the CSV with mode ‘append’). Use DESCRIBE HISTORY to see both commits. Use VERSION AS OF to read the original version.
Create a second Lakehouse (lh_workshop_silver). Create a shortcut in it that points to your first Lakehouse’s Delta table. Read the shortcutted table from a Notebook in the second Lakehouse.
Write a maintenance cell that runs OPTIMIZE and VACUUM on all tables in the Lakehouse, then prints the file count and size for each.

blocks.content: You are not just creating a storage container.

You are building the central artifact of Fabric data engineering — the item where raw data transforms into governed, queryable, reportable analytics. Every module from here forward will read from or write to a Lakehouse.

In Module DE2, we turn to Data Factory — Pipelines — the orchestration engine that automates everything you just did manually. You will learn to build production-grade pipelines that ingest data from external sources, trigger Spark notebooks, handle errors, parameterize for reuse, and run on schedule.

chapters.title: DE2 Data Factory Pipelines
chapters.description: Design and orchestrate reliable Fabric Data Factory pipelines.

blocks.content: Module DE2 Data Factory — Pipelines The Orchestration Engine Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Design and orchestrate data movement and transformation pipelines using Fabric Data Factory. Master activities, triggers, parameters, error handling, monitoring, and production patterns for automated ETL/ELT in energy data platforms.
Welcome to Module DE2

In the previous modules you learned the platform (F1), the storage layer (F2), and the Lakehouse (DE1). You can now create a Lakehouse, land data in Files/, transform it in a Notebook, and write Delta tables to Tables/. But so far, everything you have done is manual. You opened a notebook, clicked Run All, and watched it execute. That is fine for development. It is not how production energy data platforms work. Production platforms are automated. Meter readings arrive every 15 minutes. SCADA telemetry flows hourly. Weather forecasts update four times a day. Energy pricing data publishes at market close. Notebooks execute without human intervention. Failures trigger retries and alerts. Parameters change based on the date, the source system, or the grid region. One pipeline orchestrates dozens of steps in the correct order, with dependencies, branching, and error handling. That is what Data Factory Pipelines do. A pipeline is a workflow graph — a directed sequence of activities that execute in order, with control flow logic, parameterization, and monitoring. If the Lakehouse is where energy data lives, the pipeline is how data gets there and how it stays fresh. That sentence is the single most important insight in this module. This module takes you from understanding pipelines conceptually to building production-grade orchestration: the anatomy of a pipeline, the Copy Activity in depth, parameterization with dynamic content, orchestrating Notebooks and Spark jobs, scheduling with triggers, error handling and retry logic, monitoring and debugging, master pipeline patterns, and the Apache Airflow alternative. Every example uses energy industry data — smart meters, turbines, substations, pricing feeds, and weather forecasts — so the patterns are grounded in a real domain.

sections.title: Data Factory in Fabric vs. Azure Data Factory

blocks.content: If you have used Azure Data Factory (ADF), Fabric’s Data Factory will feel familiar. The concepts are the same: pipelines, activities, triggers, expressions. But there are important differences. What Migrated The pipeline canvas — the visual designer where you drag activities and connect them — is functionally identical. The activity library — Copy Activity, Notebook Activity, ForEach, If Condition, Until, Switch, Set Variable, Web Activity, Stored Procedure, Fail — carries over with minor naming differences. The expression language — @pipeline().RunId, @activity('name').output, dynamic content with string interpolation — is the same. Parameterization — pipeline parameters, system variables, global parameters — works the same way. What Changed No linked services or datasets. In ADF, you configured linked services (connection credentials) and datasets (data shapes) as separate objects. In Fabric, connections are managed centrally, and pipelines reference Fabric items (Lakehouses, Warehouses) directly. This eliminates a major source of configuration complexity. No integration runtime to manage. ADF required you to provision Self-Hosted or Azure Integration Runtimes. In Fabric, compute is drawn from the capacity pool. Pipelines run on managed infrastructure. OneLake-native. Fabric pipelines read from and write to OneLake directly. The Copy Activity can target a Lakehouse’s Files/ or Tables/ section without configuring a storage connection. Capacity-based billing. ADF billed per activity run and per DIU-hour. Fabric pipelines consume CUs from your capacity. Simpler cost tracking, but requires capacity planning. What’s New Apache Airflow Job — Run Airflow DAGs natively inside Fabric for teams that prefer code-defined orchestration. Dataflows Gen2 — Power Query-based low-code transformations that can be orchestrated from pipelines (covered in Module DE3). Tighter Notebook integration — Pass parameters to Fabric Notebooks and capture return values as pipeline outputs, all within the Fabric security boundary.

sections.title: Pipeline Anatomy — Activities, Links, and Control Flow

blocks.content: A pipeline is a directed graph of activities. Each activity performs a unit of work: copying meter readings from an SFTP server, running a Spark notebook to cleanse turbine telemetry, executing a stored procedure to refresh a grid summary, or calling a weather API. Activities are connected by dependency links that define execution order and conditional behavior. Activity Categories

Category Key Activities Purpose Data Movement Copy Activity Move data between sources and destinations. The workhorse of ingestion — copies meter CSVs, SCADA Parquet files, pricing feeds. Transformation Notebook, Dataflow, Stored Procedure, Spark Job Definition Execute transformation logic: PySpark notebooks for cleansing readings, Power Query dataflows for reference data, T-SQL procedures for warehouse aggregations. Control Flow ForEach, If Condition, Until, Switch, Wait, Execute Pipeline Branching, looping, sub-pipeline invocation. Process each grid region in parallel, skip weekends for pricing data, retry until a file appears. Utility Set Variable, Append Variable, Lookup, Get Metadata, Web Activity, Fail Read metadata, call weather APIs, set variables for logging, force a pipeline failure with a descriptive message.
Dependency Links and Conditions

Condition Symbol When It Fires Succeeded Green check The upstream activity completed successfully. Failed Red X The upstream activity failed (threw an error). Completed Blue circle The upstream activity finished, regardless of success or failure. Skipped Gray arrow The upstream activity was skipped (e.g., an If Condition branch not taken).
The Activity Graph — Energy Platform Example Here is a typical pipeline for a daily smart grid ingestion workflow:

pl_daily_grid_refresh
  │
  ├── Copy Activity: Ingest Meter Readings (SFTP → Files/landing/meters/)
  │   │
  │   ├── (Succeeded) ──▶ Notebook: nb_bronze_meters (Files/ → Tables/)
  │   │                    │
  │   │                    ├── (Succeeded) ──▶ Notebook: nb_silver_meters
  │   │                    │                    │
  │   │                    │                    └── (Succeeded) ──▶ Set Variable: log_success
  │   │                    │
  │   │                    └── (Failed) ──▶ Web Activity: alert_ops ─▶ Fail
  │   │
  │   └── (Failed) ──▶ Web Activity: alert_ops ─▶ Fail Activity
  │
  ├── Copy Activity: Ingest Weather Forecast (API → Files/landing/weather/)
  │   └── (Succeeded) ──▶ Notebook: nb_bronze_weather
  │
  └── Copy Activity: Ingest Energy Prices (SFTP → Files/landing/pricing/)
      └── (Succeeded) ──▶ Notebook: nb_bronze_pricing

This pattern — ingest from multiple sources in parallel, transform through Bronze/Silver layers sequentially, alert on failure — is the foundation of every energy data pipeline. The three Copy Activities run in parallel because they have no dependency links between them. The Notebooks run sequentially within each branch because each depends on the previous step’s output.

The Copy Activity is the most-used activity in Data Factory. It moves data from a source to a destination (called a “sink” in ADF terminology). In Fabric energy platforms, the most common patterns are copying meter readings from SFTP, pulling weather data from REST APIs, and loading pricing feeds from Azure SQL databases. Source and Sink Configuration Source — Select from 300+ built-in connectors: SFTP for meter file drops, REST APIs for weather services, Azure SQL Database for SCADA historians, Oracle for energy trading systems, SharePoint for regulatory filings, S3 for cross-cloud partner data. Each connector has specific options (query for SQL sources, file path for storage sources, HTTP method for REST). Sink (Destination) — Select a Fabric item — typically a Lakehouse (Files/ or Tables/ section), a Warehouse table, or a KQL Database. For Lakehouse targets, you choose whether to land in Files/ (raw file copy) or Tables/ (auto-converted to Delta). Copy to Files/ vs. Copy to Tables/

Destination What Happens Energy Use Case Lakehouse Files/ Raw file is copied as-is into the specified folder. No schema enforcement, no Delta conversion. Landing daily meter CSVs, weather JSON payloads, SCADA Parquet exports. Preserves the original file for audit. Lakehouse Tables/ Data is converted to Delta format and registered as a managed table. Schema is inferred or specified. Clean, structured reference data (substation registry, tariff codes) that needs no transformation.

Important: Copying directly to Tables/ is convenient but limited. You cannot apply complex transformations, handle deduplication, or implement merge logic in a Copy Activity. For production ETL with business rules — such as deduplicating meter readings by meter_id and timestamp, or validating that energy prices are positive — land in Files/ and transform with a Notebook. Energy Platform: Copy Activity Examples
Example 1: SFTP → Lakehouse (Meter Readings)
Source: Type: SFTP Host: sftp.utility-meters.example.com Path: /exports/readings/@{formatDateTime( pipeline().parameters.load_date, 'yyyy-MM-dd')}/ File format: CSV (header: true)
Sink: Type: Lakehouse Files Lakehouse: lh_bronze_grid Path: Files/landing/meters/@{formatDateTime( pipeline().parameters.load_date, 'yyyy/MM/dd')}/ File format: CSV (preserve as-is)
Example 2: REST API → Lakehouse (Weather Forecast)

Source: Type: REST URL: https://api.weather.example.com/v2/forecast Method: GET Query: region=@{pipeline().parameters.grid_region} &date=@{pipeline().parameters.load_date} Authentication: API Key (stored in Fabric connection)
Sink: Type: Lakehouse Files Path: Files/landing/weather/@{pipeline().parameters.load_date}.json
Example 3: Azure SQL → Lakehouse (Energy Prices)
Source: Type: Azure SQL Database Query: SELECT price_date, hour_block, region, price_mwh, currency, market_type FROM dbo.hourly_prices WHERE price_date = '@{pipeline().parameters.load_date}'
Sink: Type: Lakehouse Files Path: Files/landing/pricing/@{pipeline().parameters.load_date}.parquet File format: Parquet (Snappy compression)

Performance Tuning Parallelism (DIUs) — The number of parallel threads. Higher = faster for large datasets. A daily meter file with 50 million readings benefits from 16+ DIUs. A 1 MB pricing feed is fine with Auto (4 DIUs). Source partitioning — For SQL sources, enable parallel reads by partitioning the query (e.g., by region or hour_block). This reads multiple chunks simultaneously instead of a single sequential scan. File format and compression — Parquet with Snappy compression is optimal for OneLake ingestion. When you control the source format, always request Parquet. When you receive CSV, land it as-is and convert in the Bronze notebook.

Hard-coded pipelines are brittle. A pipeline that copies from one specific meter region on one specific date breaks the moment your requirements change. Parameterization makes pipelines flexible, reusable, and maintainable. Pipeline Parameters Parameters are values that you define at the pipeline level and pass in at runtime. They make a single pipeline serve multiple purposes.
Energy Platform Parameters

// Reference in a Copy Activity source query: SELECT * FROM @{pipeline().parameters.source_system}_export WHERE export_date = '@{pipeline().parameters.load_date}' AND region = '@{pipeline().parameters.grid_region}'
// Reference in a sink file path: Files/landing/@{pipeline().parameters.source_system}/ @{formatDateTime(pipeline().parameters.load_date, 'yyyy/MM/dd')}/

You can trigger this pipeline with different parameter values each time — today for meters in the NORTH region, tomorrow for SOUTH, next week for SCADA telemetry — using the same pipeline definition. System Variables

Variable Returns Energy Use Case @pipeline().RunId Unique ID for this pipeline run Audit logging: correlate every meter reading to the pipeline run that loaded it @pipeline().PipelineName Name of the current pipeline Dynamic log messages: 'pl_ingest_meters completed for 2026-03-07' @pipeline().TriggerTime Timestamp when the trigger fired Date-based file paths, partition keys for landing folders @utcnow() Current UTC timestamp Timestamping output files, _load_timestamp metadata in Bronze tables @formatDateTime(utcnow(),'yyyy-MM-dd') Formatted date string Building date-partitioned file paths: landing/meters/2026/03/07/
Dynamic Content Expressions Dynamic content uses the @{...} syntax inside activity settings and supports string interpolation, functions, and conditional logic:
// Dynamic file path based on date and source Files/landing/@{pipeline().parameters.source_system}/ @{formatDateTime(pipeline().parameters.load_date, 'yyyy/MM/dd')}/
// Conditional: choose Lakehouse based on environment @if(equals(pipeline().parameters.environment, 'prod'), 'lh_bronze_grid', 'lh_dev_grid' )

// Concatenation for log messages @concat('Loaded ', activity('CopyMeters').output.rowsCopied, ' meter readings for region ', pipeline().parameters.grid_region, ' on ', pipeline().parameters.load_date)

Dynamic content turns a static pipeline into a template. One pipeline definition can handle meters, SCADA, pricing, and weather data across all grid regions through parameterization alone. This is the foundation of scalable orchestration.

The Notebook Activity is how pipelines trigger Spark-based transformations. It is the bridge between orchestration (Data Factory) and computation (Spark in Notebooks). In energy platforms, notebooks do the heavy lifting: cleansing 50 million meter readings, validating SCADA telemetry ranges, deduplicating overlapping weather forecasts. Passing Parameters to Notebooks Pipeline parameters flow into notebook parameters, making the same notebook reusable for different dates, regions, and source systems:

# In the pipeline: define parameters
#   load_date = '2026-03-07'
#   source_system = 'meters'
#   grid_region = 'NORTH'
# Pass them to the Notebook Activity's Base Parameters section

# In the notebook (nb_bronze_meters): receive parameters
load_date = notebookutils.notebook.getParam('load_date', '2026-01-01')
source_system = notebookutils.notebook.getParam('source_system', 'meters')
grid_region = notebookutils.notebook.getParam('grid_region', 'ALL')
print(f'Processing {source_system} for {grid_region} on {load_date}')

# Use parameters in Spark logic
raw = spark.read.csv(
    f'Files/landing/{source_system}/{load_date.replace("-", "/")}/',
    header=True, inferSchema=True
)

# Add audit metadata
from pyspark.sql.functions import current_timestamp, lit
bronze = raw \
    .withColumn('_load_timestamp', current_timestamp()) \
    .withColumn('_source_system', lit(source_system)) \
    .withColumn('_grid_region', lit(grid_region))
bronze.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('raw_meter_readings')

The second argument to getParam() is the default value, used when the notebook runs interactively (outside a pipeline). This lets you develop and test with sample data, then orchestrate with production parameters. Capturing Return Values Notebooks can return values to the pipeline using notebookutils.notebook.exit(). This is essential for logging, conditional branching, and data quality gates:

# At the end of nb_bronze_meters
row_count = bronze.count()

# Return row count to pipeline
notebookutils.notebook.exit(str(row_count))

# In the pipeline, reference the output:
# @activity('BronzeMeters').output.result.exitValue
# Returns: '4283716' (the row count as a string)

# Use in an If Condition:
# @greater(int(activity('BronzeMeters').output.result.exitValue), 0)
# True = proceed to Silver, False = skip (no data to process)

Return values enable data quality gates in your pipeline. If the Bronze notebook loaded zero rows (the meter file was empty or corrupt), the pipeline skips the Silver transformation instead of running it on empty data. If the row count is suspiciously low (less than 80% of the expected daily volume), the pipeline can alert the operations team. Spark Job Definitions For production Spark workloads that do not need the notebook UI, Fabric supports Spark Job Definitions — pre-packaged PySpark or Scala JAR files that run as batch jobs. Choose Notebook Activities for evolving transformation logic; choose Spark Job Definitions for stable, tested code that runs the same way every time.
A pipeline without a trigger is a workflow that only runs when you click “Run.” Triggers automate execution based on time, events, or tumbling windows. Trigger Types

Trigger Type How It Works Energy Use Case Scheduled Runs on a CRON-like schedule: daily, hourly, every 15 minutes, specific days. Daily meter load at 02:00 AM. Hourly SCADA refresh. Weekday-only pricing feed at market close (16:30). Tumbling Window Runs on fixed, non-overlapping time windows. Each window is independent. Supports backfill. Process one day of meter data per window. If a day was missed (holiday, system outage), the trigger auto-creates backfill runs for the missed windows. Event-Based Fires when a file arrives in a storage location (OneLake, ADLS Gen2, Blob Storage). Process each meter file as soon as the utility drops it on the SFTP-to-ADLS bridge. No waiting for the next scheduled run.
Scheduled Triggers — Energy Platform Examples
// Daily meter ingestion: every day at 02:00 AM UTC // Recurrence: 1 day // Start: 2026-01-01T02:00:00Z // Time zone: UTC
// Hourly SCADA refresh: every hour on the hour // Recurrence: 1 hour // Start: 2026-01-01T00:00:00Z
// Weekday pricing at market close: Monday-Friday at 16:30 CET // CRON: 30 16 * * 1-5 // Time zone: Central European Time
// Weather forecast: 4x daily at 00:00, 06:00, 12:00, 18:00 UTC // CRON: 0 0,6,12,18 * * *

Tumbling Window Triggers Tumbling windows are ideal for energy data because meter readings, SCADA telemetry, and pricing data are inherently time-partitioned. Each window represents exactly one day (or one hour), and the trigger creates one pipeline run per window. Backfill support — If a window was missed (the pipeline was down during a weekend maintenance window), the trigger automatically creates runs for the missed days when it resumes. This is critical for energy platforms where every day’s data must be accounted for — regulators require complete meter history. Dependency support — A tumbling window trigger can depend on another tumbling window trigger, ensuring that upstream processing (Bronze load) completes before downstream (Silver cleansing) starts for the same window. Individual retry — Each window can be individually retried without affecting other windows. If March 7 failed but March 6 and 8 succeeded, you retry only the March 7 window. Event-Based Triggers Event-based triggers fire when a file appears in a monitored storage location. For energy platforms, this enables real-time processing: as soon as a utility partner drops a meter file on ADLS Gen2, the pipeline ingests and transforms it within minutes, rather than waiting for the next 02:00 AM scheduled run. Important: Event-based triggers require that the storage location supports event notifications. OneLake and ADLS Gen2 support this natively. For SFTP sources, you need an intermediate bridge (Azure Logic App or Function) that copies the file to ADLS Gen2 and fires the event.

Every production pipeline must answer the question: what happens when something goes wrong? In energy platforms, failures have consequences — a missing day of meter readings means incorrect billing calculations, a failed SCADA load means blind spots in grid monitoring. Fabric pipelines provide multiple mechanisms for handling errors gracefully. The Error Path Pattern The most important pattern uses the failure dependency condition to create an explicit error path:

Copy Activity (Ingest meter readings from SFTP)
  │
  ├── (Succeeded) ─▶ Notebook (nb_bronze_meters)
  │                  │
  │                  ├── (Succeeded) ─▶ ✔ Continue to Silver
  │                  │
  │                  └── (Failed) ─▶ Web: POST to ops-alerts channel
  │                                    │
  │                                    └─▶ Fail Activity: 'Bronze load failed
  │                                         for meters on {load_date}'
  │
  └── (Failed) ─▶ Web: POST to ops-alerts channel
                       │
                       └─▶ Fail Activity: 'SFTP copy failed
                            for meters on {load_date}'

Retry Policies
Retry count — How many times to retry on failure (default: 0, max: 3). Each retry re-executes the activity from scratch. Set to 2 for Copy Activities (handles transient SFTP/network failures) and 1 for Notebook Activities (retrying a long Spark job is expensive).
Retry interval — Seconds between retries. Use at least 30 seconds for transient failures. For SFTP sources that rate-limit, use 60–120 seconds.
Retries are appropriate for transient failures — network blips, temporary SFTP throttling, momentary capacity pressure. They are not appropriate for persistent failures — schema mismatches, missing permissions, or logic errors in notebooks.
Timeout Settings
Set realistic timeouts based on expected execution time:
•  A Copy Activity that normally copies 2 GB of meter data in 5 minutes: set timeout to 30 minutes. A 12-hour default means a hung SFTP connection burns CUs for 12 hours before the pipeline notices.
•  A Notebook that cleanses 50 million meter readings in 20 minutes: set timeout to 90 minutes. Generous enough for heavy days, tight enough to catch runaway Spark jobs.
The Fail Activity
The Fail Activity explicitly marks a pipeline run as “Failed” with a custom error message and error code. Without it, a pipeline that catches errors and handles them gracefully may report as “Succeeded” even though the underlying data load failed. This creates false positives in monitoring — the grid operations team thinks all meter data is loaded when it is not.

// Fail Activity configuration Error message: @concat( 'Pipeline ', pipeline().PipelineName, ' failed for ', pipeline().parameters.source_system, ' region ', pipeline().parameters.grid_region, ' on ', pipeline().parameters.load_date, '. Error: ', activity('CopyMeters').error.message ) Error code: GRID_INGESTION_FAILED

Warning: Always terminate error paths with an explicit Fail Activity. Without it, monitoring dashboards show the pipeline as “Succeeded” because the error-handling activities themselves succeeded. In energy platforms, silent failures lead to incomplete billing data and regulatory risk.

The Monitoring Hub The Monitoring Hub (left navigation rail) shows all pipeline runs across all workspaces. For each run: pipeline name, status, duration, trigger type, start/end time, and drill-down into individual activities. Activity-Level Drill-Down Clicking into a run shows the activity graph with real-time status. For each activity: input/output JSON, error details (full message and stack trace for failures), duration, and data volume (rows read/written for Copy Activities). The Gantt Chart View The Gantt chart shows activity execution as horizontal bars on a timeline. Essential for identifying: which activities ran in parallel (the three source ingestions should overlap), bottlenecks (is the SCADA copy taking 80% of the time?), and queue delays (long gaps between activities indicate capacity throttling). Common Debugging Scenarios

Symptom Likely Cause Diagnostic Step Copy fails with timeout SFTP server slow, network issue, or DIU too low Check Copy duration history. Increase DIUs. Verify SFTP server availability. Check firewall rules. Notebook fails Code error (schema mismatch, null pointer, library missing) Click Notebook Activity output. Find the Spark exception. Open notebook and debug interactively with the same parameters. Pipeline succeeds but data is wrong Notebook logic produces incorrect results. No quality check. Add validation: count rows, check min/max of kWh readings, verify date ranges. Use the notebook exit value for row count gates. Pipeline is slow overall Queue delays between activities. Capacity throttling under load. Check Gantt chart for gaps. Monitor the Capacity Metrics app for throttling. Consider Autoscale Spark billing. Trigger does not fire Trigger stopped, time zone mismatch, or event source misconfigured. Verify trigger status (started vs. stopped). Check time zone. For event triggers, verify the storage event subscription.

sections.title: Master Pipeline Patterns for Energy Platforms

blocks.content: As your platform grows beyond a single source, you need architectural patterns for organizing pipelines. Energy platforms typically have 5–15 source systems (meters, SCADA, weather, pricing, asset registry, outage reports, generation forecasts). Managing each as an independent pipeline quickly becomes unmanageable. Pattern 1: Master Pipeline with Execute Pipeline A master pipeline (pl_daily_grid_refresh) calls child pipelines for each source system using the Execute Pipeline activity:

pl_daily_grid_refresh (master)
  │
  ├── Execute Pipeline: pl_ingest_meters (parallel)
  ├── Execute Pipeline: pl_ingest_scada  (parallel)
  ├── Execute Pipeline: pl_ingest_weather (parallel)
  ├── Execute Pipeline: pl_ingest_pricing (parallel — weekdays only)
  │
  │   [all 4 complete]
  │
  ├── Execute Pipeline: pl_transform_silver (sequential)
  │
  ├── Execute Pipeline: pl_transform_gold (sequential)
  │
  └── Notebook: nb_maintenance (OPTIMIZE, VACUUM all tables)

The four ingestion pipelines run in parallel (no dependencies between them). Silver transformation starts only after all ingestions complete. Gold starts after Silver. Maintenance runs last. This pattern gives you: clear separation of concerns (each child pipeline is testable independently), parallel ingestion (faster overall execution), and a single monitoring entry point (the master pipeline status tells you whether the entire daily refresh succeeded). Pattern 2: ForEach with Parameterized Child Pipeline When you have many similar sources (e.g., 12 grid regions, each with the same ingestion logic), use ForEach to iterate over a list and call the same child pipeline with different parameters:
// Master pipeline: pl_ingest_all_regions

This pattern scales to any number of regions without modifying the master pipeline. Adding a new region means adding a row to the lookup table, not editing pipeline logic. Pattern 3: If Condition for Weekday/Weekend Logic Energy pricing data only publishes on trading days (Monday–Friday). The pipeline should skip the pricing ingestion on weekends:

// If Condition expression: @and( greaterOrEquals(dayOfWeek(pipeline().parameters.load_date), 1), lessOrEquals(dayOfWeek(pipeline().parameters.load_date), 5) ) // True (weekday): Execute pl_ingest_pricing // False (weekend): Skip (do nothing)
Pattern 4: Data Quality Gate After the Bronze load, check that the data volume is within expected bounds before proceeding to Silver:

This prevents silent data quality failures. A meter file with only 500 rows (instead of the expected 4 million) passes through Bronze but triggers an alert before Silver processing wastes compute on bad data.

Fabric includes native support for Apache Airflow — the open-source workflow orchestration platform. If your energy trading team already uses Airflow for scheduling market data pipelines, you can run existing DAGs inside Fabric without migration.
