Criterion Native Pipelines Apache Airflow Interface Visual drag-and-drop canvas Python code (DAG definitions) Learning curve Low — familiar to ADF users Higher — requires Python and Airflow knowledge Version control Git integration via Fabric workspace Git-native (DAGs are Python code) Testing Limited — manual pipeline testing Full Python unit testing frameworks Ecosystem Fabric-native connectors 2000+ Airflow operators and community plugins Best for Teams new to orchestration, visual workflow design Teams with existing Airflow expertise, complex DAGs

Some organizations use both: native pipelines for simple Copy Activity workflows and Airflow for complex multi-step orchestration. Both run within the same Fabric workspace and reference the same Lakehouses and Notebooks.
Module DE2 Checkpoint
You now understand:

items: Data Factory in Fabric vs. ADF — what migrated, what changed (no linked services, no integration runtimes, OneLake-native), and what’s new (Airflow, Dataflows Gen2)
Pipeline anatomy — activities (data movement, transformation, control flow, utility), dependency links (succeeded, failed, completed, skipped), and the activity graph
Copy Activity — source/sink configuration for SFTP, REST APIs, and SQL databases; column mapping; performance tuning (DIUs, parallelism); Files/ vs. Tables/ destinations
Parameterization — pipeline parameters (load_date, source_system, grid_region), system variables, and dynamic content expressions for flexible, reusable pipelines
Notebook orchestration — passing parameters with getParam(), capturing return values with exit(), and using return values for data quality gates
Triggers — scheduled (CRON for daily meter loads), tumbling window (backfill for missed days), and event-based (file arrival from SFTP bridge)
Error handling — failure dependency paths, retry policies, timeout settings, the Fail Activity for explicit failure marking, and alert integration
Monitoring — the Monitoring Hub, activity-level drill-down, Gantt chart, and common debugging scenarios
Master pipeline patterns — Execute Pipeline for source decomposition, ForEach for regional parallelism, If Condition for weekday/weekend logic, data quality gates
Apache Airflow — native Airflow support, when to choose Airflow vs. native pipelines, and the hybrid approach

blocks.content: Build an Energy Data Ingestion Pipeline
Before moving to Module DE3, build this complete pipeline:

items: Create a pipeline named pl_ingest_meters with parameters: load_date (String), grid_region (String), and target_table (String, default: ‘raw_meter_readings’).

Add a Copy Activity that reads from a sample data source (use a public CSV URL or upload a meter readings file to ADLS Gen2) and writes to your Lakehouse’s Files/landing/meters/{load_date}/ path. Use dynamic content to build the path from parameters.

Add a Notebook Activity (nb_bronze_meters) that depends on the Copy Activity (Succeeded). Pass load_date and grid_region as parameters. The notebook reads the CSV, adds _load_timestamp, _source_system (‘METERS’), and _grid_region metadata columns, and appends to a Delta table. Return the row count via exit().

Add an If Condition after the Notebook that checks whether the row count exceeds 1000 (your minimum expected volume). True path: Set Variable to log success. False path: Web Activity to alert (or just log a warning), then Fail Activity.
Add an error path: if the Copy Activity fails, add a Fail Activity with a descriptive error message using dynamic content.
Create a scheduled trigger that runs the pipeline daily at 02:00 AM UTC.
Run the pipeline manually with sample parameters. Monitor it in the Monitoring Hub. Drill into each activity. Check the Gantt chart.
Intentionally break something (invalid source URL, wrong file path) and verify that the error path fires correctly and the pipeline shows as Failed.
Create a master pipeline (pl_daily_grid_refresh) that calls pl_ingest_meters via Execute Pipeline. Add a second Execute Pipeline for a weather source. Verify both run in parallel.

blocks.content: You are not just moving data.

You are building automated, self-healing orchestration that keeps an energy data platform fresh, reliable, and auditable — processing millions of meter readings, thousands of weather data points, and hundreds of pricing records every day without human intervention. When something fails, the platform tells you. When a day is missed, it catches up automatically. When a new grid region comes online, you add a row to a lookup table, not a new pipeline.

In Module DE3, we cover Data Factory — Dataflows Gen2 — the low-code, Power Query-based transformation layer that complements pipelines for analyst-friendly data preparation. You will learn when to choose a Dataflow over a Notebook, how to build M-language transformations, and how to orchestrate Dataflows from the pipelines you just mastered.

chapters.title: DE3 Dataflows Gen2
chapters.description: Use Dataflows Gen2 for low-code transformation and reusable ingestion patterns.

blocks.content: Module DE3 Data Factory — Dataflows Gen2 Low-Code Transformation at Scale Estimated Time: 90 Minutes Module Objective: Build data transformations with Dataflows Gen2 using the Power Query Online editor. Master connectors, transformation steps, the M language, data destinations, staging, incremental refresh, and how Dataflows Gen2 complement Notebooks and Pipelines for energy reference data preparation.
Welcome to Module DE3

Module DE2 taught you to orchestrate with Pipelines — the automation layer. This module introduces the second half of Data Factory: Dataflows Gen2, the transformation layer for people who think in spreadsheets rather than code. Not every data professional on an energy platform writes PySpark. Grid operations analysts manage substation registries in Excel. Tariff planners maintain rate schedules in SharePoint. Asset managers track turbine metadata in Dynamics 365. These professionals are fluent in Power Query — the same transformation engine used in Power BI Desktop and Excel. Dataflows Gen2 brings that engine to Fabric, running at cloud scale and writing directly to Lakehouses and Warehouses. Think of it this way: a Notebook is a code-first transformation tool (Modules DE4–DE6 go deep). A Dataflow Gen2 is a visual-first transformation tool that uses the Power Query editor to connect to data, apply transformation steps, and write the result to a Fabric destination — all without writing a single line of PySpark. That does not mean Dataflows Gen2 are limited. They support 300+ data connectors, hundreds of built-in transformations, a full scripting language (M), staging through a Spark compute engine, and direct output to Lakehouse tables. They are the right choice for reference data, dimension tables, and analyst-owned datasets in your energy platform. This module covers: what Dataflows Gen2 are and how they differ from Gen1, the Power Query Online editor, connectors and query folding, visual transformations applied to energy reference data, the M language, data destinations, staging and performance, incremental refresh for growing datasets, when to use Dataflows Gen2 vs. Notebooks, orchestration from Pipelines, and a hands-on checkpoint.

A Dataflow Gen2 is a Fabric item that encapsulates a Power Query transformation with a data destination. You define: where to read data from (the source), how to transform it (the Power Query steps), and where to write the result (the destination). When the dataflow runs, it connects to the source, applies every step in order, and writes the output. Gen1 vs. Gen2

Feature Dataflows Gen1 (Power BI) Dataflows Gen2 (Fabric) Execution engine Power Query mashup engine only Power Query + Spark staging engine Output destination Power BI dataset (import) or Azure Data Lake Lakehouse, Warehouse, KQL Database, or Azure targets Performance at scale Limited — single-node mashup engine High — Spark engine for large datasets when staging enabled Scheduling Power BI refresh schedule Pipeline orchestration or standalone schedule M language support Full Full Incremental refresh Basic Advanced, with configurable watermark columns Environment Power BI Service Fabric workspace (any workload)

The critical upgrade is the Spark staging engine. When staging is enabled, Dataflows Gen2 offload heavy transformations to Fabric’s Spark cluster instead of running everything on the single-node mashup engine. This dramatically improves performance for datasets with millions of rows — such as a full year of hourly energy pricing across all grid regions. Where Dataflows Gen2 Sit in the Architecture

Energy Data Sources (Substation DB, Tariff SharePoint, Asset Dynamics 365, Weather API) │ ▼ Dataflow Gen2 (Power Query Online) │ ── Connect to source (300+ connectors) │ ── Apply transformation steps │ ── Write to destination │ ▼ Lakehouse Tables/ | Warehouse | KQL Database │ ▼ SQL Analytics Endpoint | Power BI | Notebooks

Dataflows Gen2 are a transformation layer between data sources and Fabric storage. They complement Pipelines (orchestration and data movement) and Notebooks (code-based transformation). Many production energy platforms use all three: Pipelines for scheduling, Dataflows Gen2 for analyst-managed reference data, and Notebooks for complex Spark-based meter and SCADA processing.

The Power Query Online editor is the visual interface where you build Dataflow Gen2 transformations. If you have used Power Query in Power BI Desktop or Excel, this editor will feel immediately familiar — it is the same experience, running in the browser.
Editor Layout
Queries pane (left) — Lists all queries (data sources) in the dataflow. Each query is an independent transformation pipeline. A single dataflow might have queries for substations, tariffs, and meter metadata side by side.
Diagram view (top center) — Shows a visual flow of queries and their dependencies. If the tariff query joins to a region lookup query, the diagram shows the relationship.
Data preview (center) — Shows a live preview of the data at the current transformation step. Each time you add a step, the preview updates. This immediate feedback loop is what makes Power Query so productive for analysts.
Applied steps pane (right) — Lists every transformation step in order for the selected query. Steps execute top to bottom. Click any step to see the data at that point. Reorder, rename, or delete steps as needed.
The Query Building Workflow
•  Connect: Choose a data source from the connector library. Provide credentials and select the table, file, or API endpoint.
•  Preview: The editor loads a preview. Inspect columns, data types, and sample values.
•  Transform: Apply transformation steps using the ribbon, right-click menus, or the formula bar.
•  Validate: Check the data preview at each step to confirm correctness.
•  Set destination: Configure where the output should land (Lakehouse table, Warehouse table, etc.).
•  Publish: Save the dataflow. It is now ready to run manually or be scheduled.
This workflow is visual and iterative. You see the data at every step, which makes debugging straightforward — if a transformation produces unexpected results, click the previous step and inspect the before/after.
The Formula Bar and M Preview
Every transformation step generates a line of M code behind the scenes. The formula bar at the top of the data preview shows the M expression for the currently selected step. You can edit it directly for fine-grained control. For the full M script, click “Advanced Editor” in the toolbar.

sections.title: Connectors, Data Sources, and Query Folding

blocks.content: Connector Categories

Category Examples Energy Use Case Databases SQL Server, Azure SQL, PostgreSQL, Oracle, Snowflake Substation registry from operational SQL database. SCADA historian data. Energy trading system (Oracle). Files CSV, Excel, JSON, XML, Parquet, PDF Tariff schedules in Excel. Regulatory filings in CSV. Weather station metadata in JSON. Cloud Services SharePoint Lists, Dynamics 365, Salesforce, SAP Asset management in Dynamics 365. Maintenance schedules in SharePoint. Customer accounts in SAP. Web and APIs REST/OData, Blank Query (custom M) Weather forecast API. Grid operator REST endpoints. Renewable generation feeds. Fabric Items Lakehouse, Warehouse, KQL Database, Power BI Dataset Cross-Lakehouse joins. Reading from Bronze tables to create enriched reference data. Azure Services Blob, ADLS Gen2, Cosmos DB, Azure Data Explorer Partner data in Blob Storage. IoT device registry in Cosmos DB.

Query Folding — The Most Important Performance Concept Query folding is when a transformation is pushed down to the source and executed on the source server. When a Dataflow Gen2 connects to the substation registry in Azure SQL and you apply a Filter Rows step, Power Query translates the filter into a SQL WHERE clause. The database server applies the filter, and only matching rows transfer to the dataflow. This can reduce data transfer by orders of magnitude.
Steps that fold (execute on the source): • Filter rows, remove columns, rename columns, change types, sort, group by, merge (join), append.
Steps that typically do NOT fold: • Pivot, unpivot, custom M functions, most text transformations, adding index columns.

Tip: Check folding status by right-clicking a step and selecting “View Native Query.” If the option shows a SQL query, the step folds. If grayed out, the step does not fold — and all subsequent steps also do not fold. Structure your transformations so that foldable steps come first.
Energy Example: Query Folding in Action
// Source: Azure SQL Database — substation registry // 500,000 substations across all regions

Step 1: Source (connect to database)         ← Folds
Step 2: Filter region = 'NORTH'              ← Folds (WHERE region='NORTH')
Step 3: Remove columns (drop internal_notes) ← Folds (SELECT without column)
Step 4: Change type (voltage to number)      ← Folds (CAST)
-- FOLD BOUNDARY --
Step 5: Add custom column (capacity_band)    ← Does NOT fold
Step 6: Pivot by voltage_class               ← Does NOT fold
Result: Steps 1-4 execute as a single SQL query on the database. Only ~50,000 rows (NORTH region) transfer to the mashup engine. Steps 5-6 execute locally on those 50,000 rows.
The Power Query editor provides a rich library of visual transformations. These cover the vast majority of energy reference data preparation scenarios without writing code. Core Transformations

Transformation What It Does Energy Example Filter Rows Keep or remove rows by condition Remove decommissioned substations (status ≠ 'ACTIVE'). Filter meters to a specific grid region. Remove Columns Drop unneeded columns Remove internal audit fields before writing to Gold. Drop PII columns (customer names) for a public dataset. Rename Columns Change column names Rename 'SUBST_ID' to 'substation_id'. Standardize to snake_case across all sources. Change Type Set data types Cast voltage_kv from text to decimal. Parse commission_date from text to date. Replace Values Substitute values Replace null regions with 'UNKNOWN'. Standardize 'N/A', 'n/a', 'NA' to null. Split Column Split one column into multiple Split 'City, State' from meter addresses into separate columns. Merge Columns Combine columns Create a composite key: concat(region, '-', substation_id). Add Custom Column Calculated column with M expression Capacity band: if voltage_kv >= 110 then 'Transmission' else 'Distribution'. Group By Aggregate by group Count meters per substation. Sum installed_capacity_mw per region. Pivot / Unpivot Reshape data Unpivot monthly generation columns (jan_mwh, feb_mwh, ...) into rows (month, generation_mwh). Merge Queries (Join) Join two queries Enrich meter metadata with substation attributes. Join tariff codes to tariff descriptions. Append Queries Stack rows from multiple sources Combine substation registries from NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST into a unified table.
Energy Walkthrough: Building a Substation Reference Table Let’s walk through a realistic energy transformation step by step:

Scenario The grid operations team maintains a substation registry in an Azure SQL database. The table has 500,000 rows, includes internal columns, uses inconsistent naming, and has null values in the region column. You need a clean reference table in the Gold Lakehouse for Power BI dashboards.
Step-by-step transformation:

Step 1: Source — Connect to Azure SQL Database, select dbo.substations table. Step 2: Filter Rows — Keep only rows where status = 'ACTIVE'. (Folds to SQL WHERE.) Step 3: Remove Columns — Drop internal_notes, created_by, modified_by, temp_flag. (Folds to SELECT.) Step 4: Rename Columns — SUBST_ID → substation_id, SUBST_NAME → substation_name, VOLT_KV → voltage_kv, REGION_CD → region, COMM_DT → commission_date. (Folds.) Step 5: Change Type — voltage_kv to Decimal Number, commission_date to Date. (Folds.) Step 6: Replace Values — In region column, replace null with 'UNKNOWN'. (May or may not fold depending on source.) Step 7: Add Custom Column — voltage_class: if voltage_kv >= 110 then 'Transmission' else if voltage_kv >= 33 then 'Sub-Transmission' else 'Distribution'. (Does not fold — executes locally.) Step 8: Add Custom Column — age_years: Number.Round(Duration.TotalDays(DateTime.LocalNow() - commission_date) / 365.25, 0). (Does not fold.) Step 9: Set Destination — Lakehouse: lh_gold_grid, Table: dim_substations, Mode: Replace.

The first five steps fold into a single SQL query. Only active substations with the selected columns transfer to the engine. Steps 6–8 execute locally on the filtered dataset. The result writes to the Gold Lakehouse as a clean dimension table for Power BI. Working with Dates in Energy Data Date transformations are critical for energy platforms. Power Query’s Transform tab provides: Extract (Year, Month, Quarter, Week, Day of Week, Day Name), Calculate (Age, Start/End of Month, Start/End of Quarter), and Parse (text to date with format specification). These create columns for time-based grouping in downstream analytics — revenue by quarter, maintenance schedules by month, commission year for asset aging analysis. Conditional Logic — The Add Conditional Column The “Add Conditional Column” feature creates if/then/else logic visually:

Every Power Query transformation is powered by M (Power Query Formula Language) — a functional, case-sensitive, expression-based language designed for data transformation. When you click buttons in the visual editor, M code is generated behind the scenes. Understanding M gives you access to capabilities that the visual editor does not expose. M Fundamentals: The let...in Structure
let // Step 1: Connect to the substation database Source = Sql.Database("grid-db.database.windows.net", "grid_ops"),
// Step 2: Navigate to table substations = Source{[Schema="dbo", Item="substations"]}[Data],
// Step 3: Filter active only Active = Table.SelectRows(substations, each [status] = "ACTIVE"),
// Step 4: Remove internal columns Cleaned = Table.RemoveColumns(Active, {"internal_notes", "created_by", "modified_by"}),
// Step 5: Rename to snake_case Renamed = Table.RenameColumns(Cleaned, { {"SUBST_ID", "substation_id"}, {"SUBST_NAME", "substation_name"}, {"VOLT_KV", "voltage_kv"}, {"REGION_CD", "region"} }),
// Step 6: Set types Typed = Table.TransformColumnTypes(Renamed, { {"voltage_kv", type number}, {"commission_date", type date} }),
// Step 7: Replace null regions RegionFixed = Table.ReplaceValue(Typed, null, "UNKNOWN", Replacer.ReplaceValue, {"region"}),

// Step 8: Add voltage class
    WithClass = Table.AddColumn(RegionFixed, "voltage_class", each
        if [voltage_kv] >= 110 then "Transmission"
        else if [voltage_kv] >= 33 then "Sub-Transmission"
        else "Distribution",
        type text)
in
    WithClass

Each step is a variable assignment. Each step references the previous step by name. The in clause specifies which step’s result is the output. This linear, step-by-step structure maps directly to the Applied Steps pane in the visual editor. Key M Functions for Energy Data

Function Purpose Energy Example Table.SelectRows Filter rows Keep active substations, filter by region or voltage class Table.RemoveColumns Drop columns Remove internal audit fields before writing to Gold Table.RenameColumns Rename columns Standardize legacy system column names to snake_case Table.AddColumn Calculated column Voltage class, tariff band, asset age, capacity utilization % Table.TransformColumnTypes Set data types Cast voltage from text to number, parse date strings Table.ReplaceValue Replace values Replace null regions with 'UNKNOWN', standardize codes Table.Group Aggregate Count meters per substation, sum capacity per region Table.NestedJoin Join two tables Enrich meters with substation attributes, join tariff lookups Table.Combine Append/union tables Combine regional substation files into a single table Table.UnpivotOtherColumns Unpivot Reshape monthly generation columns into month/value rows

When to Write M Directly Use the visual editor for standard transformations. Drop into M when you need: dynamic table references or parameterized sources, custom error handling (try...otherwise expressions), complex text parsing (e.g., extracting meter IDs from composite strings), list generation and iteration for date spines or lookup generation, web API pagination logic (Web.Contents with RelativePath), or reusable helper functions. Important: M is case-sensitive. Table.SelectRows is correct; table.selectrows will fail. Column names are referenced in square brackets: [column_name]. Every M expression must return a value — there are no side effects.

A Dataflow Gen2 without a data destination is a transformation that goes nowhere. The data destination is where the output of each query lands. This is the key upgrade from Gen1 — Dataflows Gen2 write directly to Fabric storage. Supported Destinations

Destination Write Modes Energy Use Case Lakehouse Table Replace, Append Most common. Substation registry to lh_gold_grid. Tariff schedules to lh_silver_reference. Warehouse Table Replace, Append Gold reference data for SQL analysts. Asset summary tables for T-SQL reporting. KQL Database Append Enriched event metadata for Real-Time Intelligence dashboards. Azure SQL Database Replace, Append Write-back to operational systems. Sync reference data to partner databases. Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 Replace Export Parquet files to external storage for cross-platform sharing.

Replace vs. Append Replace overwrites the entire destination table on every run. Simple and predictable. Use for dimension tables that are refreshed in full: substation registry, tariff schedules, meter metadata, asset catalog. These are small enough to reload completely and benefit from always having the latest full snapshot. Append adds new rows without touching existing rows. Use for growing datasets: monthly generation reports arriving as new files each month, incremental weather station readings. Be careful — without deduplication logic, running the same Append dataflow twice produces duplicates. Warning: Append mode does not handle deduplication. If you re-run a dataflow that already succeeded, it inserts the same rows again. For idempotent ingestion, use Replace mode for reference data, or combine Append with incremental refresh to process only new data. Per-Query Destinations Each query in a dataflow can target a different destination. A single dataflow might write the substation query to lh_gold_grid/dim_substations, the tariff query to lh_gold_grid/dim_tariffs, and the meter metadata query to lh_gold_grid/dim_meters. This keeps related reference data transformations in one manageable dataflow.

The Two Execution Paths Mashup engine only (staging disabled) — All transformations run on the Power Query mashup engine — a single-node process. Fast for small datasets (thousands to low hundreds of thousands of rows). Appropriate for a 5,000-row tariff schedule or a 20,000-row asset catalog. Mashup + Spark staging (staging enabled) — The mashup engine handles source connection and initial extraction. Large datasets are staged (temporarily written) to OneLake as Parquet files, and subsequent transformations run on Fabric’s Spark engine. Essential for datasets exceeding 100,000 rows — such as a 500,000-row substation registry or a year of hourly pricing data (8,760 hours × 50 regions = 438,000 rows). When to Enable Staging

Scenario Staging Why Small reference data (<100K rows) Off Mashup engine is fast enough. Staging adds overhead (serialization + Spark startup). Large dataset (>100K rows) On Spark parallelism is essential for acceptable performance. Complex joins between large tables On Spark handles distributed joins far better than the single-node mashup engine. Query folds completely to source Off If all transforms push down to the database, there is nothing for Spark to do. Dataflow takes >10 minutes without staging On The time savings from Spark almost certainly outweigh the staging overhead.
Performance Best Practices

items: Maximize query folding. Put all foldable steps first (filters, column removal, type changes) before unfoldable steps. This minimizes the data volume entering the mashup engine.
Enable staging for any query over 100,000 rows.
Remove unnecessary columns early. Every dropped column reduces memory and transfer time.
Avoid unnecessary sorting. Let the destination engine sort at query time.
Monitor dataflow refresh duration in the Monitoring Hub. If consistently over 30 minutes, investigate folding, staging, or switching to a Notebook.

blocks.content: Without incremental refresh, every dataflow run processes the entire source from scratch. For an energy pricing table with 10 million rows spanning 5 years, that means reading and writing all 10 million rows daily — even when only 1,000 new rows appeared. Incremental refresh solves this by processing only new data. How Incremental Refresh Works Incremental refresh uses a watermark column — typically a timestamp indicating when a row was created or last modified. The dataflow tracks the highest watermark from the previous run and filters the source on the next run:
Run 1: Read ALL hourly prices (initial full load) Watermark saved: 2026-03-07 23:00:00
Run 2: Read only WHERE price_timestamp > '2026-03-07 23:00:00' Only 24 new rows (today's 24 hourly prices) Watermark saved: 2026-03-08 23:00:00
Run 3: Read only WHERE price_timestamp > '2026-03-08 23:00:00' ...and so on

Configuring Incremental Refresh
•  Select the query in the dataflow editor.
•  Enable “Incremental refresh” in the query settings.
•  Select the watermark column (e.g., price_timestamp, modified_date, row_version).
•  Choose the comparison operator (greater than, or greater than or equal to).
•  Set the destination update method to “Append.”
The first run performs a full load. Subsequent runs filter to new rows only and append them.
Limitations
•  The source must have a reliable watermark column. If rows are updated without changing the watermark, the dataflow misses the changes.
•  Incremental refresh handles inserts well but does not handle updates or deletes. For those, use a Notebook with MERGE logic (Module DE6).
•  The watermark is per-query, not per-destination.
Best for: Append-only energy data: hourly pricing records, daily generation totals, monthly weather summaries. Not suitable for slowly changing dimensions (substation attributes that get updated) — use Replace mode or a Notebook with MERGE for those.

sections.title: Dataflows Gen2 vs. Notebooks — When to Use Which

blocks.content: Criterion Dataflows Gen2 Spark Notebooks Interface Visual (Power Query editor) Code (PySpark, Spark SQL) Primary persona Analysts, BI developers, citizen engineers Data engineers, data scientists Learning curve Low — familiar to Excel / Power BI users Higher — requires Python / Spark knowledge Transformation complexity Low to medium Unlimited — any logic expressible in code Data volume sweet spot Thousands to low millions of rows Millions to billions of rows MERGE / upsert support No native MERGE — only Replace or Append Full MERGE via Delta Lake Testing Manual preview in editor Unit tests, assertions, CI/CD Version control Limited (JSON-based, not human-readable) Full Git integration (Python diffs cleanly)

Decision Framework for Energy Platforms Choose Dataflows Gen2 when: the data is reference/dimensional (substation registry, tariff codes, asset catalog, meter metadata), the transformation is straightforward (filter, join, rename, type), the dataset is under a few million rows, and the owner is an operations analyst or BI developer who knows Power Query. Choose Notebooks when: the data is high-volume transactional (50M meter readings, SCADA telemetry, clickstream), the transformation requires MERGE/upsert, custom libraries, data quality frameworks, or complex business logic, and the owner is a data engineer. Use both when: a Pipeline orchestrates the entire daily refresh: Dataflows Gen2 load reference data (substations, tariffs) while Notebooks process meter readings. The Pipeline runs both in parallel, each using the best tool for its data type.

pl_daily_grid_refresh (master pipeline)
  │
  ├── Dataflow Gen2: df_reference_data   (parallel)
  │   ├── Query: Substations → dim_substations (Replace)
  │   ├── Query: Tariffs → dim_tariffs (Replace)
  │   └── Query: Meter Metadata → dim_meters (Replace)
  │
  ├── Notebook: nb_bronze_meters         (parallel)
  ├── Notebook: nb_bronze_scada           (parallel)
  ├── Notebook: nb_bronze_pricing          (parallel)
  │
  │   [all complete]
  │
  ├── Notebook: nb_silver_transform
  └── Notebook: nb_gold_star_schema

This hybrid approach uses each tool where it excels: Dataflows Gen2 for the 3 small dimension tables (owned by operations analysts), Notebooks for the 3 high-volume Bronze loads (owned by data engineers). The Pipeline orchestrates both and ensures everything completes before Silver/Gold processing begins.

Dataflows Gen2 can be called as activities within a Pipeline, just like Notebooks:
•  Drag a “Dataflow” Activity onto the pipeline canvas.
•  Select the Dataflow Gen2 to execute.
•  Connect it to other activities with dependency links.
This enables workflows like: Copy Activity (land raw meter files) → Dataflow Gen2 (refresh dimension tables) → Notebook (Bronze/Silver/Gold processing). The Pipeline orchestrates the entire chain.
Standalone Scheduling
Dataflows Gen2 can also run on their own schedule, independent of Pipelines. This is useful when dimension data refreshes at a different cadence than transactional data — for example, the substation registry refreshes weekly while meter readings load daily. Configure the schedule in the dataflow’s settings page.
Monitoring Dataflow Runs
Dataflow runs appear in the Fabric Monitoring Hub alongside pipeline and notebook runs. Each run shows: status (succeeded/failed), duration, number of rows processed, and refresh type (full or incremental). For failed runs, the error details indicate whether the failure was in the source connection, a transformation step, or the destination write.
Module DE3 Checkpoint
You now understand:

items: What Dataflows Gen2 are — Power Query transformations with data destinations, the evolution of Gen1 with Spark staging and Fabric-native output
The Power Query Online editor — queries pane, diagram view, data preview, applied steps, formula bar, and Advanced Editor
Connectors and data sources — 300+ connectors for databases, files, cloud services, APIs, Fabric items, and Azure services
Query folding — the most important performance concept: push transforms to the source, structure foldable steps first, check folding with View Native Query
Visual transformations — filter, remove, rename, type, replace, split, merge, group, pivot/unpivot, join, append, conditional columns, date extractions
The M language — let...in structure, key functions (Table.SelectRows, Table.AddColumn, Table.Group, Table.NestedJoin), and when to write M directly
Data destinations — Lakehouse, Warehouse, KQL Database, Azure SQL, ADLS Gen2; Replace vs. Append modes; per-query destination configuration
Staging and performance — mashup engine vs. Spark staging, when to enable staging (>100K rows), folding-first strategy
Incremental refresh — watermark columns, append-only pattern, limitations for updates/deletes
Dataflows Gen2 vs. Notebooks — decision framework: Dataflows for reference data and analyst-owned transforms, Notebooks for high-volume and MERGE-heavy workloads, hybrid approach in Pipelines
Orchestration — calling Dataflows from Pipelines, standalone scheduling, monitoring runs

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab: Build an Energy Reference Data Dataflow
Before moving to Module DE4, complete these exercises:

items: Create a Dataflow Gen2 in your workspace. Connect to a sample data source — upload a CSV file with substation data (columns: substation_id, name, voltage_kv, region, status, commission_date) to your Lakehouse Files/ and connect to it, or use an Azure SQL database if available.

Apply at least six transformations: filter to active substations, remove internal columns, rename columns to snake_case, change voltage_kv to Decimal Number, replace null regions with 'UNKNOWN', and add a voltage_class custom column.
Open the Advanced Editor and inspect the generated M code. Modify one step directly in M (e.g., change the voltage_class thresholds) and verify the result in the data preview.

Set the data destination to a Lakehouse table (dim_substations) with Replace mode. Publish and run the dataflow. Verify the table appears in the Lakehouse and is queryable from the SQL analytics endpoint.
If your dataset exceeds 100K rows, enable staging on the query. Re-run and compare execution time with and without staging.

Add a second query to the same dataflow: connect to a tariff schedule source (or create a simple CSV with tariff_code, description, rate_per_kwh, effective_date). Transform it, set destination to dim_tariffs with Replace mode. Publish and run — both queries execute in the same dataflow refresh.

Create a Pipeline that orchestrates the Dataflow Gen2 alongside a Notebook: add a Dataflow Activity and a Notebook Activity in parallel. Run the pipeline and verify both complete in the Monitoring Hub.

Check folding: right-click the filter step and select View Native Query. Confirm the SQL WHERE clause appears. Then right-click the custom column step and observe that View Native Query is grayed out — this is the fold boundary.

blocks.content: You are not just cleaning data.

You are building a visual transformation layer that empowers grid operations analysts and tariff planners to prepare their own reference data without writing code, at cloud scale, with direct output to the Lakehouse. Combined with Pipelines for orchestration and Notebooks for high-volume processing, you now have the complete ingestion and transformation toolkit for an energy data platform.

In Module DE4, we shift gears to the Spark Engine — the code-first transformation powerhouse. You will learn how Apache Spark works inside Fabric, how sessions start from warm pools, how the Catalyst optimizer plans your queries, how to configure Environments for library management, and how to monitor and tune Spark jobs. This is where data engineering at scale truly begins.

chapters.title: DE4 Spark Engine
chapters.description: Understand Spark execution internals, scaling levers, and workload behavior.

blocks.content: Module DE4 Apache Spark Engine in Fabric The Distributed Compute Layer Estimated Time: 120 Minutes Module Objective: Understand Apache Spark inside Fabric — architecture, session lifecycle, Notebooks, multi-language support, Environments for library management, notebookutils for production workflows, the Catalyst optimizer, the Spark UI for debugging, and performance fundamentals for processing energy data at scale.
Welcome to Module DE4

You have now seen every way data enters Fabric: Pipelines move it (DE2), Dataflows Gen2 transform reference data visually (DE3), and the Lakehouse stores it (DE1). Starting with this module, we shift from ingestion to computation. Apache Spark is the distributed compute engine that powers all code-based processing in Fabric. Every PySpark transformation you write to cleanse 50 million meter readings, every Spark SQL query you execute to aggregate turbine output by grid region, every notebook that merges daily SCADA telemetry into Silver-layer tables — all of it runs on Spark. Spark is not an optional add-on. It is the engine that turns OneLake energy data into insight. In Fabric, Spark is fully managed and serverless. You do not provision clusters. You do not install software. You do not configure YARN or tune memory allocators. You open a Notebook, attach it to a Lakehouse, and run code. Fabric allocates the compute, manages the cluster lifecycle, and releases the resources when you are done. That sentence is the single most important insight in this module. This module covers: how Spark works inside Fabric (architecture, driver/executor model, sessions, pools), the Notebook experience in detail (cells, output, Lakehouse attachment, state management), multi-language support (PySpark, Spark SQL, Scala, R), Environments for managing libraries and Spark configuration, notebookutils for production patterns, reading and writing energy data in every format, the Catalyst optimizer and how it plans your queries, the Spark UI for debugging and performance, performance fundamentals (the five pillars), anti-patterns to avoid, and production notebook best practices. Every example processes energy industry data — smart meter readings, turbine telemetry, weather forecasts, and energy pricing.

Apache Spark is a distributed computing framework designed for large-scale data processing. It splits work across multiple machines (nodes), processes partitions of data in parallel, and aggregates the results. This parallelism is why Spark can process 50 million meter readings in 3 minutes — something a single-machine tool like pandas cannot do. Spark’s Logical Architecture

Driver: The control node. It holds your SparkSession, parses your code, creates the query execution plan through the Catalyst optimizer, and coordinates work across executors. In a Fabric Notebook, the driver runs when your session starts. Executors: The worker nodes. Each executor has multiple cores (for parallel tasks) and memory (for caching data). Executors read data partitions from OneLake, process them in parallel, and return results to the driver. When your notebook reads 50 million meter readings, each executor processes a fraction of the rows simultaneously. Tasks: The smallest unit of work. Each task processes one partition of data on one core of one executor. If your meter readings DataFrame has 200 partitions and your cluster has 8 cores total, Spark runs 8 tasks in parallel and cycles through all 200 partitions in 25 waves. Understanding this architecture matters because performance tuning in Spark is fundamentally about balancing partitions, cores, and memory. Too few partitions means not enough parallelism — some cores sit idle. Too many partitions means excessive overhead — the scheduler spends more time coordinating than computing. The right balance depends on your data size and cluster configuration. How Fabric Manages Spark In traditional Spark deployments (Databricks, EMR, HDInsight), you provision and manage clusters explicitly. In Fabric, Spark is serverless: No cluster provisioning. When you run a Notebook cell, Fabric allocates a Spark session from the capacity’s compute pool. Executors are assigned from available resources. You never see a cluster — you see a session. Starter pools for fast startup. Fabric maintains pre-warmed “starter pools” — reserved compute ready to serve Spark sessions within 10–20 seconds. Without them, cold allocation could take 1–3 minutes. Automatic shutdown. Idle sessions terminate after a configurable timeout (default: 20 minutes). This releases compute back to the capacity pool. You pay CUs only while the session is active. CU-based billing. Spark compute consumes Capacity Units from your Fabric capacity. There is no separate Spark billing. CU consumption depends on the number of vCores allocated and the session duration. High-concurrency mode. Multiple users can share a single Spark session, reducing startup overhead and CU consumption. Useful for teams collaborating on exploration in the same workspace. Important: Spark sessions in Fabric compete for CUs with all other workloads (SQL, Power BI, Pipelines). If your capacity is heavily loaded — for example, during the 02:00 AM daily meter load when 5 notebooks and 3 pipelines run simultaneously — Spark session startup may be delayed. Monitor the Capacity Metrics app to understand utilization patterns. Consider Autoscale Spark billing (DE2) to isolate Spark compute from other workloads.

Every time you run code in a Notebook, a Spark session is created. The session configuration determines how much compute is available and how Spark behaves. Understanding session configuration is essential for both performance and cost control. Programmatic Configuration

%%configure { "conf": { "spark.sql.shuffle.partitions": "400", "spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes": "128m", "spark.sql.adaptive.enabled": "true", "spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled": "true" }, "driverMemory": "28g", "driverCores": 4, "executorMemory": "56g", "executorCores": 8, "numExecutors": 4 }

Important: %%configure must run before any Spark code. If you run a Spark command first and then %%configure, the configuration is ignored because the session is already started. Key Configuration Parameters

Parameter Default What It Controls Energy Tuning Tip executorCores 4 Parallel tasks per executor. More cores = more parallelism. Increase to 8 for 50M+ meter reading transformations. executorMemory 28g RAM per executor. More memory = larger joins, more cache. Increase to 56g when joining meter readings with substation metadata (large shuffle). numExecutors 2 Number of executor nodes. More = wider parallelism. Increase to 4–8 for daily meter loads; keep at 2 for small reference data notebooks. driverMemory 28g RAM for the driver. Increase for .collect() or .toPandas(). Keep default unless you aggregate meter data to pandas for visualization. spark.sql.shuffle.partitions 200 Partitions after shuffle (joins, GROUP BY). Set to 400–800 for 50M+ row meter joins. Set to 20 for small reference data. spark.sql.adaptive.enabled true Adaptive Query Execution: dynamically adjusts partitions at runtime. Always leave enabled. AQE handles most partition tuning automatically. spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes 128m Target partition size when reading files. Keep default. Ensures each task processes ~128 MB of meter Parquet data.

Starter Pools and Custom Pools Starter pools are Fabric’s default compute allocation. When you start a session without custom configuration, Fabric uses a starter pool with pre-configured resources. Optimized for fast startup (~10–20 seconds) and general-purpose workloads. Ideal for development and exploration. Custom Spark pools can be configured at the workspace level by Fabric admins. You define node size (Small, Medium, Large, X-Large), node count, and auto-scaling rules. Useful for production workloads with predictable resource needs. For the daily meter processing pipeline, a custom pool with 4 Large nodes guarantees consistent performance regardless of other workloads in the capacity.

Notebooks are Fabric’s interactive development environment for Spark. If you have used Jupyter, Databricks, or Synapse notebooks, the Fabric Notebook will feel familiar. But it has Fabric-specific capabilities worth understanding in detail.
Notebook Structure
A Fabric Notebook is a sequence of cells. Each cell is either a code cell (executable) or a Markdown cell (documentation). Cells execute sequentially — the output of one cell is available to subsequent cells because they share the same Spark session.
Code cells — Contain PySpark, Spark SQL, Scala, or R code. Execute against the attached Spark session. Output renders directly below the cell.
Markdown cells — Formatted text, headers, lists, links. Documentation for what each section does and why. Use them liberally — a well-documented notebook is readable months later.
Attaching a Lakehouse
Every Notebook can be attached to a default Lakehouse. When attached:
•  Relative file paths resolve to the Lakehouse’s OneLake folder (e.g., 'Files/landing/meters/' maps to lh_bronze_grid/Files/landing/meters/).
•  Tables/ is accessible via Spark SQL table names (e.g., spark.sql('SELECT * FROM raw_meter_readings') reads from Tables/raw_meter_readings).
•  The Lakehouse explorer panel on the left shows the attached Lakehouse’s files and tables for easy browsing.
You can attach additional Lakehouses and reference them by name or via abfss:// paths. The default Lakehouse determines the implicit context for relative paths.
Multi-Language Support

Magic Command Language Energy Use Case %%pyspark (default) Python (PySpark) Primary language for data engineering. DataFrame API for meter cleansing, joins, aggregations. pandas integration for small-scale visualization. %%sql Spark SQL SQL-first analysis. Quick ad-hoc queries on meter tables. CREATE TABLE, INSERT, MERGE. Ideal for SQL-fluent grid analysts. %%scala Scala Native Spark language. Performance-critical code. Advanced Spark APIs not available in PySpark. %%r R (SparkR) Statistical analysis. R-native data frames. Used by energy forecasting teams with R backgrounds.

You can mix languages within a single notebook. A common pattern: use %%pyspark for data engineering, switch to %%sql for ad-hoc queries, and use %%pyspark again for visualization. Variables defined in one language cell are not directly accessible in another, but you can use temporary views as a bridge:

# PySpark cell: create a temp view from meter readings
df = spark.read.format('delta').load('Tables/raw_meter_readings')
df.createOrReplaceTempView('v_meters')

%%sql -- SQL cell: aggregate meter readings by region SELECT region, COUNT(*) AS reading_count, SUM(daily_kwh) AS total_kwh, AVG(daily_kwh) AS avg_kwh FROM v_meters WHERE reading_date >= '2026-01-01' GROUP BY region ORDER BY total_kwh DESC

Output and Visualization display(df) — Renders DataFrames as interactive, sortable, filterable tables. Includes a chart icon for bar, line, scatter, and pie charts directly from the data. No library needed. df.toPandas() — Convert small DataFrames to pandas for matplotlib, seaborn, or plotly visualizations. Warning: df.toPandas() collects the entire DataFrame to the driver node’s memory. For large DataFrames (millions of rows), this will crash the driver with OutOfMemoryError. Only use toPandas() on aggregated or filtered data that fits in driver memory (typically < 1 GB). For 50M meter readings, aggregate to daily totals by region first (resulting in ~thousands of rows), then convert to pandas.

An Environment is a Fabric item that packages Python/R libraries and Spark configuration into a reusable, version-controlled unit. Instead of installing libraries at the top of every notebook (pip install ...), you create an Environment, install libraries there, and attach it to your notebooks. Why Environments Matter Without Environments, library management on an energy platform is chaotic: every notebook installs its own libraries (version conflicts across 20 Bronze/Silver/Gold notebooks), installation runs at session start (adding 2–5 minutes), there is no guarantee two notebooks use the same version (subtle bugs in production), and production depends on interactively installed packages (not reproducible). Creating an Environment for Energy Data Engineering

# Environment: env_energy_engineering
#
# Public Libraries (from PyPI):
#   great_expectations == 1.0.0    # Data quality validation
#   delta-spark == 3.2.0           # Delta Lake Python API
#   pyarrow == 17.0.0              # Fast Parquet I/O
#   holidays == 0.50               # Energy pricing calendar
#   geopy == 2.4.0                 # Geolocation for substations
#   requests == 2.32.0             # Weather API calls
#
# Spark Properties:
#   spark.sql.shuffle.partitions = 400
#   spark.sql.adaptive.enabled = true
#   spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled = true
#   spark.sql.parquet.vorder.enabled = true

Attaching Environments Attach an Environment to a Notebook from the toolbar’s Environment dropdown. When attached, all libraries are pre-installed and available for import — no pip install needed. Spark properties from the Environment apply to the session automatically. If the notebook also has %%configure settings, notebook settings override Environment settings where they conflict. Workspace-Level Default Environment Admins can set a default Environment at the workspace level. All notebooks that do not specify their own Environment use the workspace default. This ensures consistent library versions across the entire energy engineering team. Important: Publishing an Environment can take several minutes because Fabric builds a container image with all specified libraries. Plan for this when adding or updating packages. Do not update production Environments during active pipeline runs.

notebookutils is a built-in Python library available in every Fabric Notebook. It provides utility functions for file operations, secrets, sessions, and notebook orchestration. These are the functions that turn an exploratory notebook into a production-grade component of your energy platform. File Operations (notebookutils.fs)

# List meter files in the landing zone
files = notebookutils.fs.ls('Files/landing/meters/')
for f in files:
    print(f'{f.name}  {f.size / 1e6:.1f} MB')

# Move processed meter files to archive
notebookutils.fs.mv(
    'Files/landing/meters/2026-03-07/',
    'Files/archive/meters/2026-03-07/',
    True  # overwrite if exists
)

# Check if today's file has arrived
exists = notebookutils.fs.exists('Files/landing/meters/2026-03-08/')
if not exists:
    raise FileNotFoundError('Meter file not yet available')

# Delete temp staging files
notebookutils.fs.rm('Files/staging/temp/', recurse=True)
Secrets (notebookutils.credentials)

# Retrieve weather API key from Azure Key Vault
api_key = notebookutils.credentials.getSecret(
    'https://energy-keyvault.vault.azure.net/',
    'weather-api-key'
)

# Use in a REST call
import requests
response = requests.get(
    'https://api.weather.example.com/v2/forecast',
    headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {api_key}'},
    params={'region': 'NORTH', 'date': '2026-03-08'}
)
Notebook Orchestration (notebookutils.notebook)

# Run child notebook with parameters
result = notebookutils.notebook.run(
    'nb_silver_meters',
    timeout_seconds=1800,  # 30 minutes
    arguments={
        'load_date': '2026-03-08',
        'grid_region': 'NORTH',
        'mode': 'incremental'
    }
)
print(f'Silver meters returned: {result}')

# Run multiple notebooks in parallel
notebookutils.notebook.runMultiple([
    {'name': 'nb_silver_meters',  'args': {'load_date': '2026-03-08'}},
    {'name': 'nb_silver_scada',   'args': {'load_date': '2026-03-08'}},
    {'name': 'nb_silver_weather',  'args': {'load_date': '2026-03-08'}},
    {'name': 'nb_silver_pricing',  'args': {'load_date': '2026-03-08'}},
])

runMultiple is a powerful pattern for parallelizing independent Silver-layer transformations. Instead of running four notebooks sequentially (taking 4x the time), you run them in parallel, limited only by available compute. Exit Values and Parameters

# Receive parameters from Pipeline
load_date = notebookutils.notebook.getParam('load_date', '2026-01-01')
grid_region = notebookutils.notebook.getParam('grid_region', 'ALL')
print(f'Processing meters for {grid_region} on {load_date}')

# ... processing logic ...

# Return row count to Pipeline
row_count = result_df.count()
notebookutils.notebook.exit(str(row_count))
Always provide a default value as the second argument to getParam(). This lets you run the notebook interactively (without a Pipeline) and still have sensible defaults for testing with sample data.

This section consolidates the core read/write patterns for every data format you will encounter on an energy platform. Think of it as a reference you will return to throughout the course. Reading Data
Delta Tables (from attached Lakehouse)

# By path
meters = spark.read.format('delta').load('Tables/raw_meter_readings')
# By table name (equivalent, uses metastore)
meters = spark.table('raw_meter_readings')
CSV (meter exports from SFTP)

meters_csv = spark.read.csv(
    'Files/landing/meters/2026-03-08/',
    header=True,
    inferSchema=True,
    sep=','  # explicit delimiter
)
JSON (weather forecasts from API)

weather = spark.read.json(
    'Files/landing/weather/2026-03-08.json',
    multiLine=True  # single JSON object, not NDJSON
)
Parquet (SCADA telemetry exports)
scada = spark.read.parquet('Files/landing/scada/')
Cross-Lakehouse (from another workspace)

gold_substations = spark.read.format('delta').load(
    'abfss://grid_prod@onelake.dfs.fabric.microsoft.com/'
    'lh_gold_grid.Lakehouse/Tables/dim_substations'
)
With Explicit Schema (production best practice)

from pyspark.sql.types import StructType, StructField, StringType, \
    DoubleType, DateType, TimestampType

meter_schema = StructType([
    StructField('meter_id', StringType(), False),
    StructField('reading_date', DateType(), False),
    StructField('daily_kwh', DoubleType(), True),
    StructField('peak_kw', DoubleType(), True),
    StructField('region', StringType(), True),
    StructField('substation_id', StringType(), True),
])

meters = spark.read.csv(
    'Files/landing/meters/',
    header=True,
    schema=meter_schema  # explicit schema, no inferSchema
)

Why explicit schemas matter: inferSchema reads the entire file once to guess types. This doubles read time for large CSV files. Worse, it can guess wrong (a meter_id like '00012345' becomes an integer, losing the leading zeros). In production, always define the schema explicitly. Writing Data

# Managed Delta table (recommended — registers in metastore)
df.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('raw_meter_readings')

# Append to existing table (incremental loads)
df.write.format('delta').mode('append').saveAsTable('raw_meter_readings')

# Partitioned write (by reading_date for daily meter data)
df.write.format('delta') \
    .mode('overwrite') \
    .partitionBy('reading_date') \
    .saveAsTable('meter_readings_partitioned')

# MERGE (upsert) — the workhorse for Silver-layer updates
from delta.tables import DeltaTable

target = DeltaTable.forName(spark, 'silver_meters')
target.alias('t').merge(
    source=updates_df.alias('s'),
    condition='t.meter_id = s.meter_id AND t.reading_date = s.reading_date'
).whenMatchedUpdateAll() \
 .whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \
 .execute()

# Control output file count (avoid small file problem)
df.coalesce(10).write.format('delta') \
    .mode('overwrite').saveAsTable('summary_table')

saveAsTable() vs. save(): saveAsTable() registers the table in the Lakehouse metastore, making it visible to the SQL analytics endpoint and Power BI. save() writes files to a path but does not register a table. Always use saveAsTable() for tables that downstream consumers need to query.

sections.title: The Catalyst Optimizer and Tungsten Engine

blocks.content: When you write df.filter(col('region') == 'NORTH').groupBy('substation_id').agg(sum('daily_kwh')), Spark does not execute your code literally. It creates a logical plan from your transformations, passes it through the Catalyst optimizer, and produces an optimized physical plan that may look very different from what you wrote — but produces the same result, faster. The Optimization Pipeline

Your PySpark / SQL Code
          │
          ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  1. Unresolved Logical Plan            │
  │     (what you wrote, as a tree)         │
  └───────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                      ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  2. Resolved Logical Plan               │
  │     (column names and types resolved)    │
  └───────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                      ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  3. Optimized Logical Plan              │
  │     Catalyst applies rules:              │
  │     - Predicate pushdown                 │
  │     - Projection pruning                 │
  │     - Constant folding                   │
  │     - Join reordering                    │
  └───────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                      ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  4. Physical Plan                       │
  │     - Chose join strategy (broadcast vs  │
  │       sort-merge based on table sizes)   │
  │     - Tungsten code generation           │
  └───────────────────┬─────────────────────┘
                      ▼
  ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
  │  5. Execution on Executors              │
  │     Tasks process partitions in parallel │
  └─────────────────────────────────────────┘

Key Optimizations Catalyst Applies Predicate pushdown — Filters are pushed as close to the data source as possible. On a Delta table partitioned by reading_date, a WHERE reading_date = '2026-03-08' skips all partition directories except March 8th. On a Z-Ordered table, it uses min/max statistics to skip files that cannot contain matching rows. Projection pruning — Only the columns referenced in the query are read from Parquet files. If you SELECT meter_id, daily_kwh FROM raw_meter_readings, Spark reads only those two columns, skipping all others. Parquet’s columnar format makes this extremely efficient. Join reordering — Catalyst reorders multi-table joins to minimize shuffle volume. If you join meter readings (50M rows) with substations (50K rows) with regions (12 rows), Catalyst will broadcast the small tables first. Broadcast join selection — When one side of a join is small (< 10 MB by default), Catalyst automatically broadcasts it to all executors, avoiding an expensive shuffle. Your 50K-row substation table is broadcast; the 50M-row meter table stays partitioned in place. Why this matters: PySpark DataFrames and Spark SQL are both processed by the same Catalyst optimizer. They produce the same physical plan and execute identically. There is no performance difference between the two — choose by readability and team preference. This is why we say “two interfaces, one engine.” Viewing the Query Plan

# View the logical plan
df.explain()

# View the full physical plan with optimization details
df.explain(mode='extended')

# View formatted plan (most readable)
df.explain(mode='formatted')

Reading query plans is a critical debugging skill. Look for FileScan (how many files/rows read? Is partition pruning working?), Filter (is it pushed down to scan level?), Exchange (shuffle — expensive, minimize these), and BroadcastHashJoin vs. SortMergeJoin (broadcast is fast for small tables).

sections.title: The Spark UI — Debugging and Performance

blocks.content: When a Spark job runs, Fabric provides access to the Spark UI — a web-based dashboard that shows every detail of your job’s execution. This is the single most important debugging tool for Spark performance problems. Accessing the Spark UI Click the “View Spark jobs” link below a code cell after it finishes executing. This opens the Spark UI showing all jobs, stages, and tasks for that cell’s execution. Key Spark UI Tabs

Tab What It Shows When to Use Jobs All Spark jobs triggered by your code. Each action (count, write, show) creates a job. Overview: see how many jobs ran, which succeeded, and overall duration. Stages Each job is split into stages (separated by shuffles). Shows tasks per stage, data read/written. Identify the slowest stage. Check for data skew (one task takes much longer than others). Tasks Individual tasks within a stage. Duration, data size, GC time, shuffle per task. Find the specific slow task. Check for skew: one task processing 10x more data than others. Storage Cached DataFrames and their memory usage. Verify .cache() is working and not consuming too much executor memory. SQL/DataFrame Visual query execution plan with operators, row counts, and timing. Understand the full plan. Find unnecessary shuffles. Verify partition pruning on meter date columns. Environment Spark configuration, classpath, system properties. Verify %%configure or Environment settings are applied correctly.

Energy Example: Diagnosing a Slow Meter Join Your notebook joins 50M meter readings with 50K substations and the job takes 45 minutes instead of the expected 5. Here is how you use the Spark UI to diagnose:

items: Open the SQL/DataFrame tab. Find the join operator. Is it SortMergeJoin (expensive) or BroadcastHashJoin (fast)?

If SortMergeJoin: the substation table was not broadcast. Check its size — if it’s under 10 MB, Catalyst should have broadcast it. Look for spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold in the Environment tab.
Check the Stages tab. Find the shuffle stage (Exchange). How much data was shuffled? If 50M rows were shuffled, the join is redistributing the entire meter table.
Check Tasks within the slow stage. Is one task processing 90% of the data? That’s skew — one partition has far more meters than others (e.g., the NORTH region has 40M of the 50M rows).
Fix: force a broadcast hint on the substation table, or salt the join key to distribute the NORTH region’s data across multiple partitions.

blocks.content: 1. Minimize Shuffles A shuffle redistributes data across partitions — every row is sent to the partition that matches its new key. Shuffles involve serialization, network transfer, and deserialization. They are the most expensive operation in Spark. Shuffles happen during: joins (SortMergeJoin), GROUP BY aggregations, DISTINCT, repartition(), and window functions with PARTITION BY. Minimize them by: using broadcast joins for small tables (like substation or tariff lookups), pre-partitioning data to match join keys, and combining multiple aggregations into a single GROUP BY. 2. Right-Size Partitions Too few partitions: some cores sit idle while others are overloaded. Too many: excessive task scheduling overhead. Target 128 MB per partition for most energy workloads. For 50M meter readings at ~50 bytes/row = ~2.5 GB, that is about 20 partitions. For a daily append of 2M rows = ~100 MB, that is 1 partition. Use spark.sql.shuffle.partitions to control post-shuffle count, and spark.sql.files.maxPartitionBytes for read-time partitioning. 3. Push Filters Early Apply WHERE clauses as early as possible so Catalyst can push them down to the file scan. On Delta tables partitioned by reading_date, a filter on date enables partition pruning — entire directories are skipped. On Z-Ordered tables, it enables file pruning based on min/max statistics. Filtering 50M rows to 2M (one day’s data) before a join is 25x faster than joining first and filtering after. 4. Cache Judiciously If you read a DataFrame multiple times (once for quality checks, once for writing), cache it: df.cache(). This stores it in executor memory, avoiding re-reading from OneLake. But do not cache everything — cache consumes memory that could be used for shuffles and computation. Cache only DataFrames that are reused and expensive to recompute. For a large meter readings DataFrame that feeds both a Silver MERGE and a quality validation, caching saves a second full Delta scan. 5. Use Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) AQE is enabled by default in Fabric. It dynamically adjusts shuffle partitions at runtime, converts sort-merge joins to broadcast joins when one side turns out to be small after filtering, and coalesces small partitions after shuffles. Leave it enabled. AQE handles 80% of partition tuning automatically, which is why the default shuffle.partitions of 200 works reasonably well for most workloads.
Common Anti-Patterns

Anti-Pattern Why It Hurts Fix df.collect() on large DataFrames Pulls all 50M meter rows to driver memory. Crashes with OOM. Use .show(20), .take(100), or .limit(1000). Only collect aggregated results. UDFs (User Defined Functions) Serializes data row-by-row between JVM and Python. Extremely slow for 50M rows. Use built-in pyspark.sql.functions (when, otherwise, regexp_replace). Rewrite logic with Column expressions. for loop over rows Python loops negate Spark’s parallelism. Processes rows sequentially on the driver. Express logic as DataFrame operations (withColumn, when, otherwise). Think in columns, not rows. Reading entire table when only a subset needed Reads all 365 days of meter data when you need 1 day. Filter in the read: spark.table('meters').filter(col('reading_date') == '2026-03-08'). Use partitioning. Too many small writes Appending 1,000 rows every hour creates 24 tiny Parquet files/day. Use coalesce(n) before writing. Run OPTIMIZE weekly to compact files. Not using explicit schemas on CSV inferSchema reads the file twice. Guesses types wrong (meter_id '00012345' becomes int). Define StructType schema explicitly. Faster and correct.

A notebook that works interactively is not necessarily ready for production. Production notebooks on an energy platform must be reliable (process 50M meter readings without failure), maintainable (readable by the next engineer), and monitorable (report status to the Pipeline).
Structure and Organization
•  Start every notebook with a Markdown header: title, purpose, author, last modified date, parameter descriptions.
•  Organize code into logical sections with Markdown cells: Configuration, Read, Transform, Validate, Write, Cleanup.
•  Use parameters (getParam) for all values that change between runs: dates, table names, file paths, modes.
•  End every notebook with exit() to return a status or metric to the calling Pipeline.
Error Handling Pattern

try:
    # Read
    meters = spark.read.format('delta').load('Tables/raw_meter_readings')
    row_count = meters.count()
    if row_count == 0:
        raise ValueError('raw_meter_readings is empty — aborting')

    # Transform
    result = meters.filter(col('daily_kwh') >= 0) \
        .withColumn('reading_month', month(col('reading_date')))

    # Validate
    result_count = result.count()
    null_count = result.filter(col('meter_id').isNull()).count()
    if null_count > 0:
        raise ValueError(f'{null_count} null meter_ids detected')

    # Write
    result.write.format('delta').mode('overwrite') \
        .saveAsTable('silver_meter_readings')
notebookutils.notebook.exit(f'SUCCESS: {result_count} rows')

except Exception as e:
    print(f'ERROR: {str(e)}')
    notebookutils.notebook.exit(f'FAILED: {str(e)}')
    raise  # Re-raise so Pipeline sees the failure

Data Quality Checks
•  Row count checks — Is the output within expected bounds? (Expect 1M–5M daily meter readings, alert if below 500K.)
•  Null checks — Are required columns (meter_id, reading_date) free of NULLs?
•  Range checks — Are daily_kwh values between 0 and 10,000? Are dates within the expected range?
•  Uniqueness checks — Is the combination of meter_id + reading_date unique (no duplicate readings)?
•  Schema checks — Does the output DataFrame match the expected schema (correct column names and types)?
Version Control
Fabric Notebooks support Git integration through workspace Git settings. When enabled, notebook changes are committed to a Git repository (Azure DevOps or GitHub). Treat notebooks as production code: use Git, write clear documentation, add error handling, and never hard-code values that should be parameters.
Module DE4 Checkpoint
You now understand:

items: Spark architecture in Fabric — driver/executor model, tasks and partitions, serverless provisioning, starter pools, CU-based billing, high-concurrency mode
Session configuration — %%configure for executors, memory, cores, and shuffle partitions; starter pools vs. custom pools; key parameters for energy workloads
The Notebook experience — code and Markdown cells, Lakehouse attachment, multi-language support (PySpark, SQL, Scala, R), temp views for cross-language bridging, display() and toPandas()
Environments — centralized library management (PyPI packages for energy data quality, geolocation, holiday calendars), Spark property configuration, workspace-level defaults
notebookutils — fs (file operations and archiving), credentials (Key Vault secrets for weather APIs), notebook.run/runMultiple (parallel Silver-layer processing), exit/getParam (Pipeline integration)
Reading and writing energy data — Delta, CSV, JSON, Parquet reads; explicit schemas; saveAsTable vs. save; MERGE for Silver-layer upserts; coalesce for file count control; cross-Lakehouse access
Catalyst optimizer — the 5-stage optimization pipeline, predicate pushdown, projection pruning, join reordering, broadcast join selection; why PySpark and SQL produce identical plans
Spark UI — Jobs, Stages, Tasks, SQL/DataFrame tabs; diagnosing a slow meter join step-by-step
Performance fundamentals — five pillars (minimize shuffles, right-size partitions, push filters, cache judiciously, use AQE); six anti-patterns with fixes
Production best practices — notebook structure, error handling pattern, data quality checks, Git version control

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab
Before moving to Module DE5, complete these exercises:

items: Create an Environment (env_energy_engineering) with at least two PyPI libraries (e.g., great_expectations, holidays). Publish it. Attach it to a new Notebook and verify the libraries import without error.

Create a Notebook attached to your Bronze Lakehouse. Read a CSV from Files/landing/ with an explicit schema (no inferSchema). Add metadata columns (_load_timestamp, _source_system). Write the result as a Delta table with saveAsTable().
Switch to a %%sql cell. Create a temp view from the Delta table. Write a GROUP BY query aggregating daily_kwh by region. Verify the result renders with display().

Open the Spark UI after running your transformation. Navigate to the SQL/DataFrame tab. Identify the FileScan operator (how many files were read?), any Filter operators, and any Exchange operators (shuffles).
Run df.explain(mode='formatted') on a filtered, grouped query. Identify whether the filter was pushed down to the scan level (predicate pushdown) or applied after the scan.

Write a parameterized notebook that receives load_date via getParam() and returns a row count via exit(). Create a Pipeline with a Notebook Activity that passes a specific date. Run the Pipeline and verify the return value appears in the monitoring output.

Intentionally introduce a data quality issue (insert rows with NULL meter_id). Add a validation check in the notebook that catches the issue, raises an exception, and exits with a failure message. Verify the Pipeline detects the failure.
Use notebookutils.notebook.runMultiple to run two notebooks in parallel. Verify both complete and print their return values.

blocks.content: You are not just writing code in a notebook.

You are operating a distributed compute engine that can process 50 million meter readings in minutes, orchestrated through Pipelines, governed by Environments, and monitored through the Spark UI. This is industrial-scale energy data engineering.

In Module DE5, we go deep into PySpark Transformations — the DataFrame API that you will use for every transformation in this course. You will master selects, filters, joins, aggregations, window functions, null handling, type casting, UDFs, and the complete PySpark toolkit for turning raw meter readings into clean, governed, analytics-ready energy data. Think in columns, not in rows.

chapters.title: DE5 PySpark Transformations
chapters.description: Apply PySpark transformation patterns for production data pipelines.

blocks.content: Module DE5 PySpark Transformations Deep Dive Think in Columns, Not in Rows Estimated Time: 150 Minutes Module Objective: Master the PySpark DataFrame API — selects, filters, column expressions, joins, aggregations, window functions, null handling, type casting, deduplication, pivot/unpivot, UDFs, and the complete transformation toolkit for production energy data engineering.
Welcome to Module DE5

Module DE4 taught you the Spark engine, the Notebook experience, and the infrastructure. This module is about what you do with that engine: transform data. The PySpark DataFrame API is the primary language of data engineering in Fabric. Every transformation — filtering invalid meter readings, joining readings to substation metadata, calculating running consumption totals, deduplicating overlapping SCADA exports, handling null voltage measurements, casting timestamp strings, pivoting regional generation reports — is expressed through this API. Mastering it is not optional. It is the core skill. The mental model that unlocks PySpark is: think in columns, not in rows. In a Python for loop, you process one meter reading at a time. In PySpark, you describe what should happen to an entire column, and Spark applies it in parallel across 50 million readings. You never touch individual rows. You describe transformations declaratively, and Spark executes them across the cluster. This module is code-heavy by design. Every concept includes a working code example using energy data that you should run in a Fabric Notebook. Reading is not enough — you must write and run the code to build muscle memory. We will cover: the DataFrame and Column objects, selecting and renaming columns, filtering rows, adding computed columns, type casting, null handling, string and date functions, joins (with broadcast patterns for dimension lookups), aggregations, window functions (running totals, lag/lead for period comparisons, deduplication), pivot/unpivot, union, and writing output. Every example processes smart grid data: meter readings, substations, SCADA telemetry, energy pricing, and weather forecasts.

A DataFrame is PySpark’s core data structure. It is a distributed, immutable collection of rows organized into named columns. Every read, every transformation, and every write operates on DataFrames. Key Properties Distributed — The data is split into partitions stored across multiple executors. You interact with the DataFrame as if it were a single table; Spark handles the distribution. Immutable — You cannot modify a DataFrame in place. Every transformation returns a new DataFrame. The original is unchanged. This immutability enables Spark’s lazy evaluation and fault tolerance. Lazily evaluated — Transformations (filter, select, join) are recorded but not executed immediately. Spark builds an execution plan and only runs it when you trigger an action (count, show, write). This lets Spark optimize the entire plan before executing anything. Typed columns — Each column has a data type (StringType, DoubleType, DateType, TimestampType, etc.). Types are enforced on write to Delta tables. Creating DataFrames — Energy Data

from pyspark.sql.functions import *
from pyspark.sql.types import *

# Read from Delta table (most common in Fabric)
meters = spark.table('raw_meter_readings')

# Read from CSV (meter export files)
meters = spark.read.csv('Files/landing/meters/', header=True, inferSchema=True)

# Create from Python list (useful for testing)
data = [
    ('M001', '2026-03-08', 42.5, 8.1, 'NORTH', 'SUB-101'),
    ('M002', '2026-03-08', 38.2, 7.4, 'NORTH', 'SUB-101'),
    ('M003', '2026-03-08', 51.0, 9.8, 'SOUTH', 'SUB-205'),
]
schema = ['meter_id', 'reading_date', 'daily_kwh', 'peak_kw',
          'region', 'substation_id']
meters = spark.createDataFrame(data, schema)

# Inspect
meters.printSchema()    # Column names and types
meters.show(5)          # First 5 rows as text
display(meters)         # Fabric rich rendering
meters.count()          # Row count (triggers execution!)
meters.columns          # ['meter_id', 'reading_date', ...]
Transformations vs. Actions

Type Examples Behavior Transformation select, filter, join, groupBy, withColumn, drop, orderBy Lazy — recorded but not executed. Returns a new DataFrame. Action show, count, collect, write, first, take, toPandas Triggers execution of all preceding transformations. Returns a result.

Important: Every action triggers the entire transformation chain from the source read. If you call meters.count() and then meters.show(), Spark reads the Delta table twice. To avoid this, cache the DataFrame before multiple actions: meters.cache().
Selecting Columns

# Select specific columns
meters.select('meter_id', 'reading_date', 'daily_kwh')

# Select with Column objects (equivalent)
meters.select(col('meter_id'), col('reading_date'), col('daily_kwh'))

# Select with inline expressions
meters.select(
    col('meter_id'),
    col('daily_kwh'),
    (col('daily_kwh') * 0.12).alias('estimated_cost_eur'),
    col('region').alias('grid_region')
)
Renaming Columns

# Rename a single column
meters.withColumnRenamed('daily_kwh', 'consumption_kwh')

# Rename multiple (chain)
meters.withColumnRenamed('daily_kwh', 'consumption_kwh') \
    .withColumnRenamed('peak_kw', 'peak_demand_kw')

# Bulk rename: convert SCADA uppercase columns to snake_case
import re
scada = spark.table('raw_scada_telemetry')
for c_name in scada.columns:
    new_name = re.sub(r'([A-Z])', r'_\1', c_name).lower().lstrip('_')
    scada = scada.withColumnRenamed(c_name, new_name)
Dropping Columns

# Drop internal audit columns before writing to Silver
meters.drop('_source_file', '_load_batch_id', 'internal_flag')

Tip: When reading wide SCADA tables with 200+ columns, select only the columns you need immediately after reading. This reduces data transfer and memory consumption across all subsequent operations: scada = spark.table('raw_scada').select('turbine_id', 'timestamp', 'power_output_kw', 'wind_speed_ms', 'rotor_rpm').
Filtering removes rows that do not match a condition. filter() and where() are interchangeable. Basic Filters — Energy Examples

# Filter by region
meters.filter(col('region') == 'NORTH')

# Filter by consumption threshold (detect high-usage meters)
meters.filter(col('daily_kwh') > 500)

# Filter by date range (one month of readings)
meters.filter(
    (col('reading_date') >= '2026-03-01') &
    (col('reading_date') < '2026-04-01')
)

# Filter by list of substations
meters.filter(col('substation_id').isin('SUB-101', 'SUB-102', 'SUB-103'))

# Exclude negative readings (data quality)
meters.filter(col('daily_kwh') >= 0)

# NOT condition: exclude decommissioned meters
meters.filter(~col('status').isin('DECOMMISSIONED', 'REMOVED'))
Combining Conditions

# AND: active meters in NORTH region with valid readings
meters.filter(
    (col('region') == 'NORTH') &
    (col('daily_kwh') >= 0) &
    (col('daily_kwh') < 10000)  # reject implausible values
)

# OR: meters in either transmission substations
meters.filter(
    (col('substation_id') == 'SUB-101') |
    (col('substation_id') == 'SUB-102')
)

Warning: Always wrap each condition in parentheses when combining with & or |. Without parentheses, Python operator precedence produces unexpected results. (col('a') > 1) & (col('b') > 2) is correct. col('a') > 1 & col('b') > 2 is NOT. Null Filtering

# Keep readings where daily_kwh is NOT null
meters.filter(col('daily_kwh').isNotNull())

# Find meters with missing substation assignment
meters.filter(col('substation_id').isNull())

# Drop rows where ANY key column is null
meters.dropna(subset=['meter_id', 'reading_date', 'daily_kwh'])
withColumn() adds a new column or replaces an existing column. It is the workhorse for energy data transformation. Computed Columns — Energy Examples

# Energy cost estimation (rate * consumption)
