•  Search for tables by name, description, or tag across all workspaces.
•  View table schemas, column descriptions, and sample data.
•  Apply endorsements: Promote Silver tables that are ready for data scientists. Certify Gold tables that are approved for regulatory reporting.
•  Organize into domains: create a ‘Grid Operations’ domain, assign workspaces, enable domain-level discovery.
Documentation Best Practices
•  Add descriptions to every table and column in the Lakehouse. These descriptions surface in the OneLake Catalog and in Power BI field pickers.
•  Create a data dictionary mapping every Gold table’s columns, business definitions, source lineage, and refresh frequency.
•  Document grain for every fact table. The grain statement should be unambiguous: “One row per meter per day.”
•  Tag tables with domain labels (grid_operations, energy_trading, regulatory) for catalog discoverability.
•  Use Notebook markdown cells to document transformation logic inline with the code. The best documentation lives next to the code it describes.

sections.title: The Complete Pipeline — End to End

blocks.content: Here is how every module in the DE track connects through the medallion architecture:

pl_daily_grid_refresh (DE2: Pipeline)
  │
  ├── Copy Activity: SFTP → Files/landing/meters/ (DE2)
  ├── Copy Activity: API → Files/landing/weather/ (DE2)
  ├── Dataflow Gen2: Substations → dim_substations (DE3, Replace)
  ├── Dataflow Gen2: Tariffs → dim_tariffs (DE3, Replace)
  │
  ├── Notebook: nb_bronze_meters  (DE4/DE5: read CSV, add metadata, append)
  ├── Notebook: nb_bronze_weather (DE4/DE5: read JSON, add metadata, append)
  │
  ├── Notebook: nb_silver_meters  (DE5/DE6: dedup, cast, clean, MERGE)
  ├── Notebook: nb_silver_weather (DE5/DE6: dedup, cast, MERGE)
  │
  ├── Notebook: nb_quality_gate   (DE7: check rules, halt if CRITICAL fail)
  │
  ├── Notebook: nb_gold_dim_substations (DE7: SCD Type 2)
  ├── Notebook: nb_gold_fact_meters     (DE7: resolve surrogate keys, write fact)
  ├── Notebook: nb_gold_agg_daily       (DE7: aggregate for dashboards)
  │
  └── Notebook: nb_maintenance_weekly   (DE6: OPTIMIZE + VACUUM all layers)

Every module in the DE track contributes a piece: DE1 established the Lakehouses, DE2 built the Pipeline orchestration, DE3 loaded reference dimensions via Dataflows Gen2, DE4 provided the Spark engine, DE5 and DE6 supplied the PySpark and SQL transformation skills, and this module (DE7) defined the architectural framework that organizes all of it into a governed, layered, production-ready energy data platform.
Module DE7 Checkpoint
You now understand:

items: The medallion architecture — Bronze (raw, append-only, metadata-enriched), Silver (cleansed, deduplicated, conformed), Gold (star schema, aggregates, DirectLake-optimized); four benefits of layering
Bronze layer — schema-on-read, append-only contract, metadata columns, four ingestion patterns (file landing, full snapshot, incremental, CDC), late-arriving meter data handling
Silver layer — six standard transformations for energy data (dedup, cast, null handling, standardize, validate, conform), the Silver MERGE pattern, Silver table inventory

Gold layer — star schema design with fact_meter_readings at center, four dimension tables (substations, meters, date, tariffs), aggregate tables for dashboard performance, dim_date generation with SQL sequence
Implementing medallion in Fabric — one Lakehouse per layer layout, full workspace diagram with all items, naming conventions for every item type
Surrogate keys and dimension types — surrogate key generation, six dimension types (standard, date, degenerate, junk, role-playing, conformed)
Slowly changing dimensions — five SCD types compared, full SCD Type 2 implementation in PySpark for dim_substations (identify changes, expire, insert versions, handle new entities)
Data quality — six quality dimensions for energy data, reusable quality check framework in PySpark, quality gates in Pipelines, audit logging to quality_log table
Lineage and documentation — item-level lineage in Fabric portal, OneLake Catalog for discovery, endorsements, documentation best practices
End-to-end pipeline — complete pl_daily_grid_refresh showing how every DE track module contributes to the medallion flow

blocks.content: Hands-On Lab: Build a Medallion Energy Platform
Before moving to Module DE8 (Capstone), build the complete medallion architecture:

items: Create three Lakehouses: lh_bronze_grid, lh_silver_grid, lh_gold_grid.
Ingest raw data from at least two energy sources (meter readings CSV + weather JSON or pricing data) into lh_bronze_grid. Add metadata columns. Use append-only writes.
Build Silver notebooks: nb_silver_meters (deduplicate by meter_id + reading_date, cast types, validate ranges, MERGE) and nb_silver_weather or nb_silver_pricing.
Build a dim_date table using the SQL sequence generation pattern (2020–2030).
Build dim_substations with SCD Type 2: process a batch where one substation’s voltage_kv changes. Verify the old row is expired (is_current = False) and a new row is inserted.
Build fact_meter_readings: join Silver meters to Gold dimensions using surrogate keys. Calculate estimated_cost_eur. Write the fact table.
Build agg_daily_grid_summary as a pre-computed aggregate table.

Add data quality checks between Silver and Gold: validate no null PKs, no duplicates, referential integrity (all fact substation_sk values exist in dim_substations). Log results to audit_quality_log. Add a quality gate that halts the pipeline on CRITICAL failures.
Create a Pipeline (pl_daily_grid_refresh) that orchestrates the full flow: Bronze ingest → Silver MERGE → Quality gate → Gold build → Aggregates → Maintenance.
Verify lineage in the Fabric portal: navigate to the lineage view and confirm the flow from Lakehouse → Notebook → Lakehouse is visible.

blocks.content: You have designed and built the architecture.

The medallion pattern, star schema modeling, SCD handling, and quality gates are the structural decisions that determine whether an energy data platform stays healthy at scale or collapses into an unmaintainable tangle of ad-hoc queries. When a new grid region comes online, you add a row to dim_substations and a filter parameter to your Pipeline — you do not redesign the architecture.

In Module DE8 (Capstone), you will build the complete platform end-to-end — from an empty Fabric workspace to a production-ready energy data platform with ingestion, transformation, modeling, quality gates, Power BI dashboards, security, and deployment pipelines. Everything you have learned in this track comes together in one integrated project.

chapters.title: DE8 Capstone
chapters.description: Deliver an end-to-end data engineering solution across the full track.

blocks.content: Module DE8 Capstone — End-to-End Data Engineering Pipeline Everything You Have Learned, Unified Into One Platform Estimated Time: 180 Minutes Module Objective: Apply everything from Modules F1 through DE7 to build a complete, production-ready energy data engineering platform in Microsoft Fabric. Receive a business brief, design the architecture, implement medallion ingestion, build a star schema, enforce data quality, orchestrate the full pipeline, and verify the platform end-to-end.
Welcome to the Capstone

Nine modules. Ninety lessons. You have learned the entire data engineering toolkit in Microsoft Fabric: OneLake, Lakehouses, Pipelines, Dataflows Gen2, the Spark engine, PySpark transformations, Spark SQL, the medallion architecture, star schema modeling, slowly changing dimensions, and data quality frameworks. Now you build it all. The capstone is not a tutorial. It is a project. You will receive a business brief with requirements for an energy grid data platform. You will design the architecture. You will implement every layer — from raw meter file ingestion to a governed star schema that Power BI can read via DirectLake. You will orchestrate the full flow with a master Pipeline, enforce quality gates, and maintain the platform with OPTIMIZE and VACUUM. This module is structured as ten sequential phases. Each phase builds on the previous. At the end, you will have a complete data engineering platform that demonstrates mastery of every skill in the DE track. There is no step-by-step code in this module. Each phase describes what to build and the acceptance criteria, but the implementation decisions are yours. Choose the patterns, write the code, and make the trade-offs. This is how real platforms are built.
Phase 1: Business Brief and Architecture Design

The Business Brief You are the lead data engineer for NordGrid Energy, a multi-region utility company operating smart grid infrastructure across four regions (NORTH, SOUTH, EAST, WEST). The company has five data sources and three analytical requirements:
Data Sources

Source Format Volume Delivery Smart Meter Readings CSV files dropped daily onto SFTP (bridged to ADLS Gen2) ~2–5 million readings/day across 500K active meters Daily batch. Incremental (today’s readings only). SCADA Turbine Telemetry Parquet files exported hourly from SCADA historian ~500K sensor records/hour across 2,000 turbines Hourly batch. Full hour snapshot. Weather Forecasts JSON from a REST API (weather service) ~10K forecast records per load (50 stations × 48-hour horizon × 4 updates/day) 4x daily via Pipeline HTTP connector. Energy Prices Azure SQL Database (energy trading system) ~1K rows/day (24 hourly prices × 4 regions × multiple market types) Daily batch (weekdays only). Incremental by price_date. Substation Registry + Tariff Schedules CSV from asset management (substation registry, ~50K rows). Excel from SharePoint (tariff codes, ~500 rows). Small reference data, refreshed weekly/monthly. Dataflow Gen2 (analyst-managed).
Analytical Requirements

Requirement Description Consumer R1: Grid Operations Dashboard Daily consumption by region, substation load ranking, peak demand trends, anomaly alerts. Drill-through from region to substation to individual meter. Grid operations analysts (Power BI via DirectLake on Gold layer) R2: Billing Data Foundation Accurate daily meter readings joined to tariff rates for billing calculations. Must be deduplicated, quality-validated, and auditable for regulatory compliance. Billing system (reads from Gold fact_meter_readings via SQL analytics endpoint) R3: Asset Analytics Substation aging analysis, maintenance scheduling based on commission dates and voltage class. Track attribute changes over time (SCD Type 2). Asset management team (reads from Gold dim_substations via SQL analytics endpoint)
Architecture Design Task Before writing any code, design the architecture. Produce a diagram that answers:

items: Which workspace(s)? Named how?
Which Lakehouses? One per medallion layer?
Which Pipelines? One master or one per source?
Which Notebooks? Named and scoped to which transformation?
Which Dataflows Gen2? For which reference data?
Which Environment? With which libraries?
Where do quality gates live in the pipeline flow?
How does cross-Lakehouse access work (shortcuts or abfss://) ?

blocks.content: Reference Architecture

ws_nordgrid_dev / ws_nordgrid_prod
  │
  ├── lh_bronze_grid
  │   ├── Files/landing/meters/     ← Pipeline Copy Activity
  │   ├── Files/landing/scada/      ← Pipeline Copy Activity
  │   ├── Files/landing/weather/    ← Pipeline Copy Activity (REST)
  │   ├── Files/landing/pricing/    ← Pipeline Copy Activity (SQL)
  │   ├── Files/archive/            ← Processed files moved here
  │   └── Tables/ raw_meter_readings, raw_scada, raw_weather, raw_pricing
  │
  ├── lh_silver_grid
  │   └── Tables/ meters, scada_telemetry, weather_forecasts, energy_prices
  │
  ├── lh_gold_grid
  │   └── Tables/ fact_meter_readings, dim_substations (SCD2),
  │       dim_meters, dim_date, dim_tariffs, agg_daily_grid_summary
  │
  ├── env_energy_engineering     ← Environment (libraries + Spark config)
  │
  ├── df_reference_data          ← Dataflow Gen2 (substations + tariffs)
  │
  ├── nb_bronze_meters, nb_bronze_scada, nb_bronze_weather, nb_bronze_pricing
  ├── nb_silver_meters, nb_silver_scada, nb_silver_weather, nb_silver_pricing
  ├── nb_quality_gate

Important: This reference architecture is a suggestion. You may adjust it based on your reasoning. The key is that every item exists for a purpose and every data flow is traceable from source to consumer.
Phase 2: OneLake and Lakehouse Setup
Deliverables

items: Create the workspace (ws_nordgrid_dev). Assign it to your Fabric capacity.
Create lh_bronze_grid, lh_silver_grid, lh_gold_grid Lakehouses.
Establish the Files/ landing zone structure in lh_bronze_grid: /landing/meters/, /landing/scada/, /landing/weather/, /landing/pricing/, /archive/.
Create the Environment (env_energy_engineering) with libraries: great_expectations or equivalent, holidays, pyarrow, requests. Set Spark properties: shuffle.partitions=400, adaptive.enabled=true.
Create cross-Lakehouse shortcuts if using the shortcut pattern (Silver shortcuts to Bronze tables, Gold shortcuts to Silver tables).

blocks.content: Acceptance Criteria

items: Three Lakehouses exist with correct names and are accessible.
The Files/ landing zone has the correct folder structure.
The Environment publishes successfully and libraries import without error in a test notebook.
Cross-Lakehouse access works (shortcut or abfss:// path confirmed with a test read).

blocks.content: Modules Referenced: F1 (Platform Foundations), F2 (OneLake), DE1 (Lakehouse Fundamentals), DE4 (Environments).
Phase 3: Data Ingestion — Pipelines and Dataflows
Deliverables

items: Pipeline pl_ingest_meters: Copy Activity to land daily meter CSV from ADLS Gen2 (or simulated source) into lh_bronze_grid/Files/landing/meters/{load_date}/. Parameterize load_date.
Pipeline pl_ingest_scada: Copy Activity to land hourly SCADA Parquet into Files/landing/scada/.
Pipeline pl_ingest_weather: Copy Activity with REST connector to pull weather JSON from an API (or simulate with a local JSON file). Land in Files/landing/weather/{load_date}.json.
Pipeline pl_ingest_pricing: Copy Activity from Azure SQL Database (or simulated source). Query with WHERE price_date = load_date. Land as Parquet in Files/landing/pricing/.

Dataflow Gen2 df_reference_data: Two queries — (1) Substations from CSV/SQL source, transformed (filter active, rename to snake_case, add voltage_class, replace null region), destination: lh_gold_grid/dim_substations with Replace mode. (2) Tariffs from Excel/CSV, destination: lh_gold_grid/dim_tariffs with Replace mode.
Master Pipeline pl_daily_grid_refresh: calls the four ingestion pipelines in parallel, then calls df_reference_data.

blocks.content: Acceptance Criteria

items: Meter, SCADA, weather, and pricing files land in the correct Files/ folders.
Dataflow Gen2 writes dim_substations and dim_tariffs to lh_gold_grid. Both tables are queryable from the SQL analytics endpoint.
The master Pipeline runs all ingestions in parallel and completes successfully.
Parameters work: running pl_ingest_meters with a different load_date lands files in the correct date folder.

blocks.content: Modules Referenced: DE2 (Pipelines — Copy Activity, parameterization, triggers, Execute Pipeline), DE3 (Dataflows Gen2 — connectors, transformations, destinations).
Phase 4: Bronze Layer — Raw Data Landing
Deliverables

items: Notebook nb_bronze_meters: Read landed CSVs from Files/landing/meters/{load_date}/. Add metadata columns (_load_timestamp, _source_system = 'UTILITY_SFTP', _source_file via input_file_name()). Append to raw_meter_readings Delta table. Parameterized (load_date). Returns row count via exit().
Notebook nb_bronze_scada: Read Parquet from Files/landing/scada/. Add metadata. Append to raw_scada_telemetry.
Notebook nb_bronze_weather: Read JSON from Files/landing/weather/. Add metadata. Append to raw_weather_forecasts.
Notebook nb_bronze_pricing: Read Parquet from Files/landing/pricing/. Add metadata. Append to raw_energy_prices.
Add all four Bronze notebooks as Notebook Activities in pl_daily_grid_refresh (after ingestion, before Silver). Run in parallel (they are independent).
Archive processed files: move from landing/ to archive/ after successful Bronze load.

blocks.content: Acceptance Criteria

items: Four Bronze Delta tables exist in lh_bronze_grid: raw_meter_readings, raw_scada_telemetry, raw_weather_forecasts, raw_energy_prices.
All tables have correct metadata columns (_load_timestamp, _source_system, _source_file).
Running the Bronze notebooks a second time appends new data (append-only contract preserved).
The master Pipeline now executes: Ingestion (parallel) → Bronze (parallel).
Processed files are moved to Files/archive/.

blocks.content: Modules Referenced: DE4 (Spark Engine, notebookutils for file operations and parameters), DE5 (PySpark — withColumn, input_file_name, write with append mode), DE7 (Bronze layer design).
Phase 5: Silver Layer — Cleansing and MERGE
Deliverables

items: Notebook nb_silver_meters: Read raw_meter_readings (filter by load_date or watermark). Deduplicate by meter_id + reading_date (keep latest _load_timestamp). Cast daily_kwh and peak_kw to DOUBLE, reading_date to DATE. Validate: filter daily_kwh >= 0 AND < 10000, reject future dates. Standardize: upper(trim(region)), fillna region='UNKNOWN', substation_id='UNASSIGNED'. Drop internal metadata columns. MERGE into lh_silver_grid.meters (upsert by meter_id + reading_date). Return row count via exit().
Notebook nb_silver_scada: Deduplicate by turbine_id + event_timestamp. Cast sensor values. Filter NULL/NaN power_output_kw. Rename SCADA columns to snake_case. MERGE into scada_telemetry.
Notebook nb_silver_weather: Deduplicate by station_id + forecast_date + hour. Cast temperature and wind_speed. MERGE into weather_forecasts.
Notebook nb_silver_pricing: Deduplicate by region + price_date + hour_block. Cast price_mwh. Validate positive prices. MERGE into energy_prices.
Add all four Silver notebooks to pl_daily_grid_refresh after Bronze. Use notebookutils.notebook.runMultiple to run them in parallel.

blocks.content: Acceptance Criteria

items: Four Silver tables exist in lh_silver_grid: meters, scada_telemetry, weather_forecasts, energy_prices.
No duplicate business keys in any Silver table (verify with COUNT vs. COUNT DISTINCT on the business key).
All columns have correct data types. No raw strings where doubles or dates belong.
Nulls in critical columns (meter_id, reading_date) are zero. Verify with a null count query.
Running the Silver notebooks a second time with the same data produces no changes (MERGE idempotency verified).
The master Pipeline now executes: Ingestion → Bronze → Silver (parallel).

blocks.content: Modules Referenced: DE5 (PySpark — dedup with window, cast, filter, fillna, when/otherwise), DE6 (Spark SQL — MERGE patterns), DE7 (Silver layer design).
Phase 6: Data Quality Gate
Deliverables

items: Notebook nb_quality_gate: Run quality checks on all Silver tables after the Silver MERGE completes. Checks include:
Completeness: meter_id IS NOT NULL (threshold: 100%)
Completeness: daily_kwh IS NOT NULL (threshold: 99%)
Uniqueness: COUNT(meter_id, reading_date) = COUNT(DISTINCT meter_id, reading_date) (threshold: 100%)
Range: daily_kwh >= 0 AND daily_kwh < 10000 (threshold: 99.9%)
Freshness: MAX(reading_date) >= CURRENT_DATE - 2 (threshold: 100%)
Log all results to an audit_quality_log Delta table (table name, check name, failures, total, pass_rate, status, timestamp).
Exit with 'PASS' if all CRITICAL checks pass. Exit with 'FAIL' if any CRITICAL check fails.
In the Pipeline: add an If Condition after nb_quality_gate. If exit value = 'FAIL', trigger the error path (Web Activity alert + Fail Activity). If 'PASS', continue to Gold.

blocks.content: Acceptance Criteria

items: Quality checks run after every Silver refresh and log results to audit_quality_log.
Introducing a deliberate quality issue (e.g., inserting a row with NULL meter_id into Bronze, running the pipeline) causes the quality gate to fail and halt the pipeline before Gold layer refresh.
The audit_quality_log table accumulates historical check results, enabling trend analysis.
The master Pipeline now executes: Ingestion → Bronze → Silver → Quality Gate → (continue or halt).

blocks.content: Modules Referenced: DE7 (Data quality rules, quality check framework, quality gates in Pipelines).
Phase 7: Gold Layer — Star Schema and Aggregates
Deliverables

items: Notebook nb_gold_dim_date: Generate dim_date using SQL sequence (2020–2030). Columns: date_key (INT, yyyyMMdd), calendar_date, year, quarter, month, month_name, day_of_week, day_name, is_weekend, week_of_year. Write once with overwrite mode.

Notebook nb_gold_dim_substations: Implement SCD Type 2 from lh_silver_grid.substations (via Dataflow Gen2 refresh). Surrogate key (substation_sk), business key (substation_id), substation_name, voltage_kv, region, status, voltage_class, commission_date, effective_from, effective_to, is_current. Identify changed rows, expire old, insert new versions, handle brand-new substations.

Notebook nb_gold_fact_meters: Join Silver meters to Gold dimensions using surrogate keys. Resolve meter_id → meter_sk (from dim_meters or use business key if dim_meters is skipped), substation_id → substation_sk (from dim_substations WHERE is_current=1), reading_date → date_key (from dim_date). Calculate estimated_cost_eur = daily_kwh * tariff rate_per_kwh. Add consumption_band (High/Medium/Low). Write fact_meter_readings.

Notebook nb_gold_agg_daily: Build agg_daily_grid_summary from fact_meter_readings joined to dim_date and dim_substations. Columns: calendar_date, year, month, region, voltage_class, total_kwh, total_cost_eur, active_meters, peak_demand_kw, avg_meter_kwh.
Add all Gold notebooks to pl_daily_grid_refresh after the Quality Gate (sequential: dim_date first, then dim_substations, then fact, then aggregates).

blocks.content: Acceptance Criteria

items: Star schema exists in lh_gold_grid: dim_date, dim_substations (SCD2), dim_tariffs, fact_meter_readings, agg_daily_grid_summary.

dim_substations has at least one example of SCD Type 2: a substation whose voltage_kv or region changed, with an expired row (is_current = false, effective_to = change date) and a current row (is_current = true).
fact_meter_readings uses surrogate keys for all dimension joins. No orphan keys (verify: every fact substation_sk exists in dim_substations.substation_sk).
agg_daily_grid_summary totals match fact_meter_readings when queried for the same region and date.
All Gold tables are queryable from the SQL analytics endpoint. The billing team can query fact_meter_readings with T-SQL.
The master Pipeline now executes: Ingestion → Bronze → Silver → Quality Gate → Gold.

blocks.content: Modules Referenced: DE5 (PySpark joins, withColumn), DE6 (Spark SQL DDL, MERGE, CTAS), DE7 (Gold layer star schema, SCD Type 2, surrogate keys, aggregate tables, dim_date generation).
Phase 8: Master Pipeline Orchestration
Deliverables • Complete the master Pipeline pl_daily_grid_refresh to orchestrate the full flow:

pl_daily_grid_refresh
  Parameters: load_date (String), grid_region (String, default: 'ALL')
  │
  ├── [PARALLEL] Ingestion:
  │   ├── Execute Pipeline: pl_ingest_meters (pass load_date)
  │   ├── Execute Pipeline: pl_ingest_scada
  │   ├── Execute Pipeline: pl_ingest_weather
  │   ├── Execute Pipeline: pl_ingest_pricing (If Condition: weekday only)
  │   └── Dataflow Gen2: df_reference_data
  │
  ├── [PARALLEL] Bronze loading:
  │   ├── Notebook: nb_bronze_meters (pass load_date)
  │   ├── Notebook: nb_bronze_scada
  │   ├── Notebook: nb_bronze_weather
  │   └── Notebook: nb_bronze_pricing
  │
  ├── [PARALLEL] Silver MERGE:
  │   ├── Notebook: nb_silver_meters
  │   ├── Notebook: nb_silver_scada
  │   ├── Notebook: nb_silver_weather
  │   └── Notebook: nb_silver_pricing
  │
  ├── [SEQUENTIAL] Quality Gate:
  │   ├── Notebook: nb_quality_gate
  │   └── If Condition: exit = 'FAIL' → Alert + Fail Activity
  │
  ├── [SEQUENTIAL] Gold build:
  │   ├── Notebook: nb_gold_dim_date (run once, then skip)
  │   ├── Notebook: nb_gold_dim_substations (SCD Type 2)
  │   ├── Notebook: nb_gold_fact_meters
  │   └── Notebook: nb_gold_agg_daily
  │
  └── [SEQUENTIAL] Maintenance:
      └── Notebook: nb_maintenance_weekly (OPTIMIZE + VACUUM, run weekly)

•  Add error paths: if any Bronze or Silver notebook fails, trigger a Web Activity (or Set Variable) alert and a Fail Activity with a descriptive error message using dynamic content.
•  Add the weekday-only If Condition for pricing ingestion (skip on Saturday/Sunday).
•  Create a scheduled trigger: daily at 02:00 AM UTC.
•  Set realistic timeouts: Copy Activities at 30 minutes, Bronze/Silver notebooks at 60 minutes, Gold notebooks at 90 minutes.
•  Configure retry policies: 2 retries with 60-second intervals on Copy Activities, 1 retry on notebook activities.

Acceptance Criteria
•  The complete pipeline runs end-to-end: Ingestion → Bronze → Silver → Quality Gate → Gold → Maintenance.
•  Parallel stages (ingestion, Bronze, Silver) execute concurrently (verify in Gantt chart).
•  The quality gate halts Gold processing when a deliberate quality issue is introduced.
•  Error paths fire correctly when an activity fails (test by breaking a source URL).
•  The pipeline is monitored in the Monitoring Hub with activity-level drill-down.
Modules Referenced: DE2 (Pipelines — Execute Pipeline, ForEach, If Condition, error handling, triggers, monitoring).
Phase 9: Maintenance and Verification
Deliverables

items: Notebook nb_maintenance_weekly: Loop through all tables in lh_bronze_grid, lh_silver_grid, and lh_gold_grid. Run OPTIMIZE (with ZORDER BY on meter_id, reading_date for meter tables). Run VACUUM (7-day retention). Print file count and size for each table. Return a summary string via exit().
Verification queries: write a verification notebook (or %%sql cells) that confirms:
Row counts: Bronze >= Silver >= Fact (rows decrease through dedup and filtering)
No orphan foreign keys: all fact.substation_sk values exist in dim_substations
SCD2 correctness: for a changed substation, exactly one row has is_current=true and effective_to='9999-12-31'
Aggregate consistency: agg_daily_grid_summary totals match fact_meter_readings for the same dimensions
Quality log: audit_quality_log has entries for every pipeline run
Run DESCRIBE DETAIL on the largest Silver table before and after OPTIMIZE. Document the file count reduction.
Run DESCRIBE HISTORY on Silver meters to show the MERGE operations, OPTIMIZE, and VACUUM in the audit trail.

blocks.content: Acceptance Criteria

items: Maintenance notebook runs successfully on all tables and reports file counts.
OPTIMIZE reduces file count on tables with frequent appends (Bronze tables especially).
All verification queries pass.
DESCRIBE HISTORY shows a clean audit trail of operations.

Phase 10: Architecture Review and Reflection

The capstone concludes with a review of the completed platform. This is not a formality — it is a critical professional skill. Every data platform needs to be communicated to stakeholders who will never open a Notebook.
Architecture Documentation
•  Draw the complete architecture diagram: sources on the left, Bronze/Silver/Gold in the center, consumers on the right. Annotate with item names, data volumes, and refresh frequencies.
•  Draw the star schema entity-relationship diagram for the Gold layer. State the grain of each fact table.
•  Document the Pipeline flow: which activities run in parallel, which are sequential, where the quality gate sits, and what happens on failure.
Architecture Review Questions
Answer these questions about your own platform. These are the questions a senior architect would ask:

•  What happens if the utility adds a new column to the meter CSV tomorrow? Which layer absorbs the change?
•  What happens if pl_daily_grid_refresh fails at the Silver stage at 3:00 AM? How do you know, and how do you recover?
•  What happens if a new grid region (CENTRAL) comes online? How many items do you need to change?
•  What happens if daily meter volume grows from 2M to 20M rows? Which components need tuning and which are already elastic?
•  A regulator asks you to reproduce the exact meter readings that were used for last month’s billing. Can you? How?
•  A substation’s region attribute changed last week. An analyst queries last quarter’s data. Do they see the old region or the new one? Why?
•  If you could rebuild this platform with one change, what would it be?
Completion Checklist

Component Status Workspace created with meaningful name ☐ Three Lakehouses (lh_bronze_grid, lh_silver_grid, lh_gold_grid) ☐ Environment (env_energy_engineering) published and attached ☐ Files/landing/ zone with folders per source ☐ Four batch ingestion Pipelines (meters, SCADA, weather, pricing) ☐ Dataflow Gen2 for reference data (substations + tariffs) ☐ Four Bronze notebooks with metadata columns and append-only writes ☐ Four Silver notebooks with deduplication, type casting, and MERGE ☐ Quality gate notebook with 5+ checks, audit logging, and pipeline halt on FAIL ☐ dim_date generated with SQL sequence (2020–2030) ☐ dim_substations with SCD Type 2 (at least one changed substation demonstrating expire + insert) ☐ dim_tariffs loaded via Dataflow Gen2 ☐ fact_meter_readings with surrogate keys and calculated estimated_cost_eur ☐ agg_daily_grid_summary pre-computed aggregate table ☐ Master Pipeline (pl_daily_grid_refresh): Ingestion → Bronze → Silver → Quality Gate → Gold → Maintenance ☐ Parallel execution verified in Gantt chart (Ingestion, Bronze, Silver stages) ☐ Error paths with descriptive Fail Activity messages ☐ Weekday-only If Condition for pricing ingestion ☐ Scheduled trigger (daily at 02:00 AM UTC) ☐ Maintenance notebook: OPTIMIZE + VACUUM on all layers ☐ Verification queries: row counts, FK integrity, SCD2 correctness, aggregate consistency ☐ audit_quality_log table with historical check results ☐ All Gold tables queryable from the SQL analytics endpoint ☐ Architecture diagram documenting the complete platform ☐ Architecture review questions answered ☐
Data Engineering Track Complete

You have built a complete energy data engineering platform in Microsoft Fabric. Not a demo. Not a proof of concept. A production-ready system with automated ingestion from five sources, Bronze/Silver/Gold medallion processing, a governed star schema, data quality gates, SCD Type 2 dimensions, pre-computed aggregates, and a fully orchestrated master Pipeline.

You understand not just how to use each tool, but why it exists and when to choose it. You understand the architectural principles — medallion layers, star schemas, slowly changing dimensions, quality gates — that keep a platform maintainable as it scales. You understand the operational discipline — parameterization, error handling, OPTIMIZE, VACUUM, audit logging — that keeps a platform reliable in production.

The platform you built today is Version 1. Real platforms evolve. New sources arrive (a solar generation feed, a battery storage telemetry stream). New business questions emerge (demand forecasting, carbon accounting). New team members need onboarding. The skills you have learned in this track give you the foundation to adapt, extend, and optimize the platform for whatever comes next.
